title: Man and Medicine: Towards Health for All
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter traces the historical evolution of medicine from primitive times to the modern era, highlighting the shift from supernatural beliefs to scientific, evidence-based practices. It covers the major ancient systems of medicine (Indian, Islamic, Chinese, Egyptian, Mesopotamian, Greek, and Roman), the dawn of scientific medicine during the Renaissance, the sanitary awakening, the germ theory, and the emergence of modern medicine. It explores the changing concepts of public health, the development of social and preventive medicine, and the contemporary health care revolution centered around Primary Health Care, the 'Health for All' goal, Millennium Development Goals (MDGs), and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

DEFINITIONS
- Ayurveda: Meaning 'Knowledge of Life' or the knowledge by which life may be prolonged. It is derived from the Atharvaveda (medical knowledge of Atharvaveda gradually developed into the science of Ayurveda).
- Family Medicine: Family-oriented medicine, i.e., health care centered on the family as a unit, from first contact to the ongoing care of chronic problems (from prevention to rehabilitation). It is neither disease nor organ-oriented and is specially designed to deliver Primary Care.
- Community Medicine: A specialty which deals with populations and comprises those doctors who try to measure the needs of the population (both sick and well), who plan and administer services, and who are engaged in research and teaching in the field. It emphasizes the prevention of disease and promotion of health. It is the successor of what was previously known as Public Health, Community Health, or Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM).
- Social Medicine: The study of man as a social being in his total environment, focusing on the health of the community as a whole. It emphasizes the strong relationship between medicine and social sciences.
- Preventive Medicine: A branch of medicine applied to 'healthy' people, customarily by actions affecting large numbers or populations, with the primary objective of prevention of disease and promotion of health.
- Primary Health Care: Essential health care based on practical, scientifically sound, and socially acceptable methods and technology made universally accessible to individuals and families in the community through their full participation and at a cost that the community and country can afford.

CORE CONCEPTS
- The Supernatural Theory of Disease: Primitive man attributed disease and suffering to the wrath of gods, the invasion of the body by 'evil spirits', and the malevolent influence of stars and planets. Treatment involved appeasing gods by prayers, rituals, sacrifices, witchcraft, and the use of charms and amulets.
- Tri-Dosha Theory of Disease (Ayurveda): Disease is explained as a disturbance in the equilibrium of the 3 Humors: Vata (Wind), Pitta (Gall Fluid), and Kapha (Mucus). This theory is strikingly similar to the Theory of 4 Humors in Greek medicine.
- Concept of Yin and Yang (Chinese Medicine): Based on two opposing principlesâ€”Yang (an active, masculine principle) and Yin (a negative, feminine principle). The balance of these two opposing forces meant good health.
- Theory of 4 Humors (Greek Medicine): Greek medicine rejected the supernatural theory and believed matter was made up of four elements (earth, air, fire, water) corresponding to qualities (cold, dry, hot, moist). These were represented in the body by 4 Humors: Phlegm (Mucus), Yellow Bile (Gall), Black Bile (Intestinal Matter), and Blood. Health prevailed when these were in equilibrium.
- Germ Theory of Disease: Pioneered by Louis Pasteur (who demonstrated bacteria in air and disproved spontaneous generation) and Robert Koch (who showed anthrax and tuberculosis were caused by bacteria). This shifted focus from miasma to microbes as the definitive causative agents of diseases.
- Multifactorial Causation: First mooted by Pettenkofer, it recognizes that modern diseases (cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular diseases) cannot be explained by the germ theory alone. It involves social, economic, genetic, environmental, and psychological 'risk factors'.
- Changing Concepts in Public Health: Public health has evolved through four distinct phases: (1) Disease control phase (1880-1920) focusing on sanitary legislation; (2) Health promotional phase (1920-1960) focusing on personal health services and basic health services; (3) Social engineering phase (1960-1980) addressing chronic diseases and risk factors; (4) 'Health for All' phase (1981-2000) aiming to minimize inequalities in health globally.

MEDICINE IN ANTIQUITY
Indian Medicine
- Ayurveda dates back to 400 - 600 BC. The Golden Age of Indian Medicine was between 800 BC - 600 AD.
- Indian Medicine in Asia has played the same role as Greek medicine in the West.
- Celebrated Authorities in Ayurvedic Medicine:
  - Atreya: 1st Indian Physician & Teacher of medicine who lived in the ancient University of Takshashila (about 800 B.C.).
  - Charaka: Father of Indian Medicine. He compiled the 'Charaka Samhita', was a Court Physician to the Buddhist king Kanishka, and mentioned some 500 drugs.
  - Sushruta: Father of Indian Surgery & Father of Plastic Surgery. Compiled his surgical knowledge in the 'Sushruta Samhita'. Known for rhinoplasty and cataract operations.
  - Vagbhata: Medical Writer who compiled all works & gave the Science of Ayurveda.
- Dhanvantri: The Hindu God of Medicine, born from the churning of the oceans.
- Laws of Manu: A code of Personal Hygiene in ancient India. Excavations at Mohenjo-daro and Harappa revealed advanced sanitation.

Islamic Medicine
- Unani Tibb System: A system of the Medieval Age, promoted and compiled by Muslims in the Arab world. It originated in Greece (Graeco-Roman) and was introduced to India by the Muslim Rulers of the 10th Century.
- Key Figures:
  - Rhazes (Abu Bakr al-Razi): Gave the first known book of Child Disease. Noted for keen observation, he worked on Pupillary Reactions, Mercury Purgatives, and Pharmacology.
  - Avicenna (Ibn Sina): An intellectual prodigy who worked extensively on Pharmacology. Compiled a 21-volume encyclopedia, the 'Canon of Medicine'.

Chinese Medicine
- Claims to be the World's 1st Organized Body of Medical Knowledge (dating back to 2700 B.C.).
- Chinese Medicine is a Pioneer in the concept of Specific Protection by Vaccine (early pioneers of immunization using variolation against smallpox).
- Utilized Acupuncture & Acupressure, Hydro Therapy & Massage Therapy, Dietetics & Nutrition. Included the system of 'barefoot doctors'.

Egyptian Medicine
- Medicine was compiled & recorded on Papyrus.
  - Edwin Smith Papyrus: Oldest treatise on surgery (Head & Neck Surgery, accurately describing partial paralysis from skull fractures).
  - Ebers Papyrus: Focused on Prescription Writing & Drugs (contains ~800 prescriptions based on ~700 drugs).
- Concept of Doctor & Priest in combination. Specialization prevailed (eye, head, and tooth doctors).
- Imhotep: The 1st Egyptian Physician, architect of the step pyramid, considered both a doctor and divinity.
- Horus: God of Egyptian medicine/health.

Mesopotamian Medicine
- Known as the Cradle of Civilization. Characterized by Geomancy (interpretation of dreams) & Prescription Writing in cuneiform on tablets.
- Specialties for Diseases included: 
  - Herbs Doctors (Internal Medicine)
  - Knife Doctors (Surgery)
  - Spell Doctors (Psychiatry)
- Code of Hammurabi: Code of Conduct by Doctors & Ethics. It was the 1st codification of Medical Practice, containing drastic laws and penalties for harmful therapy.

Greek Medicine
- Aesculapius (1200 B.C.): God of Medicine. Had two daughters: Hygiea (Goddess of Health / Preventive Medicine) and Panacea (Goddess of Medicine / Curative).
- Hippocrates (460-370 B.C.): Father of Medicine. Regarded as the 1st True Epidemiologist because he distinguished between endemic and epidemic diseases. Author of the book 'Airs, Waters & Places'. He challenged the tradition of magic in medicine and set a high moral standard with the 'Hippocratic Oath'.
- Based on the 4 Humors: Phlegm (Mucus), Yellow Bile (Gall), Black Bile (Intestinal Matter), and Blood.

Roman Medicine
- The Romans were highly practical and gave the concept of Quarantine, Sanitation (Public Sewage System / aqueducts / baths), Public Health, and Health Policy.
- Galen (130-205 A.D.): A 'Medical Dictator' whose teachings in Anatomy & Physiology ruled for 14 centuries. He observed that disease is due to three factors: predisposing, exciting, and environmental.

DAWN OF SCIENTIFIC MEDICINE
- Revival of Medicine (1453-1600 A.D.):
  - Paracelsus: Revived medicine by publicly burning the works of Galen and Avicenna, turning medicine towards rational research.
  - Fracastorius (Fracus Torius): Founder of Epidemiology. Enunciated the 'Theory of Contagion', recognizing the transfer of infection via minute invisible particles (e.g., syphilis).
  - Andreas Vesalius: Father of Modern Anatomy. Considered the '1st Man of Modern Medicine'. Authored the '1st Book on Scientific Medicine' titled 'Fabrica'.
  - Ambroise Pare: Father of Modern Surgery.
  - Thomas Sydenham: The English Hippocrates, set the example for the true clinical method.
  - Morgagni: Founded pathologic anatomy.
- Sanitary Awakening (mid-19th Century England): Sparked by the industrial revolution's ill effects (slums, filth). Edwin Chadwick's 1842 report led to the Public Health Act of 1848. John Snow established the role of polluted water in cholera. William Budd linked typhoid to drinking water. Sir John Simon became the first medical officer of health in London.
- Birth of Preventive Medicine: James Lind advocated fresh fruit for scurvy prevention (1753). Edward Jenner discovered the smallpox vaccination (1796).

MODERN MEDICINE / SCIENTIFIC MEDICINE
- Curative Medicine: Aims at the removal of disease from the patient using refined diagnostic techniques and treatments (e.g., allopathic medicine, antibiotics).
- Preventive Medicine: Focuses on healthy populations. Triumphs include bacterial/viral vaccines, discoveries in nutrition, and synthetic insecticides (DDT). Includes concepts like disease eradication and presymptomatic screening.
- Social Medicine: Envisages medicine as a social science. Initiated by Neumann and Virchow (1848), revived by Alfred Grotjahn (social pathology) in 1911, and established by John Ryle in England. It focuses on epidemiology and the medical needs/care of society.

HEALTH CARE REVOLUTION
- Health for All: The 30th World Health Assembly (1977) set the target to attain a level of health permitting all citizens to lead a socially and economically productive life by the year 2000.
- Primary Health Care: The Alma-Ata Declaration (1978) called for a revolutionary approach to health care based on equity, wider coverage, community involvement, and intersectoral coordination. It involves the 'deprofessionalization of medicine' by utilizing community health workers.
- Millennium Development Goals (MDGs): Adopted in 2000, setting 8 goals for 2015. Three of the eight goals were directly health-related.
- Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): Adopted in 2015 for 2030, containing 17 goals. SDG 3 is comprehensively dedicated to health: 'Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages'.

KEY POINTS
- First Indian Physician & Teacher: Atreya.
- Father of Indian Medicine: Charaka.
- Father of Indian Surgery & Plastic Surgery: Sushruta.
- First Egyptian Physician: Imhotep.
- 1st codification of Medical Practice: Code of Hammurabi.
- Father of Medicine: Hippocrates.
- 1st True Epidemiologist: Hippocrates.
- Medical Dictator: Galen.
- Founder of Epidemiology / Theory of Contagion: Fracastorius (Fracus Torius).
- Father of Modern Anatomy / 1st Man of Modern Medicine: Andreas Vesalius.
- 1st Book on Scientific Medicine: Fabrica.
- Father of Modern Surgery: Ambroise Pare.
- 1st Organized Body of Medical Knowledge: Chinese Medicine.
- Pioneer in concept of Specific Protection by Vaccine: Chinese Medicine.
- The term 'Community Medicine' has replaced Public Health, Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM).

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
There are no formulas or specific calculations in this chapter.

MNEMONICS
- To remember the 3 Humors of Ayurveda (Tri-Dosha): VPK - Vata (Wind), Pitta (Gall Fluid), Kapha (Mucus).
- To remember the daughters of Aesculapius: H & P (Health & Prevention = Hygiea; Pills & Cures = Panacea).

title: Concept of Health and Disease
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The chapter provides a foundational understanding of health, disease, and the multitude of factors that influence them. Moving away from the traditional biomedical model, health is now viewed as a dynamic, multidimensional, and holistic state. The chapter covers the dimensions and determinants of health, indices to measure the quality of life (PQLI, HDI, MPI), the epidemiological triad, natural history of diseases, levels of prevention, and the distinct paradigms of monitoring, surveillance, and community diagnosis.

DEFINITIONS
Health: The WHO defines health (1948) as "a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely an absence of disease or infirmity." In recent years, this has been amplified to include the ability to lead a "socially and economically productive life."
Operational Definition of Health: A condition or quality of the human organism expressing the adequate functioning of the organism in given conditions, genetic or environmental.
Disease: A physiological/psychological dysfunction; an alteration of the human body interrupting the performance of vital functions. 
Illness: A subjective state of the person who feels aware of not being well.
Sickness: A state of social dysfunction, i.e., a role that the individual assumes when ill ("sickness role").
Public Health: The science and art of preventing disease, prolonging life & promoting health through organized community efforts (C.E.A. Winslow).
Community Medicine: The field concerned with the study of health and disease in the population of a defined community, focusing on community diagnosis and treatment.
Community Diagnosis: The identification and quantification of health problems in a community in terms of mortality and morbidity, and describing the pattern of disease based on age, sex, vital statistics, and incidence/prevalence.

CORE CONCEPTS

Changing Concepts of Health
1. Biomedical / Biological Concept: Health is viewed as the "absence of disease." Based on the germ theory of disease. It views the human body as a machine and disease as a breakdown. Limitation: It minimizes the role of environmental, social, psychological, and cultural determinants of health.
2. Ecological Concept: Views health as a dynamic equilibrium between man and his environment, and disease as a maladjustment of the human organism to the environment.
3. Psychosocial Concept: Health is not only a biomedical phenomenon but is also influenced by social, psychological, cultural, economic, and political factors.
4. Holistic Concept: A synthesis of all the above. It recognizes the strength of social, economic, political, and environmental influences. It implies a sound mind, in a sound body, in a sound family, in a sound environment.

Dimensions of Health
Health is multidimensional. 
- Physical dimension: State in which every cell and organ is functioning at optimum capacity and in perfect harmony with the rest of the body.
- Mental dimension: A state of balance between the individual and the surrounding world. It relates to cognition/knowing and implies being free from internal conflicts.
- Social dimension: Quantity and quality of an individual's interpersonal ties and the extent of involvement with the community.
- Spiritual dimension: Refers to that part of the individual which strives for meaning and purpose in life.
- Emotional dimension: Relates to "feeling" (whereas mental relates to cognition).
- Vocational dimension: Work often plays an important role in promoting both physical and mental health.
- Other dimensions: Cultural, Environmental, Nutritional, Economic, Preventive, Educational, etc.

Determinants of Health
Health is multifactorial. The major determinants include:
- Biological: Diseases known to be of genetic origin (e.g., chromosomal anomalies, diabetes).
- Behavioral and Socio-cultural: Lifestyles and personal habits. In developed countries: Obesity, drug addiction. In developing countries: Lack of sanitation, poor nutrition.
- Environment: Internal environment (tissue, organ, organ system) and External environment (all that which is external to the individual: physical, biological, psychosocial).
- Socio-economic: Economic status (purchasing power), Education (especially female education), Occupation (unemployment leads to psychological and social damage).
- Health System: Health and family welfare services, immunization, provision of safe water.
- Ageing of the population: Increased prevalence of chronic diseases and disabilities.
- Gender: Women's health issues.

Concept of Well-being
Well-being has objective and subjective components:
- Standard of living (Objective): Refers to the scale of our expenditure, goods we consume, and services we enjoy. Includes income, occupation, housing, sanitation, nutrition, and health services. Depends on per capita GNP.
- Level of living (Objective): Parallel term used by the UN, consisting of 9 components including health, food consumption, education, and social security.
- Quality of life (Subjective): Physical, mental, and social well-being as perceived by happiness, satisfaction, and gratification.

Indices of Health and Development
Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI): 
- Subjective component of quality of life.
- Consolidates 3 indicators: Infant Mortality Rate (IMR), Life expectancy at age one, and Literacy.
- Scaled from 0 (worst) to 100 (best). India's PQLI is ~65.
- Does  measure economic growth (per capita GNP is excluded).

Human Development Index (HDI): 
- Composite index measuring achievements in basic dimensions of human development.
- Dimensions: 
  1. Health (Life expectancy at birth)
  2. Education (Mean years of schooling & Expected years of schooling)
  3. Standard of living (Real GNI per capita in PPP US$)
- Value ranges between 0 to 1. India is in the Medium category (HDI = 0.645).
- Calculated as the geometric mean of the three dimension indices.

Multi-dimensional Poverty Index (MPI): 
- Replaced the Human Poverty Index (HPI).
- Uses 3 dimensions and 10 indicators: 
  1. Health (Nutrition, Child mortality)
  2. Education (Years of schooling, School attendance)
  3. Standard of living (Cooking fuel, Sanitation, Drinking water, Electricity, Housing, Assets).

Kuppuswamy Scale
Scale of Socio-Economic Status (SES) used for Urban families. It has 3 components:
1. Education status of the Head of the family
2. Occupation status of the Head of the family
3. Total per capita family income (per month)

Indicators of Health
An ideal indicator should be Valid, Reliable, Sensitive, Specific, Feasible, and Relevant.
- Mortality Indicators: Life expectancy (positive indicator), Crude death rate, Age-specific death rate, IMR (best indicator for availability, utilization & effectiveness of health services; most universally accepted), Child mortality rate, Under-5 proportionate mortality rate, Maternal mortality rate, Disease-specific mortality rate, Proportional mortality rate (measures burden of a disease), Case Fatality Rate (represents killing power/virulence of the disease).
- Morbidity Indicators: Incidence, Prevalence, Attendance at OPD, Admission at IPD, Duration of stay, Notification rates.
- Disability Indicators: 
  -  Days of restricted activity, Bed disability days, Work loss days.
  -  Limitation of mobility, Limitation of activity.
  -  Disability Adjusted Life Years. Measures the burden of disease. Expresses years lost to premature death & years lived with disability.
  -  Disability Free Life Expectancy (Sullivan's index). Average number of years an individual is expected to live free of disability. 
  -  Health Adjusted Life Expectancy. Replaced DALE. Adjustment for time spent in poor health.
  -  Quality Adjusted Life Years. Number of quality years added by a medical intervention.
- Utilization Rates: E.g., Proportion of fully immunized infants, ANC coverage, Bed occupancy rate (negative value indicates over/under utilization; slight positive value indicates optimum utilization), Bed turnover ratio.

Health Promotion & Ottawa Charter
Health promotion is the process of enabling people to increase control over, and to improve their health. 
Ottawa Charter (1986):
- Logo: Circle with 3 wings.
- 3 Basic Strategies: Advocate, Enable, Mediate.
- 5 Key Action Areas: 
  1. Build healthy public policy 
  2. Create supportive environment for health 
  3. Strengthen community action for health 
  4. Develop personal skills 
  5. Reorient health services

Concept of Disease & Epidemiological Triad
- Epidemiological Triad: Useful in showing the interaction & interdependence of Agent, Host, and Environment. A fourth factor, Time (incubation period/duration), is also considered.
- Agent: Substance living or nonliving whose presence or absence initiates a disease process (Biological, Nutrient, Physical, Chemical [Endogenous/Exogenous], Mechanical, Social).
- Host: Person that harbors the disease (Demographic, Biological, Social, Lifestyle factors).
- Environment: Surroundings that allow disease transmission (Physical, Biological, Psychosocial).
- Web of Causation: Suggested by MacMahon & Pugh. Ideally suited for chronic diseases where the exact agent is unknown. Emphasizes the complex interrelationship of multiple factors.

Iceberg Phenomenon of Disease
Disease in a community is compared to an iceberg.
- Floating tip: Represents what the physician sees (Clinical cases, diagnosed cases).
- Submerged portion: Represents the hidden mass of disease (Latent, inapparent, presymptomatic, undiagnosed cases, carriers). The epidemiologist is concerned with this portion.
- Waterline: Represents the demarcation between apparent & inapparent disease.
-  Iceberg phenomenon is NOT shown by Rabies, Tetanus, and Measles.

Natural History of Disease
Signifies the way a disease evolves over time from the earliest stage to its termination (recovery, disability, or death) in the absence of treatment. Best established by cohort/longitudinal studies.
1. Prepathogenesis phase: The period preliminary to the onset of disease in man. The agent has not yet entered the host, but the interacting factors are present in the environment.
2. Pathogenesis phase: Begins with the entry of the disease agent into the susceptible host. Multiplies and induces physiological changes. Progresses from early (presymptomatic) to late pathogenesis (clinical signs appear).

Levels of Prevention & Modes of Intervention
1. Primordial Prevention: Prevention of the  in populations where they have not yet appeared. It is primary prevention in its purest sense (e.g., educating children to prevent childhood obesity).
   -  Health Education.
2. Primary Prevention: Action taken prior to the onset of disease. Intervention in the prepathogenesis phase. Based on elimination of risk factors.
   -  
     a)  Health education, Environmental modification (safe water, sanitary latrines), Nutritional interventions, Lifestyle/behavioral changes.
     b)  Immunization, chemoprophylaxis, contraceptives, iodization of salt, use of seatbelts/helmets, protective goggles.
3. Secondary Prevention: Action to halt the progress of a disease at its incipient stage & prevent complications. Intervention in early pathogenesis.
   -  Early diagnosis (Screening tests, Pap smear, case finding) and Adequate Treatment (DOTS, MDT).
4. Tertiary Prevention: Action taken to limit impairment & disabilities. Intervention in late pathogenesis.
   -  
     a)  Prevents transition from impairment to handicap (e.g., spectacles for refractory error, resting limb in neutral position in polio).
     b)  Medical, Vocational, Social, Psychological (e.g., reconstructive surgery in leprosy, establishing schools for the blind).

Disease Progression Pathway:
Disease (Abnormal condition) -> Impairment (Loss of anatomical structure/physiological function, e.g., loss of foot) -> Disability (Inability to perform an activity considered normal, e.g., cannot walk) -> Handicap (Disadvantage preventing fulfillment of a role, e.g., unemployed).

Monitoring vs. Surveillance
- Monitoring: Continuous oversight of activities to ensure they are proceeding according to plan. Done by technicians. One-time activity. Stops once disease is eliminated/eradicated.
- Surveillance: Continuous scrutiny of factors that determine occurrence & distribution of disease. Done by professionals. Continuous cycle (continues even after elimination). Includes feedback and in-built action components.
  -  Health worker actively goes to the house to detect cases (e.g., NVBDCP). More expensive.
  -  Data is itself reported to the health system (patients come to PHC). Less expensive.
  -  Identifies missing cases & supplements notified cases. Estimates disease prevalence and trends in larger populations (e.g., HIV surveillance in STD clinics & ANC clinics).

International Classification of Disease (ICD)
Produced by WHO. Used as a base in health fields for epidemiological & health management purposes. Revised every 10 years.
- ICD-10: Came in Jan 1993. Coding system is alphanumeric. Arranged in 22 chapters & 3 volumes.
- ICD-11: Came into full effect in Jan 2022. Numerical (Arabic) coding. 3 volumes, 26 chapters. Includes new chapters on: Sleep wake disorders, Sexual health, Diseases of blood & immune system, Traditional medicine, and Extension codes.

Patient's Care Settings
- Inpatient: Observation care expected to be â‰¥ 24 hours.
- Outpatient: Care received in hospital/clinic/emergency where patient is released within 23 hours.
- Observation status: Active treatment required to determine if patient needs hospitalization or discharge.
- Urgent care: Requires stabilization due to an emergency situation.
- Domiciliary care: Personal services rendered by doctors to patients in their homes or nursing homes.

KEY POINTS
- PQLI focuses purely on health outcomes (IMR, life expectancy at age 1, literacy) without factoring in economic metrics like per capita GNP, making it a subjective measure of the quality of life.
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR) is the ultimate measure of the killing power or virulence of a disease organism.
- Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) is the most universally accepted indicator of the health status of a whole population and their socio-economic conditions.
- Screening tests fall under Secondary Prevention because they aim at early diagnosis to halt disease progression.
- Kuppuswamy Scale relies heavily on the education and occupation of the head of the family, and total family income to classify urban socio-economic status.
- Health care services should be appropriate, comprehensive, adequate, available, accessible, affordable, and feasible.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
Mortality Rates:
- Crude Death Rate (CDR) = (Total deaths / Midyear population) x 1000
- Age Specific Death Rate (ASDR) = (Total number of deaths in a specific age group / Midyear population of the same age group) x 1000
- Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) = (Total infant deaths / Total live births) x 1000
- Child Mortality Rate (CMR) = (Total deaths of 1-4 years / Midyear population of 1-4 years) x 1000
- Disease Specific Mortality Rate = (Deaths from a specific disease / Midyear population) x 1000
- Proportional Mortality Rate = (Deaths from a specific disease / Total deaths) x 100
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR) = (Total no. of deaths due to specific disease / Total no. of cases due to that specific disease) x 100

Disability Measures:
- DALY = YLL (Years of Lost Life) + YLD (Years Lost to Disability)
- YLL = Number of deaths x Expected remaining years of life
- YLD = Number of cases x Average duration of the disease x Weighting factor
- Sullivan's index (DFLE) = Life expectancy - Duration of disability

Indices:
- HDI Dimension Index = (Actual value - Minimum value) / (Maximum value - Minimum value)
- HDI = âˆ›(Life expectancy index x Education index x Income index)
- Per capita family income = Total income of the family / Total number of family members

MNEMONICS
Dimensions of Health:

- Vocational, Cultural
- Nutritional, Environmental
- Physical, Social, Mental
- Spiritual, Emotional, Economical

Determinants of Health:

- Gender
- Health system
- Socio-economic
- Environmental
- Ageing
- Biological
- Behavioral

Ottawa Charter Key Actions:

- Public's: Public health policy
- Skilled: Personal Skills development
- Action: Strengthen Community Action
- Reorients: Reorient health services
- Environment: Supportive Environment

Web of Causation:

- Web of causation was given by MacMahon & Pugh.

Iceberg Phenomenon Exceptions:

- Measles
- Kutta (Dog bite -> Rabies)
- Tota (Tetanus)

ICD-11 New Chapters:

- Sleep wake disorders
- Sexual health
- Blood & immune system diseases
- Integration of Traditional medicine
- Extension codes

title: Principles of Epidemiology, Disease Transmission & Immunization
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
Epidemiology is the basic science of preventive and social medicine. Historically, John Snow's investigation of cholera in London laid its foundations. Modern epidemiology evaluates health services, disease distribution, and risk factors, extending its scope from infectious to chronic diseases and health-related events. The primary aims are to describe the distribution and magnitude of disease, identify aetiological (risk) factors, and provide data essential for the planning, implementation, and evaluation of health services.

DEFINITIONS
- Epidemiology: The study of the occurrence and distribution of health-related events, states, and processes in specified populations, including the study of the determinants influencing such processes, and the application of this knowledge to control relevant health problems.
- Epidemic: The occurrence of cases of an illness in a community or region clearly in excess of normal expectancy.
- Endemic: Constant presence or usual/expected frequency of a disease within a given geographic area or population group. (Hyperendemic: Constant presence at high incidence affecting all ages equally. Holoendemic: High level beginning early in life, affecting mostly children).
- Sporadic: Cases occur irregularly, haphazardly, and are widely separated in space and time with no recognizable common source (e.g., Tetanus, Rabies).
- Pandemic: An epidemic occurring over a very wide area, crossing international boundaries, and usually affecting a large number of people (e.g., Influenza, COVID-19).
- Zoonoses: Infection transmissible under natural conditions from vertebrate animals to man (e.g., Rabies, Plague).
- Epizootic: Outbreak (epidemic) of disease in an animal population.
- Epornitic: Outbreak of disease in a bird population.
- Enzootic: Endemic occurring in animals.
- Nosocomial Infection: Hospital-acquired infection; develops after 48 hours of hospitalization (surgical wound infection is mostly via direct contact).
- Iatrogenic Disease: Physician/Doctor-generated disease; adverse effects of medical interventions.
- Opportunistic Infection: Infection with organisms that are normally innocuous but become pathogenic when the host's immunological defenses are compromised.
- Ecdemic: Disease that originates outside of the area in which it occurs.
- Exotic: Disease imported into a country which was not otherwise present.

CORE CONCEPTS
Components of Epidemiology
1. Disease Frequency: Measurement of rates, ratios, and proportions.
2. Distribution of Disease: By time, place, and person (Descriptive Epidemiology).
3. Determinants of Disease: Identifying underlying causes or risk factors (Analytical Epidemiology).

Epidemiological Approach
1. Asking Questions: Related to health events (What, Where, When, Who, Why) and health action (What can be done, How to prevent).
2. Making Comparisons: Weighing, balancing, and contrasting study and control groups to find aetiological clues. Ensures comparability through randomization or matching.

Basic Measurements in Epidemiology
1. Rate: Measures the occurrence of a particular event in a defined population during a given time period. Numerator is a part of the denominator. It includes a time multiplier (e.g., per 1000). Expresses probability/risk.
2. Ratio: Expresses a relation between two random quantities. The numerator is NOT a component of the denominator (e.g., Sex ratio).
3. Proportion: A ratio where the numerator is always included in the denominator, usually expressed as a percentage.

Tools of Measurement
- Numerator: Number of times an event occurred.
- Denominator: Mid-year population (assessed on 1st July), Population at-risk (only those capable of acquiring the disease), Person-time (combines persons and time of observation), Total events.

Measurement of Mortality
- Crude Death Rate (CDR): Total deaths in a year per 1000 mid-year population. Limitation: Does not account for age composition.
- Specific Death Rates: Cause-specific, age-specific, sex-specific. Used to identify at-risk groups.
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR): Represents the killing power or virulence of a disease. Total deaths from a disease / Total cases of the same disease x 100. It is a ratio/proportion. Best indicator of severity of an acute disease.
- Proportional Mortality Rate (PMR): Simplest measure to estimate the burden of a disease. Number of deaths from a specific disease / Total deaths from all causes x 100. It is a proportion.
- Survival Rate: Proportion of survivors in a group over a period (e.g., 5 years). Useful for assessing cancer therapies. Potential bias due to earlier diagnosis without prolonged life (Lead time bias).
- Standardized/Adjusted Rates: Used for valid comparison of two populations with different age structures.
  > Direct Standardization: Uses a standard population.
  > Indirect Standardization: Uses Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR). SMR = (Observed deaths / Expected deaths) x 100.

Measurement of Morbidity
1. Incidence: Number of NEW cases occurring in a defined population during a specified period of time. Expressed as a rate. Not influenced by disease duration. Best measure of disease frequency in etiological studies. Used for acute conditions.
2. Prevalence: Total number of ALL current cases (Old + New) existing at a given point in time (Point Prevalence) or over a period of time (Period Prevalence). It is a proportion, not a rate. Tells about disease distribution rather than etiology.

Relationship between Incidence and Prevalence
- Assuming a stable population and unchanging disease rates: Prevalence = Incidence x Mean Duration (P = I x D).
- Incidence reflects the causal factor; Duration reflects the prognostic factor.
- Impact of Interventions:
  > New effective treatment (cures disease): Incidence unchanged, Duration decreases, Prevalence decreases.
  > Treatment prevents death but doesn't cure (prolongs life): Incidence unchanged, Duration increases, Prevalence increases.
  > Improved preventive measure (New Vaccine): Incidence decreases, Duration unchanged, Prevalence decreases.
  > Highly fatal disease: Duration decreases, Prevalence decreases.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL METHODS
I. Observational Studies (Nature takes its course; no intervention)
1. Descriptive Studies: Hypothesis formulation.
2. Analytical Studies: Hypothesis testing.
   - Ecological/Correlational (Unit of study: Population).
   - Cross-sectional/Prevalence (Unit of study: Individual).
   - Case-Control (Unit of study: Individual).
   - Cohort (Unit of study: Individual).
   - Nested Case-Control.
   - Case Series.

II. Experimental Studies (Hypothesis confirmation)
- Randomized Controlled Trials (RCT) (Unit: Patients).
- Field Trials (Unit: Healthy people).
- Community Trials (Unit: Community).

DESCRIPTIVE EPIDEMIOLOGY
Steps: Define population -> Define disease -> Describe disease (Time, Place, Person) -> Measurement -> Compare -> Formulate Hypothesis.

Time Distribution (Fluctuations):
1. Short-term (Epidemics):
   - Point Source / Single Exposure: Explosive, sharp rise and fall, clustering of cases in a narrow interval, all cases within ONE incubation period, no secondary waves (e.g., Food poisoning, Bhopal gas tragedy).
   - Continuous / Multiple Exposure: Sharp rise, but fall is interrupted by secondary waves (e.g., Contaminated well in a village).
   - Propagated: Gradual rise and fall over a long period. Person-to-person transmission. Speed depends on herd immunity and secondary attack rate (e.g., Polio, Measles).
2. Periodic:
   - Seasonal Trend: e.g., Upper respiratory infections in winter, GI infections in summer.
   - Cyclical Trend: e.g., Measles every 2-3 years, Influenza every 7-10 years.
3. Long-term (Secular) Trends: Progressive increase or decrease over decades (e.g., CHD, cancer, diabetes increasing; polio decreasing).

Place Distribution:
- International (Japan: Stomach cancer, India: Oral cancer).
- National (Goiter in North, Lathyrism in Central).
- Rural-Urban (Urban: Accidents, CVD; Rural: Zoonotic, Soil-transmitted helminths).
- Local (Spot maps/Shaded maps). John Snow used spot maps for Cholera.

Person Distribution:
Age, Sex, Marital status, Occupation, Social class. Bimodality: Two peaks in age incidence (e.g., Hodgkin's lymphoma).

ANALYTICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY
1. Ecological Study
- Unit of study is the POPULATION.
- Provides the least satisfactory type of evidence on causality.
- Ecological Fallacy: An error in interpretation where characteristics ascribed to a group are incorrectly assumed to apply to individuals.

2. Cross-Sectional Study
- Also called a prevalence study or snapshot of a population.
- Exposure and outcome are assessed simultaneously at one point in time.
- Cannot establish a temporal sequence (causality).
- Best for finding disease burden.

3. Case-Control Study
- Retrospective / Backward looking (Effect to Cause).
- Both exposure and outcome have already occurred.
- Starts with Disease (Cases) and no disease (Controls).
- Ideal for RARE DISEASES.
- Rapid and inexpensive.
- Employs Matching to remove confounding.
- Cannot measure incidence. Estimates risk via ODDS RATIO (Cross-product ratio = ad/bc).

4. Cohort Study
- Prospective / Forward looking (Cause to Effect).
- Starts with Exposure (cohorts identified prior to disease appearance).
- Ideal for RARE EXPOSURES.
- Long follow-up required, expensive, prone to attrition (loss to follow-up).
- Yields Incidence rates.
- Estimates risk via RELATIVE RISK and ATTRIBUTABLE RISK.
- Types: Prospective (Current), Retrospective (Historical / Non-concurrent), Combined.

5. Nested Case-Control Study
- A case-control study nested within a cohort study.
- Eliminates recall bias (baseline data taken at the start).
- Maintains temporal association.

EXPERIMENTAL EPIDEMIOLOGY
Randomized Controlled Trials (RCT)
- Gold standard for clinical research.
- Used for evaluating new therapies/drugs.
- Randomization: Heart of RCT. Allocates participants into study and control groups. Equalizes extraneous variables. Eliminates SELECTION BIAS.
- Blinding: Eliminates Subject, Observer, and Analyzer bias.
  > Single Blind: Participant unaware.
  > Double Blind: Participant + Investigator unaware (Most frequent).
  > Triple Blind: Participant + Investigator + Analyzer unaware.

Phases of Clinical Trials
- Phase 0: Micro-dosing on healthy volunteers.
- Phase I: Safety & non-toxicity profile. Maximum tolerated dose evaluated.
- Phase II: Efficacy. Maximum drug failure reported here.
- Phase III: Comparison with existing (old) drug. RCT phase. Drug launched to market after Phase III.
- Phase IV: Post-marketing surveillance. Long-term and rare side effects found here.

Meta-Analysis
- Systematic review combining results of several independent studies.
- Increases precision and sample size.
- Forest Plot: Describes distribution of effect size. Size of square = weight of study. Diamond = combined results.
- Funnel Plot: Used to check for Publication Bias.

BIAS & CONFOUNDING
- Bias: Systematic error in study design or data collection.
  > Subject Bias: Recall bias (Cases remember better), Hawthorne effect (Subjects alter behavior because they are observed).
  > Investigator Bias: Selection bias, Berkesonian bias (hospital admission rates differ for different diseases).
  > Lead Time Bias: Overestimation of survival time due to early detection by screening.
  > Neyman (Prevalence-Incidence) Bias: Missing fatal, short-duration, or mild cases.
  > Publication Bias (File Drawer Effect): Tendency to only publish positive results.
- Confounding: A factor associated with both exposure and outcome, and has an independent effect. Addressed by Randomization, Matching, Restriction, Stratification, and Multivariate analysis.

ASSOCIATION AND CAUSATION
Bradford Hill Criteria:
1. Temporal Association (Most important/essential): Cause must precede the effect.
2. Strength of Association: Large Relative Risk.
3. Specificity: One-to-one relationship (Weakest criterion).
4. Consistency: Replicable in different settings.
5. Biological Plausibility: Fits current biological knowledge.
6. Coherence: Supported by known facts.
7. Dose-Response Relationship (Reversibility).

INTERNATIONAL DEATH CERTIFICATE (IDC)
- Used for uniformity in mortality data.
- Part I: Disease directly leading to death.
  > Line I.a: Condition directly leading to death.
  > Line I.b: Intervening antecedent cause.
  > Line I.c: Main underlying cause (The essence of the death certificate, the actual cause).
  > Line I.d: Used if needed.
- Part II: Other significant conditions contributing to death but not related to the disease causing it.

DISEASE TRANSMISSION IN INFECTIOUS DISEASES
Levels of Prevention/Intervention
1. Disease Control: Agent persists but ceases to be a public health problem.
2. Disease Elimination: Interruption of transmission, but organism persists in the environment. India has eliminated: Maternal & Neonatal Tetanus, Yaws, Guinea worm, Leprosy. Next likely targets: Kala-azar, Lymphatic filariasis.
3. Disease Eradication: Complete extermination of the infectious agent globally. All or none phenomenon. Smallpox and Rinderpest are eradicated. Polio type 2 and 3 are eradicated.

Source & Reservoir
- Source: Person, animal, or substance from which the agent passes directly to the host.
- Reservoir: Natural habitat where the agent lives and multiplies. Types: Human, Animal, Non-living (Soil for tetanus).

Cases and Carriers
- Primary Case: First case introduced into the population.
- Index Case: First case to come to the attention of the investigator.
- Secondary Case: Develops from contact with the primary case.
- Suspect Case: Has signs/symptoms but no confirmed diagnosis.
- Carriers: Harbors agent without clinical disease. Less infectious but epidemiologically more dangerous than cases (e.g., Typhoid Mary).
  > Incubatory: Sheds during incubation (Measles, Mumps, Polio).
  > Convalescent: Sheds during recovery (Typhoid, Cholera).
  > Healthy: Subclinical infection but sheds (Polio, Meningococcus).
  > Paradoxical: Acquires from another carrier.

Time Periods
- Incubation Period: Time from invasion by agent to appearance of first sign/symptom. Pathogen undergoes multiplication. Determines period of quarantine.
- Latent Period: Equivalent of incubation period for non-infectious diseases (initiation to detection).
- Window Period: Time between exposure and laboratory detection of the disease.
- Generation Time: Interval between receipt of infection and maximal infectivity of the host.
- Serial Interval: Time gap between onset of primary case and secondary case.

Modes of Transmission
- Direct: Skin-to-skin, droplet (sneezing), contact with soil, transplacental/vertical.
- Indirect: 
  > Vehicle-borne (Water, food, blood - e.g., Hepatitis A, Cholera).
  > Vector-borne: Mechanical (Housefly) or Biological (Propagative: Plague in fleas; Cyclo-developmental: Guinea worm in cyclops; Cyclo-propagative: Malaria in mosquito). 
  > Airborne: Droplet nuclei (TB, Measles) or Dust.
  > Fomite-borne: Contaminated inanimate objects.

Isolation vs Quarantine
- Isolation: Applied to INFECTED persons. For the Period of Communicability. 
- Quarantine: Applied to HEALTHY contacts/well persons exposed to disease. For the maximum Incubation Period.

Emporiatrics (Travel Medicine)
- Deals with health of travelers.
- Yellow Fever: Vaccination certificate is the ONLY legally required certificate for international travel (endemic zones).

Disinfection
- Concurrent: Disinfection applied immediately after discharge of infectious material (e.g., urine/feces).
- Terminal: Applied after the patient is removed (death/discharge).
- Precurrent/Prophylactic: Chlorination of water, pasteurization.

IMMUNIZATION & COLD CHAIN
Types of Vaccines
1. Live Attenuated: Multiplies in host, single dose usually suffices, produces both Humoral and Cellular (IgG & IgA) immunity. Contraindicated in pregnancy and immunocompromised (Exceptions: Yellow fever can be given in HIV if CD4 > 400). Examples: BCG, OPV, Measles, Mumps, Rubella, Yellow Fever, Rotavirus.
2. Inactivated/Killed: Heat or chemically killed. Requires multiple doses. Stimulates only Humoral immunity (IgG). Examples: IPV, Rabies, Hepatitis A, whole-cell Pertussis.
3. Toxoids: Detoxicated exotoxins. Examples: Diphtheria, Tetanus.
4. Subunit/Recombinant: Hepatitis B, HPV.

Specific Vaccine Strains (From Notes)
- BCG: Danish 1331 strain.
- OPV/IPV: P1, P2, P3 strains.
- Measles: Edmonston Zagreb strain.
- Mumps: Jeryl Lynn strain.
- Rubella: RA 27/3 strain.
- Japanese Encephalitis: SA-14-14-2 (cell culture derived live attenuated).
- Swine Flu: A7 / California / 2009.
- Typhoid: Ty 21 A (Oral).
- Malaria: RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix).
- Chickenpox: OKA strain.
- Rotavirus: Rotavac (Monovalent), Rotarix (Monovalent), RotaTeq (Pentavalent, bovine & human reassortant).

Cold Chain
System of storage and transport of vaccines at low temperatures from manufacturer to the actual vaccination site.
- WIC (Walk-in Cooler) & WIF (Walk-in Freezer): State/Regional level.
- ILR (Ice Lined Refrigerator): District/PHC level. Maintains +2 to +8Â°C. Best for holding over during power cuts. Keep freeze-sensitive vaccines at the top, heat-sensitive at the bottom.
- Deep Freezer (DF): Maintains -15 to -25Â°C. Used for OPV bulk storage and freezing ice packs.
- Reverse Cold Chain: Storing and transporting stool samples (e.g., for Polio) from the field to the laboratory at low temperatures.

Temperature Sensitivity
- Most Heat Sensitive: OPV > Measles > BCG.
- Most Freeze Sensitive: Hepatitis B > DPT > TT > Pentavalent. (Do NOT freeze these; use Shake Test to check for damage. If sediment falls faster than control, vaccine is damaged and must be discarded).

Vaccine Vial Monitor (VVM)
Heat-sensitive material (Para-toluene sulphonate) on vaccine vial to register cumulative heat exposure.
- Stage 1: Inner square lighter than outer circle (USE).
- Stage 2: Inner square still lighter (USE).
- Stage 3: Inner square matches outer circle (DISCARD).
- Stage 4: Inner square darker than outer circle (DISCARD).

Open Vial Policy (2015)
Opened multi-dose vials can be used in subsequent sessions for up to 28 days if: expiry date not passed, cold chain maintained, VVM intact, and septum not submerged in water.
- APPLICABLE to: bOPV, IPV, Pentavalent, Hepatitis B, DPT, Td, TT, PCV.
- NOT APPLICABLE to (must discard after 4 hours): BCG, Measles/MR, JE, Rotavirus (live attenuated reconstituted vaccines without preservatives).

Mission Indradhanush
- Launched Dec 2014. Catch-up campaign for unvaccinated/partially vaccinated children.
- 7 colors representing 7 preventable diseases (initially): TB, Polio, Hepatitis B, Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Measles.
- Intensified Mission Indradhanush (IMI): Targets >90% coverage. Covers 12 diseases now.

Specific Vaccines Highlights
- BCG: Intradermal (Tubercle syringe). Leaves permanent scar (Papule -> Ulcer -> Scar). Reconstituted with Normal Saline. Dose: 0.05ml up to 1 month, 0.1ml > 1 month.
- OPV: Sabin (Live). Gives humoral and gut immunity. VAPP/VDPV risk. Switch from tOPV to bOPV in 2016.
- IPV: Salk (Killed). Fractional dose (f-IPV) given intradermally (0.1 ml) at 6 and 14 weeks. No risk of VAPP.
- Measles: Subcutaneous. Reconstituted with sterile water. Prevents SSPE. Toxic shock syndrome if used >4 hours after reconstitution.
- DPT: Given IM. Pertussis whole cell is reactogenic. Adjuvant: Aluminum phosphate (increases immunogenicity). Preservative: Thiomersal. Contraindicated in progressive neurological disease (use DT instead).
- PCV: Conjugated. Given IM at 6 & 14 weeks, booster at 9 months.
- Pentavalent: Replaces DPT, Hep B, Hib. Given IM.
- MIP (Mycobacterium Indicus Pranii): Leprosy vaccine. Decreases bacterial load.

Adverse Events Following Immunization (AEFI)
1. Vaccine product-related (e.g., extensive limb swelling post-DTP).
2. Vaccine quality defect-related (e.g., failure to inactivate polio vaccine -> paralytic polio).
3. Immunization error-related (e.g., non-sterile injection causing toxic shock syndrome / Staphylococcus).
4. Immunization anxiety-related (e.g., Vasovagal syncope/fainting).
5. Coincidental (e.g., fever due to malaria coincidentally happening after vaccination).

KEY POINTS
- The denominator of Case Fatality Rate is Total cases of the same disease.
- SMR is an example of Indirect Standardization.
- Incidence measures absolute risk. Relative Risk measures the strength of association.
- Randomization is the heart of an RCT and eliminates selection bias.
- Blinding eliminates investigator and subject bias.
- Herd immunity does not protect the individual in the case of Tetanus.
- Eradication is an "all or none" global phenomenon.
- Quarantine limits movement of healthy contacts; Isolation separates cases.
- BCG diluent is Normal Saline; Measles diluent is Distilled Water.
- Reconstituted BCG, Measles, and JE must be discarded after 4 hours (JE after 2 hours).
- VVM monitors cumulative heat exposure, not freezing. The Shake Test detects freeze damage.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
- Crude Death Rate (CDR) = (Total deaths in a year / Total mid-year population) x 1000
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR) = (Total deaths from a specific disease / Total cases of the same disease) x 100
- Proportional Mortality Rate (PMR) = (Total deaths from a specific disease / Total deaths from all causes) x 100
- Incidence = (Number of NEW cases / Population at risk) x 1000
- Prevalence = Incidence x Mean Duration (P = I x D)
- Secondary Attack Rate (SAR) = (Number of exposed persons developing disease within IP / Total number of susceptible contacts) x 100
- Odds Ratio (OR) = (a x d) / (b x c) [Cross-product ratio in Case-Control]
- Relative Risk (RR) = Incidence among exposed / Incidence among non-exposed
- Attributable Risk (AR) = ((Incidence exposed - Incidence non-exposed) / Incidence exposed) x 100
- Population Attributable Risk (PAR) = ((Incidence in total pop - Incidence in non-exposed) / Incidence in total pop) x 100
- Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) = (Observed deaths / Expected deaths) x 100

MNEMONICS
- Types of Bias in Meta-Analysis: GIGO (Garbage In Garbage Out), Apple & Oranges effect, File drawer effect.
- Live vaccines: BCG, OPV(Sabin), MR, Rota vaccine, Yellow fever, Rubella, Varicella.
- Freeze Sensitive Vaccines (Do not freeze): DPT, TT, Pentavalent, Hep B, IPV, PCV. (These get ruined and fail the shake test).

title: Screening for Disease
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The chapter focuses on the principles and methodologies of screening for unrecognized diseases in apparently healthy populations. It covers the iceberg phenomenon of disease, the crucial differences between screening and diagnostic tests, the concept of lead time, types of screening, criteria for evaluating diseases and screening tests, and methods for assessing the effectiveness of screening programs.

DEFINITIONS
Screening: Defined as the active search for unrecognized disease or defect by means of rapidly applied tests, examinations, or other procedures in apparently healthy individuals. It is a presumptive identification of disease.
Lead Time: The period between diagnosis by early detection (via screening) and the usual time of diagnosis. It is the advantage gained by screening.
Validity (Accuracy): The ability of a test to accurately measure what it purports to measure, distinguishing those who have the disease from those who do not.
Repeatability (Reliability/Precision): The ability of a test to give consistent results when repeated more than once on the same individual or material under the same conditions.
Yield: The amount of previously unrecognized disease that is diagnosed as a result of the screening effort.

CORE CONCEPTS

Iceberg Phenomenon of Disease
- Epidemiologists observe that the pattern of disease in hospitals is quite different from that in the community. A far larger proportion of disease (e.g., diabetes, hypertension) is hidden from view.
- Submerged portion: Represents the hidden mass of disease (sub-clinical cases, carriers, undiagnosed cases).
- Floating tip: Represents what the physician sees in clinical practice (overt or apparent disease).
- The hidden part constitutes the mass of unrecognized disease in the community, and its detection is a primary challenge in preventive medicine.

Concept of Screening
- Screening is a fundamental aspect of prevention. 
- It differs from periodic health examinations in three major respects: 
  1. Capable of wide application.
  2. Relatively inexpensive.
  3. Requires little physician-time (the physician is not required to administer the test, only to interpret it).

Difference between Screening Test & Diagnostic Test
- Done on: Screening (Apparently Healthy individuals) vs. Diagnostic (Sick individuals or those with indications).
- Applied to: Screening (Groups) vs. Diagnostic (Single Patient).
- Based on: Screening (Cut-off Criteria / One criterion) vs. Diagnostic (Evaluation of Signs/Symptoms/Lab Findings).
- Test Results: Screening (Arbitrary & Final) vs. Diagnostic (Not Final / Modifiable).
- Time taken: Screening (Rapid) vs. Diagnostic (Time Consuming).
- Accuracy: Screening (Less Accurate) vs. Diagnostic (More Accurate).
- Expense: Screening (Less Expensive) vs. Diagnostic (More Expensive).
- Basis of Treatment: Screening (No) vs. Diagnostic (Yes).
- Done by/Initiative: Screening (Health Care providing Agencies / Investigator) vs. Diagnostic (Doctor's advice / Patient with a complaint).

Lead Time and Lead Time Bias
- 1st Possible Point of Diagnosis: The earliest point a disease can be detected. A screening test CANNOT detect a disease before it.
- Final Critical Point: The point after which treatment of a disease may NOT yield the Desirable Beneficial Effects.
- A screening test is most useful if applied between the 1st Possible Point of Diagnosis and the Final Critical Point.
- Lead Time Bias: Occurs when the survival time appears longer simply because the diagnosis was done earlier (by screening), irrespective of whether the patient actually lived longer. In cases of long Lead Time without significant advancement in the treatment of the disease, there is a VIRTUAL increase in Survival Rate or a decrease in Mortality.

Types of Screening
1. Mass Screening: Screening of a Whole Population. It is employed for a disease with high prevalence (e.g., Chest X-Ray for TB on a large population). 
2. High Risk / Selective Screening: The Most Economical & Best screening method. Applied selectively to High-Risk Groups (e.g., Cervical Cancer screening on Low SES Females; DM screening on people having a Family History of DM; HIV screening on High-Risk Groups like Prostitutes, IV Drug Users, Truck Drivers).
3. Multiphasic Screening: Application of 2 or more Screening Tests in combination to a large number of people at one time (e.g., Annual Health Checkups).

Uses / Objectives of Screening
1. Case Detection (Prescriptive Screening): People are screened for their Own Benefit (e.g., Breast Cancer, Cervical Cancer, Anemia, Diabetes). Represents Secondary Prevention (Early Diagnosis).
2. Control of Disease (Prospective Screening): People are examined for the Benefit of Others (e.g., Tuberculosis, Screening of Immigrants, HIV screening among Sex Workers). Represents Primary Prevention (Specific Protection).
3. Research Purposes: For diseases whose Natural History is not fully known.
4. Educational Opportunities: For creating Public Awareness.

Screening for Diseases at Different Age Groups
- Pregnancy: Anemia, Hypertension, Diabetes.
- Infancy: Congenital Heart Disease, Haemoglobinopathies, Sickle Cell Anemia.
- Middle Aged Person: Obesity, Hypertension, Diabetes.
- Elderly: Cancer, Cataract, Glaucoma.

Examples of Screening Tests
- Cervical Cancer: Pap Smear, VIA (Visual Inspection with Acetic acid at PHC).
- Endometrial Cancer: USG, Office Endometrial Washing.
- Breast Cancer: BSE (Breast Self-Examination; best time is 1 week after menses), Mammography.
- Prostate Cancer: DRE (Digital Rectal Examination), PSA (Prostate Specific Antigen).
- Colorectal Cancer: Fecal Occult Blood Test.
- Oral Cancer: Bimanual Oral Examination.
- HIV: ELISA, RAPID Test.
- DM: Urine for Sugar, Random Blood Sugar, Fasting Blood Sugar (most reliable).
- Developmental Anomalies in Fetus: AFP (Alpha-Feto-Protein).
- TB: Chest X-Ray for TB on large population.
- Vitamin D Deficiency: 25 Hydroxy Vitamin D.

Criteria for Diseases to be Screened & Screening Test

- It should be an Important Health Problem.
- A recognizable latent or early asymptomatic stage must exist.
- Natural history of disease can be adequately understood.
- Effective Treatment should be available.
- Diagnostic Test or Examination should be available.
- 


1. Acceptability: Discomforting screening tests are not likely to be acceptable (e.g., PR or PV examination).
2. Repeatability / Consistency / Reproducibility / Reliability (Precision): Gives consistent results when repeated more than once on the same individual under same conditions. It gives the extent of Variation & measurement of Contained Behavior. Evaluated using a Range Chart (R chart). Influenced by:
   -  Intra-observer (within the same observer) and Inter-observer (between observers).
   -  E.g., fluctuations in blood pressure, regression to the mean.
   -  Defective instruments, faulty reagents.
3. Validity (Accuracy): Means close to True or Actual Value. Ability of the test to separate those who have the disease from those who don't. Measured using Mean Chart, Levy Jennings (LJ) Chart, Shewhart Control Chart. It has two components:
   - Sensitivity
   - Specificity

Components of Validity: Sensitivity & Specificity
- Sensitivity: Ability of a test to identify all those who have the Disease (True Positives). 
  - It measures the ability to correctly diagnose the percentage of Sick People who are having the condition.
  - 
  - Interpretation: If Sensitivity is 90%, it means 90% of diseased people screened by the test will give a True Positive result, and the remaining 10% will give a False Negative.
  - A screening test with High Sensitivity suggests that those testing Positive are likely to have the Disease (especially if Disease Prevalence is high).
  - If True Positive (TP) is High, it means Prevalence is High. If False Positive (FP) is High, it means Prevalence is Low.
  -  It is the most important factor for being a good screening test.
- Specificity: Ability of a test to identify correctly all those who do NOT have the disease (True Negatives).
  - 
  - Interpretation: If specificity is 90%, 90% of non-diseased persons will give a True Negative result, and 10% will give a False Positive.

Predictivity of Screening Test
- Reflects the Diagnostic Power of the Test. Depends on Sensitivity, Specificity, and Prevalence of a Disease.
- Positive Predictive Value (PPV): Ability of a screening test to identify correctly all those who have the disease, out of all those who are Tested Positive. 
  - PPV is directly proportional to Prevalence (PPV Î± Prevalence).
  - 
- Negative Predictive Value (NPV): Ability of a test to identify correctly all those who do NOT have the disease, out of all those who are Tested Negative. 
  - Reflects the diagnostic power of a test to correctly Exclude the Disease.
  - NPV is inversely proportional to Prevalence (NPV Î± 1/Prevalence).
- Errors in Predictivity:
  - False Positive Error Rate = Type I Error.
  - False Negative Error Rate = Type II Error.
  -  (as it burdens diagnostic facilities and causes anxiety among healthy individuals).

Ideal Screening Test & Screening Programs
- An Ideal Screening Test should be Acceptable, Repeatable, Valid, Safe, Rapid, Simple, Cheap & Easily administrable. It should be 100% Sensitive & 100% Specific (but practically, NO test achieves this).
- A screening test with High Sensitivity has few False Negatives. High Specificity has few False Positives.
- Screening in General Population: Done in cancers of Colon, Pancreas, Breast, Cervix. It is NOT routinely recommended for Testicular Cancer, Lung Cancer, or Ovarian Cancer.
- Among Cancers, Colorectal Cancer is ideal for screening as it is common in both Men & Women and is curable if detected in early stages. Maximum Survival Rate is seen in screening for Colon Cancer.
- Blood Screening is routinely done for HIV, HBV, HCV.
- National Screening Programs include: DM under NPCDCS, Carcinoma Cervix under NPCDCS, Refractory Error under NPCBVI.

Screening Tests in Series & Parallel
- Tests in Series: 1st Screening Test is followed by 2nd Screening Test. The 2nd test is applied only on those individuals whose 1st Screening Test is Positive.
  - Combined Sensitivity: Decreases
  - Combined Specificity: Increases
  - Combined PPV: Increases
  - Combined NPV: Decreases
- Tests in Parallel: 2 or more Screening Tests applied at the Same Time. Each individual is subjected to Both (All) Tests.
  - Combined Sensitivity: Increases
  - Combined Specificity: Decreases
  - Combined PPV: Decreases
  - Combined NPV: Increases

Interpretation of Distribution Curve (Shifting Cut-offs)
- For unimodal distributions (e.g., blood sugar, blood pressure), there is no sharp dividing line between normal and diseased.
- If Cut-off value is shifted to the LEFT:
  - False Positive (FP) is increased, so Specificity is decreased.
  - False Negative (FN) is decreased, so Sensitivity is increased.
- If Cut-off value is shifted to the RIGHT:
  - False Negative (FN) is increased, so Sensitivity is decreased.
  - False Positive (FP) is decreased, so Specificity is increased.

Likelihood Ratio
- Provides a direct estimate of how much a Test result will change the Odds of having a Disease.
- LR of Positive result: Tells how much the Odds of Disease Increase when a Test is Positive.
- LR of Negative result: Tells how much the Odds of Disease Decrease when a Test is Negative.

Receiver Operator Characteristic (ROC) Curve
- A plot between Sensitivity (True Positive Rate) on the Y-axis & 1 - Specificity (False Positive Rate) on the X-axis.
- Used for Comparison of 2 or more Screening Tests.
- Best ROC: Maximum Sensitivity & Minimum 1-Specificity (located Up & Left on the graph).
- It determines the Threshold of screening for a Positive Test.
- Area Under Curve (AUC): Represents the Overall Performance of the test.
- Youden Index (J): Enables selection of the Optimal Threshold Value (Cutoff). Tells about the Utilization of investigation.

Evaluation of Screening Programmes
- Randomized Controlled Trials (RCT): Ideal method of evaluation, comparing screened group with unscreened group. Often prohibitive due to cost and length.
- Uncontrolled Trials: Seeing if people with disease detected through screening appear to live longer. Susceptible to lead time bias.
- Other methods: Case control studies and comparison in trends between areas with different degrees of screening coverage.

KEY POINTS
- Sensitivity denotes the usefulness of a screening test and is the most important factor for being a good screening test.
- PPV is the most affected parameter by changes in disease prevalence.
- Type 1 error (False Positives) amounts to giving healthy people unnecessary anxiety and diagnostic procedures, which can discredit a screening program.
- Specificity and Sensitivity are inversely related; increasing one will automatically decrease the other depending on the cut-off point chosen.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
Validity Measures:
- Sensitivity = [True Positive / Total Diseased (i.e., TP + FN)] Ã— 100
- Specificity = [True Negative / Total Non-Diseased (i.e., FP + TN)] Ã— 100

Predictivity Measures:
- Positive Predictive Value (PPV) = [True Positive / Total Positive (i.e., TP + FP)] Ã— 100
- Negative Predictive Value (NPV) = [True Negative / Total Negative (i.e., TN + FN)] Ã— 100

Error Rates:
- False Positive Error Rate (Type I Error) = 1 - Specificity
- False Negative Error Rate (Type II Error) = 1 - Sensitivity

Tests in Series & Parallel:
- Series Combined Sensitivity = Sensitivity A Ã— Sensitivity B
- Series Combined Specificity = (Specificity A + Specificity B) - (Specificity A Ã— Specificity B)
- Parallel Combined Sensitivity = (Sensitivity A + Sensitivity B) - (Sensitivity A Ã— Sensitivity B)
- Parallel Combined Specificity = Specificity A Ã— Specificity B

Likelihood Ratio (LR):
- LR+ = Sensitivity / (1 - Specificity)
- LR- = (1 - Sensitivity) / Specificity

ROC Curve Parameters:
- Youden Index (J) = Sensitivity + Specificity - 1

Bayes' Theorem:
- PPV =[(Sensitivity Ã— Prevalence) / {(Sensitivity Ã— Prevalence) + (1 - Specificity) Ã— (1 - Prevalence)}] Ã— 100
- NPV =[(Specificity Ã— (1 - Prevalence)) / {(Specificity Ã— (1 - Prevalence)) + (1 - Sensitivity) Ã— (Prevalence)}] Ã— 100

Actual Bayes' Theorem:
- Post Test Probability (PTP) is the same as PPV. Pre Test Probability (pTP) is the same as Prevalence.
- PTP =[(Sensitivity Ã— pTP) / {(Sensitivity Ã— pTP) + (1 - Specificity) Ã— (1 - pTP)}] Ã— 100

title: Epidemiology of Communicable Diseases
content:
PART I: GENERAL EPIDEMIOLOGY OF COMMUNICABLE DISEASES
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers the dynamics of infectious disease transmission. It details the chain of infection, the various stages of infection, types of carriers, and the critical public health interventions used to break the chain of transmission, including immunization, isolation, and cold chain logistics.


DEFINITIONS
INCUBATION PERIOD: The time interval between the invasion by an infectious agent and the appearance of the first sign or symptom of the disease.
GENERATION TIME: The time interval between the receipt of infection by a host and the maximal infectivity of that host.
SERIAL INTERVAL: The gap in time between the onset of disease in the primary case and the onset of disease in the secondary case.
SECONDARY ATTACK RATE (SAR): The number of exposed persons developing the disease within the range of one incubation period following exposure to a primary case.
ZOONOSIS: An infection or infectious disease transmissible under natural conditions from vertebrate animals to man.

CORE CONCEPTS
DYNAMICS OF DISEASE TRANSMISSION: Transmission requires a Source or Reservoir, a Mode of Transmission, and a Susceptible Host.

CARRIERS: An infected person or animal that harbors a specific infectious agent in the absence of discernible clinical disease but serves as a potential source of infection. 
Types include:
1. INCUBATORY CARRIER: Sheds agent during incubation (e.g., Measles, Mumps).
2. CONVALESCENT CARRIER: Sheds agent during recovery (e.g., Typhoid, Cholera).
3. HEALTHY CARRIER: Sheds agent without ever showing symptoms (e.g., Polio).
COLD CHAIN AND VVM: The logistical system of transporting and storing vaccines at recommended temperatures from manufacturer to the point of use.
- ILR (ICE LINED REFRIGERATOR): Maintains +2 C to +8 C. The most reliable equipment for PHCs due to prolonged cold life during power cuts.
- DEEP FREEZER: Maintains -15 C to -25 C. Used for storing OPV and freezing ice packs.

KEY POINTS
Generation time is often shorter than the incubation period, meaning a person can be highly infectious before they even know they are sick (e.g., COVID-19, Measles).
Typhoid carriers primarily harbor the bacteria in the gallbladder. Fecal carriers are more common and more dangerous than urinary carriers.
Vaccine Vial Monitors (VVM) indicate cumulative heat exposure. If the inner square is lighter than the outer circle, the vaccine is usable. If it matches or is darker, discard it immediately.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
SECONDARY ATTACK RATE = (NUMBER OF EXPOSED PERSONS DEVELOPING THE DISEASE WITHIN ONE INCUBATION PERIOD / TOTAL NUMBER OF SUSCEPTIBLE CONTACTS) X 100

MNEMONICS
VVM STAGES EVALUATION (L L M D):
L - Lighter (Usable)
L - Lighter, but progressing (Usable)
M - Matches (Discard)
D - Darker (Discard)


--- PART II: RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter focuses on major respiratory droplet-borne infections, detailing their epidemiology, clinical hallmarks, severe complications, and prevention strategies. It emphasizes highly contagious viral diseases like Measles, Rubella, Influenza, and Mumps, as well as bacterial infections like Diphtheria.

DEFINITIONS
DROPLET NUCLEI: Microscopic aerosolized particles (less than 5 microns) containing infectious agents that can remain suspended in the air for long periods and travel long distances.
ANTIGENIC DRIFT: Minor, gradual mutations in the hemagglutinin (HA) or neuraminidase (NA) antigens of the influenza virus, leading to seasonal epidemics.
ANTIGENIC SHIFT: Major, sudden changes in the HA or NA antigens of the influenza A virus (often through genetic reassortment with animal strains), leading to global pandemics.
CONGENITAL RUBELLA SYNDROME (CRS): Fetal anomalies occurring when a pregnant woman is infected with the Rubella virus, especially during the first trimester.

CORE CONCEPTS
MEASLES (RUBEOLA): RNA Paramyxovirus. Highly contagious (SAR > 90%). Koplik spots appear on the buccal mucosa 1-2 days before the maculopapular rash. 
RUBELLA (GERMAN MEASLES): RNA Togavirus. Mild in adults but teratogenic in fetuses. The classic CRS triad consists of Sensorineural Deafness, Cataracts, and Cardiac malformations (primarily Patent Ductus Arteriosus).
INFLUENZA: Orthomyxovirus. Causes rapid outbreaks. Shift vs Drift is a highly tested concept explaining why vaccines must be updated annually.
DIPHTHERIA: Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Characterized by a tough, grayish-white pseudomembrane over the tonsils/pharynx that bleeds upon scraping. Toxin production is evaluated by the Elek test.

KEY POINTS
Otitis media is the most common complication of measles.
Pneumonia is the most common cause of death in measles.
Subacute Sclerosing Panencephalitis (SSPE) is a rare, fatal, late complication of measles.
Administration of Vitamin A (1 lakh to 2 lakh IU) significantly reduces mortality and severity of measles in children.
Mumps commonly causes epididymo-orchitis in post-pubertal males (usually unilateral, rarely leading to complete sterility) and oophoritis in females.
The Schick test historically determined susceptibility to Diphtheria, but is largely obsolete now due to universal immunization.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
CRS TRIAD (D C P):
D - Deafness (Sensorineural)
C - Cataracts
P - Patent Ductus Arteriosus (Cardiac defect)
COMPLICATIONS OF MEASLES (O P S):
O - Otitis media (Most common)
P - Pneumonia (Most fatal)
S - SSPE (Late, fatal neurological)


--- PART III: INTESTINAL INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers major communicable diseases transmitted primarily through the feco-oral route and parenterally. It explores the epidemiology, clinical manifestations, prevention, and public health control strategies for Poliomyelitis, Viral Hepatitis, Cholera, Typhoid, and other food-borne or water-borne pathogens.

DEFINITIONS
VAPP: Vaccine-Associated Paralytic Poliomyelitis. A rare adverse event following the administration of live Oral Poliovirus Vaccine (OPV) where the attenuated virus reverts to neurovirulence.
CVDPV: Circulating Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus. Mutated strains of the OPV virus that regain neurovirulence and transmissibility, causing outbreaks in communities with low immunization coverage.
CARRIER STATE (TYPHOID): An individual who excretes Salmonella typhi in feces or urine for more than one year after the acute illness.
FULMINANT HEPATITIS: A severe, rapidly progressive form of hepatitis resulting in massive hepatic necrosis and liver failure.

CORE CONCEPTS
POLIOMYELITIS: Caused by Poliovirus (Types 1, 2, 3). India is officially certified polio-free. The Polio Endgame Strategy involves the phased withdrawal of OPV (starting with the type 2 component) and the introduction of Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine (IPV) to prevent VAPP and cVDPV.
VIRAL HEPATITIS:
ENTERICALLY TRANSMITTED: Hepatitis A and E (Feco-oral route). Hepatitis E is particularly dangerous in the third trimester of pregnancy.
PARENTERALLY TRANSMITTED: Hepatitis B, C, and D (Blood, sexual contact, vertical transmission). Hepatitis B virus is a DNA virus, while the others are RNA viruses. Chronic infection is marked by the persistence of HBsAg for more than 6 months. HBeAg positivity indicates high viral replication and maximum infectivity.
CHOLERA: Caused by Vibrio cholerae. Characterized by sudden onset of painless, profuse, watery diarrhea (rice-water stools) and vomiting, leading to severe dehydration and cardiovascular collapse. The mainstay of treatment is prompt rehydration with Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) or intravenous fluids (Ringer's Lactate).
TYPHOID FEVER: Caused by Salmonella typhi. Characterized by sustained step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, coated tongue, and rose spots. The gallbladder is the principal reservoir for chronic fecal carriers.

KEY POINTS
HEPATITIS E IN PREGNANCY: Carries a disproportionately high mortality rate (up to 20 percent) due to fulminant hepatic failure.
NEEDLE STICK INJURY RULE OF 3S: The risk of transmission following a percutaneous injury from a known positive source is roughly 30 percent for Hepatitis B, 3 percent for Hepatitis C, and 0.3 percent for HIV.
CHOLERA DIAGNOSIS: The classic rapid diagnostic sign under a microscope is the darting motility of Vibrio cholerae in a hanging drop preparation.
TYPHOID CARRIERS: Fecal carriers are more common and pose a significantly higher public health risk than urinary carriers. Chronic carriage is heavily associated with gallstones.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT HEAVILY FORMULA-BASED. HOWEVER, ORS OSMOLARITY MUST BE KNOWN: WHO STANDARD LOW OSMOLARITY ORS IS 245 MOSM/L.

MNEMONICS
HEPATITIS TRANSMISSION (VOWELS FOR BOWELS):
HEPATITIS A AND E (THE VOWELS) ARE TRANSMITTED VIA THE BOWELS (FECO-ORAL).
HEPATITIS B, C, AND D ARE TRANSMITTED VIA BLOOD AND BODY FLUIDS.


--- PART IV: ARTHROPOD-BORNE INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter examines diseases transmitted by arthropod vectors, representing a massive public health burden in India. It details the vector bionomics, transmission cycles, clinical features, and integrated vector management strategies for Malaria, Dengue, Chikungunya, Japanese Encephalitis, and Lymphatic Filariasis.

DEFINITIONS
DEFINITIVE HOST: The host in which the parasite undergoes its sexual cycle of reproduction (e.g., the mosquito in Malaria).
INTERMEDIATE HOST: The host in which the parasite undergoes its asexual cycle of reproduction (e.g., humans in Malaria).
EXTRINSIC INCUBATION PERIOD: The time required for the development of the disease agent in the vector, from the time of blood meal to the time the vector becomes infective.
BIOLOGICAL TRANSMISSION: Occurs when the agent multiplies or undergoes developmental changes within the vector. Types include Propagative (Plague bacilli in flea), Cyclo-developmental (Filarial parasite in mosquito), and Cyclo-propagative (Malaria parasite in mosquito).

CORE CONCEPTS
MALARIA: Transmitted by female Anopheles mosquitoes. Plasmodium falciparum is the most dangerous species, causing severe/cerebral malaria due to its ability to cause sequestration of infected red blood cells in deep microvasculature. Radical cure for P. vivax and P. ovale requires 14 days of Primaquine to eradicate liver hypnozoites and prevent relapse.
DENGUE: Transmitted by Aedes aegypti (a day-biting mosquito that breeds in clean, stagnant artificial water containers). Dengue Hemorrhagic Fever (DHF) is characterized by increased vascular permeability, leading to plasma leakage, hemoconcentration (rising hematocrit), and thrombocytopenia.
CHIKUNGUNYA: Also transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes. Clinically distinguished from Dengue by the presence of severe, prolonged, and debilitating polyarthralgia (joint pain) that can persist for months.
JAPANESE ENCEPHALITIS (JE): Transmitted by Culex tritaeniorhynchus, which breeds in flooded rice paddies. The transmission cycle involves Ardeid birds as natural reservoirs and pigs as amplifying hosts. Humans are dead-end hosts because the viremia is insufficient to infect feeding mosquitoes.
LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS: Caused predominantly by Wuchereria bancrofti and transmitted by Culex quinquefasciatus. Characterized by nocturnal periodicity of microfilariae in the peripheral blood. Control relies heavily on Mass Drug Administration (MDA).

KEY POINTS
ANOPHELES MOSQUITO VECTORS IN INDIA: Anopheles culicifacies (rural/plains), Anopheles stephensi (urban), Anopheles fluviatilis (hills/forests).
WARNING SIGNS IN DENGUE: Severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hepatomegaly, and a rising hematocrit with a rapid drop in platelet count.
TOURNIQUET TEST: A clinical test used in Dengue to assess capillary fragility. It is considered positive if 10 or more petechiae per square inch are observed after inflating a blood pressure cuff midway between systolic and diastolic pressure for 5 minutes.
PIGS IN JE: Pigs act as the ultimate amplifying host for the JE virus. The virus multiplies exponentially in the pig without causing any clinical disease to the animal itself.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
ANNUAL PARASITE INCIDENCE (API) = (CONFIRMED CASES DURING 1 YEAR / POPULATION UNDER SURVEILLANCE) X 1000
ANNUAL BLOOD EXAMINATION RATE (ABER) = (NUMBER OF BLOOD SMEARS EXAMINED IN A YEAR / TOTAL POPULATION) X 100

MNEMONICS
TYPES OF BIOLOGICAL TRANSMISSION (P C CP):
PROPAGATIVE: PLAGUE (Multiplies only)
CYCLO-DEVELOPMENTAL: FILARIASIS (Changes stage only, no multiplication)
CYCLO-PROPAGATIVE: MALARIA (Multiplies AND changes stage)


--- PART V: ZOONOSES AND SURFACE INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers infectious diseases naturally transmitted between vertebrate animals and humans (Zoonoses), as well as diseases acquired through contact with contaminated soil or surfaces. It provides an exhaustive look at Rabies, Brucellosis, Plague, Tetanus, and Leprosy, focusing on highly tested post-exposure prophylaxis guidelines.

DEFINITIONS
ZOONOSES: Infections or infectious diseases transmissible under natural conditions from vertebrate animals to man.
ANTHROPOZOONOSES: Infections transmitted to man from lower vertebrate animals (e.g., Rabies).
ZOOANTHROPONOSES: Infections transmitted from man to lower vertebrate animals (e.g., Human tuberculosis to cattle).
