As a result, a major national programme, renamed as the "National Iodine Deficiency Disorders (IDD) Control Programme", was initiated. The new approach shifted the focus from area-specific use to nation-wide promotion of iodized salt.

It was decided as a national policy to fortify all edible salt in a phased manner by the end of the 8th Five Year Plan (and supported by policies initiated since the 3rd Five Year Plan).

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- To assess the magnitude of Iodine Deficiency Disorders (IDD) through surveys in districts.
- Supply of iodized salt in place of common salt.
- Resurveys to assess iodine deficiency disorders and the impact of iodized salt after every 5 years in districts.
- Laboratory monitoring of iodized salt and urinary iodine excretion.
- Health education and publicity.

COMPONENTS AND STRATEGIES

The essential components of the national IDD programme are:
- Use of iodized salt in place of common salt.
- Monitoring and surveillance.
- Manpower training.
- Mass communication.

CURRENT STATUS AND ACHIEVEMENTS

- Notification banning the sale of non-iodized salt for human consumption in the entire country has been issued under the "Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 and Regulations 2011".
- For effective implementation of the National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme, 34 States/UTs have established Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Cells in their State Health Directorate.
- Consequent upon the liberalization of iodized salt production, the Salt Commissioner has issued licenses to 824 salt manufacturers, out of which 777 units have commenced production.
- These manufacturing units have an annual production capacity of 222 lakh metric tonnes of iodized salt.
- The production and supply of iodized salt from April 2015 to August 2015 stood at 26.44 lakh tonnes and 25.12 lakh tonnes, respectively.

subsections.title: Universal Immunization Programme (UIP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The Expanded Programme on Immunization (EPI) was launched by the WHO in 1974 globally and introduced in India in 1978. The primary objective was to reduce mortality and morbidity from vaccine-preventable diseases of childhood and achieve self-sufficiency in the production of vaccines.

In 1985, the programme was renamed as the Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) in India. The aim was to achieve 100 percent coverage of pregnant women with 2 doses of tetanus toxoid (or a booster dose) and at least 85 percent coverage of infants with 3 doses each of DPT and OPV, one dose of BCG, and one dose of measles vaccine by 1990.

UIP targets 12 vaccine-preventable diseases: Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Polio, Measles, Rubella, severe form of Childhood Tuberculosis, Hepatitis B, and Meningitis & Pneumonia caused by Haemophilus Influenzae type B, Japanese Encephalitis, Pneumococcal Pneumonia, and Rotavirus diarrhea.

PULSE POLIO IMMUNIZATION PROGRAMME

The Pulse Polio Immunization Programme was launched in the country in the year 1995. 

KEY MILESTONES AND TARGETS
- 1st round: Conducted over 2 immunization days 6 weeks apart on 9th December 1995 and 20th January 1996.
- Target age: Initially targeted children under 3 years of age. Later on, as per WHO guidelines, it was expanded to children under 5 years of age.
- Pulse: Refers to the sudden simultaneous mass administration of OPV on a single day to all children 0-5 years of age, regardless of their previous immunization status.
- PPI rounds occur in 2 rounds about 4 to 6 weeks apart during the low transmission season of polio, i.e., between November to February.

STEPS TAKEN TO MAINTAIN POLIO FREE STATUS
- All states and UTs to develop Rapid Response Teams (RRT) and Emergency Preparedness and Response Plans (EPRP).
- In UP and Bihar, every newborn is vaccinated and tracked for 8 subsequent rounds.
- Apart from vaccinating at fixed booths and through house-to-house visits, vaccination is also done at railway stations, bus stops, and religious congregations.
- Special booths are established in areas bordering neighboring countries like Wagah border, Attari station, and Munabo train station.
- High level of vigilance is maintained through surveillance across the country for Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus (VDPV).
- Multi-pronged strategy implemented to ensure sanitation, hygiene, and clean drinking water in 107 high-risk blocks.
- Social mobilizing activities are conducted by involving local influencers, community, and religious leaders.
- Emergency stock of OPV is maintained to respond to Wild Poliovirus (WPV) and circulating Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus (cVDPV).

POLIO ENDGAME STRATEGIC PLAN
- Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine (IPV) introduced into the national immunization programme from November 2015.
- Trivalent OPV (tOPV) switched to Bivalent OPV (bOPV) from April 2016 (The Big Switch).

MISSION INDRADHANUSH

Launched by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare in December 2014.

GOALS AND STRATEGY
- Indradhanush depicts 7 colours of the rainbow for prevention against 7 vaccine-preventable diseases.
- Diseases included: Diphtheria, Whooping cough, Tetanus, Polio, Tubercular meningitis, Measles, and Hepatitis B. It also includes Japanese Encephalitis (JE) and Haemophilus influenzae type B in selected districts.
- Pregnant women are also immunized against tetanus.
- Objective: To fully immunize children who are either unvaccinated or partially vaccinated and those who have not been covered during routine vaccination sessions.

PHASES OF MISSION INDRADHANUSH
- 1st Phase: 
  - 1st round started from 7th April 2015.
  - Covered 201 high-focus districts.
  - Carried out for more than a week.
  - Followed by 3 rounds in the months of May, June, and July 2015, starting from the 7th of each month.
- 2nd Phase: 
  - Started from 7th October 2015.
  - Covered 352 districts.
  - Consisted of 4 rounds.
  - Covering all children under 2 years of age and pregnant women.

MEASLES-RUBELLA (MR) CAMPAIGN

The Government of India expanded the measles vaccine across the country in 1985 under the UIP. In 2010, a second dose of measles was introduced at 16-24 months of age, which led to a significant reduction in the disease burden. Recently, the Measles-Rubella (MR) Vaccine was introduced through a phased campaign.

GOAL
- Elimination of measles and control of rubella and Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS) by 2020.
- Achieve and maintain at least 95 percent population immunity.

PHASING AND TARGETS
- Targets over 400 million children in 36 states and UTs in a span of two years. Included under the National Health Mission (NHM).
- 1st Phase: February 2017 in 5 states (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Goa, Lakshadweep, and Puducherry).
- Next Round: August 2017 in 8 states/UTs (Andhra Pradesh, Chandigarh, Dadra & Nagar Haveli, Daman & Diu, Himachal Pradesh, Kerala, Telangana, and Uttarakhand).

STRATEGY FOR MR CAMPAIGNS
- MR vaccine introduced through a campaign in a phased manner across the country without sub-phasing within the states.
- Target group: All children in the target age group are vaccinated irrespective of previous immunization status or history of measles/rubella disease.
- MR vaccination is given at fixed and outreach session sites (schools, CHC/PHC/AWC, community halls, village headmen houses, or private doctor clinics).
- Mobile teams are deployed to cover high-risk areas and populations during the campaign.
- Introduction into the routine program by replacing the two doses of measles vaccine currently administered. The first dose is given between 9 and 12 months of age, and the second dose is given at 16-24 months of age.

COMMUNICATION OBJECTIVES AND STRATEGY
- Positive positioning of the MR campaign among stakeholders, communities, and media.
- Facilitate community acceptance of MR vaccination through social mobilization interventions.
- Build an enabling environment through positive media reporting.
- Combat and counter negative responses and myths.
- Components of communication strategy: Pre-campaign building of an enabling environment, social media monitoring and reporting, AEFI crisis communication, advocacy with stakeholders and medical practitioners, social mobilization to create demand, and maximizing reach through mass media and IEC materials.
- Steps for community mobilization: Training of frontline workers (ANM, AWW, ASHA, and community volunteers), name-based enlisting of educational institutions and beneficiaries by ASHA and AWW, preparation of due lists, and making plans to mobilize schools and communities.
- IEC Activities: Auto miking (loudspeakers), posters, banners, hoardings, leaflets to parents and community leaders, and organizing rallies at the community level.

ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
- State Level: High-level political commitment, use of social media, printing and distribution of IEC materials, media sensitization, AEFI crisis communication, and campaign monitoring.
- District Level: CMO has overall accountability and District Immunization Officer (DIO) is the programme officer. Responsible for ensuring intersectoral coordination, monitoring implementation, and reviewing planned activities daily.
- Block Level: BMO has overall accountability. Responsible for building a team, supervising activities, preparing plans for sub-centres, IPC training of ANM, ASHA, and AWW, meeting local leaders, and ensuring IEC materials are displayed.
- Village Level:
  - ANM: Visit all schools for seeking support, organize MR vaccination session sites with the help of ASHA and AWW.
  - ASHA / AWW: Identify children in their area who are not immunized, mobilize the community and children to the session site, and conduct IPC with families of the target age group (9 months to less than 15 years).

MR VACCINE CHARACTERISTICS
- Live attenuated vaccine.
- Contains Measles vaccine (Edmonston strain) and Rubella vaccine (RA 27/3 strain).
- Vaccination results in high (> 95 percent) seroconversion rates, and protection is generally assumed to be lifelong.
- Before reconstitution, it is stable when stored between 2 to 8 degrees Celsius.
- Very sensitive to sunlight, hence it comes in colored glass vials.
- Open vial policy is NOT applicable to reconstituted MR vaccine.
- Must be used within 4 hours after reconstitution. At the session site, the reconstituted vaccine should be kept inside the well of the icepack.
- Induces both humoral and cellular immune responses.

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
- Lyophilized and reconstituted with diluent (provided by the manufacturer) immediately prior to administration.
- Dose: 0.5 ml.
- Administered subcutaneously in the right upper arm.
- Other live and inactivated bacterial and viral vaccines can be administered simultaneously on the same day along with MR vaccine, at different sites.

ADVERSE REACTIONS AND CONTRAINDICATIONS
- Adverse reactions: Slight pain and tenderness at the site of injection, mild fever, febrile seizures, transient rash, thrombocytopenic purpura, arthralgia/joint pain, and anaphylaxis (very rare).
- Contraindications: 
  - High fever (> 102 degrees F / 38-39 degrees C).
  - Serious disease.
  - Pregnancy.
  - Persons with a history of an anaphylactic reaction to neomycin or gelatin.
  - Severely immune-compromised individuals.
  - Individuals receiving immunosuppressive therapeutic radiation.
  - Vaccination should be delayed for 3 to 11 months after the administration of blood or blood products.
  - Following MR vaccination, the administration of blood products should be avoided for 2 weeks.

LESSONS LEARNT AND RECOMMENDATIONS FROM CATCH-UP CAMPAIGNS
- Phasing of campaigns should consider geographical contiguity (advisable to take a state as a whole for the MR campaign).
- Interdepartmental coordination between Health, Education, and Women and Child Development (WCD) is crucial.
- Sensitization of all key stakeholders and partners, including leading pediatricians, private school principals, IMA, and IAP.
- Social mobilization through IPC, developing special strategies for high-risk areas.
- Focus on injection safety, AEFI surveillance, cold chain management, safe injection practices, and waste management.
- Use of standardized AEFI management kits.
- Establishing an alternate pool of vaccinators identified from medical colleges, nursing colleges, and pharmacy colleges trained in advance.
- Ensure quality monitoring and supervision by identifying supportive supervisors and independent external monitors at all levels.

subsections.title: National Health Mission (NHM)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND KEY MILESTONES

The National Health Mission (NHM) aims to increase access to the decentralized health system by establishing new infrastructure in deficient areas and upgrading the infrastructure in existing institutions. The overarching umbrella of NHM integrates various schemes and programmes to holistically address health (covering prevention, promotion, and ambulatory care) at primary, secondary, and tertiary levels.

Key Components of NHM:
- Health system strengthening in rural and urban areas.
- Reproductive, Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health (RMNCH+A).
- Control of communicable and non-communicable diseases.

Key Milestones:
- 1992: Child Survival and Safe Motherhood Programme (CSSM).
- 1997: Reproductive and Child Health (RCH) Phase I.
- 2005: RCH Phase II and launch of National Rural Health Mission (NRHM).
- 2011: Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK).
- 2013: RMNCH+A strategy and launch of National Health Mission (NHM).
- 2014: India Newborn Action Plan (INAP).

TARGETS AND ACHIEVEMENTS OF NHM

Targets:
- Reduce Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) to 25 / 1000 live births.
- Reduce Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR) to 1 / 1000 live births (or 100 / 100,000 live births).
- Reduce Total Fertility Rate (TFR) to 2.1.
- Reduce prevalence of leprosy to < 1 / 10000 population.
- Annual malaria incidence to be < 1 / 1000.

Achievements:
- IMR reduced to 39 / 1000 live births.
- MMR reduced to 1.67 / 1000 live births.
- TFR reduced to 2.3.
- Leprosy prevalence rate achieved at < 1 / 10000.
- Annual malaria incidence achieved at < 1 / 1000.

NATIONAL URBAN HEALTH MISSION (NUHM)

NUHM seeks to improve the health status of the urban population, particularly slum dwellers and other vulnerable sections (homeless, rag pickers, street children, rickshaw pullers, brick / lime kiln workers, sex workers, and migrants) by facilitating their access to quality health care. It focuses on sanitation, clean drinking water, vector control, and strengthening of public health capacity.

Urban Health Care Services and Infrastructure:
- Mahila Arogya Samiti (MAS): Covers 250-500 households. NUHM provides an annual grant of Rs. 5000 to MAS every year.
- ASHA / Link Worker (LW): Covers 1000-2500 population. Ensures participation by creation of MAS and Rogi Kalyan Samitis (RKS).
- ANM: Covers 10,000 population.
- Urban Primary Health Centre (U-PHC): Covers 50,000 population. Located within or near slums. Functional from 12 pm to 8 pm. No IPD services. Services include OPD, Lab, Drugs, RCH services, and Counseling for diseases.
- Urban Community Health Centre (U-CHC): Serves as a satellite hospital for 4-5 U-PHCs. Covers 2,50,000 population (5,00,000 and 100 bedded in metro cities). Has 30-50 beds with IPD services. Services include medical care, minor surgery, and institutional delivery.

Services rendered by ASHA in NUHM:
- Promoter of good health practices.
- Facilitate awareness on RCH services.
- Facilitate access to health-related services at AWC / PHC.
- Accompany pregnant woman and children requiring treatment.
- Community action for prevention of diseases.
- Formation and promotion of MAS.
- Maintain records about births and deaths, immunization, and ANC services.

Indicators in NUHM:
- Community process: Number of MAS formed, Number of ASHA trained.
- Health system: Number of ANM recruited, Number of U-PHCs and U-CHCs made operational, Number of RKS created.
- RCH services: ANCs registered in 1st trimester, TT 2nd dose coverage in pregnant women, Number of children fully immunized.
- Communicable diseases: Number of malaria cases detected, Number of TB cases identified.
- Non-communicable diseases: Number of diabetes, cancer, and hypertension cases screened.

Targets of NUHM:
- 40 percent reduction in Under-5 Mortality Rate (U5MR).
- 50 percent reduction in MMR.
- 100 percent ANC coverage.
- 100 percent institutional delivery.
- TFR 2.1.

NATIONAL RURAL HEALTH MISSION (NRHM)

The aim of NRHM is to provide accessible, affordable, accountable, effective, and reliable primary health care, and bridge the gap in rural health care by creating a cadre of Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHA). It brings the Indian system of medicine (AYUSH) to the mainstream of health care.

Programmes integrated into NRHM:
- RCH II
- NVBDCP
- NLEP
- RNTCP
- NPCB
- IDDP
- IDSP

Plan of Action and Infrastructure Pyramid:
1. Creation of ASHA: Must be a female resident of the village, preferably married/widow/divorced, aged 25-45 years, with communication skills and leadership qualities. General norm is one ASHA per 1000 population (relaxed to one per habitation in tribal, hilly, and desert areas).
2. Village Level (1000 Population): ASHA, Anganwadi Worker (AWW), Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Committee (VHSNC). Focus on Village Health and Nutrition Days (VHND), drug kits, and referral chains.
3. Gram Panchayat / Sub Health Centre Level (5-6 villages): Strengthening of SCs with additional ANM. Skill up-gradation of educated RMPs. Staffing of 2 ANMs and 1 male MPW. Services include telephone links, MCH/Immunization days, drugs, and MCH clinics.
4. Cluster of GPs / PHC Level (30-40 villages): Strengthening of PHCs with AYUSH practitioner and a second doctor (1 male and 1 female). Staffing includes 3 Staff Nurses and 1 LHV for 4-5 SCs. Services include ambulance/hired vehicle, fixed day MCH/Immunization clinics, telephone, MO i/c, emergencies handled by nurses 24x7, round the clock services, drugs, and TB/Malaria tests.
5. Block Level Hospital (100,000 population / 100 villages): Strengthening of CHCs with posting of anesthetist and promotion of RKS for hospital management. Services include ambulance, telephone, obstetric/surgical medical emergencies 24x7, and round the clock services.
6. District Level: All vertical health and family welfare programmes merged into a common District Health Mission. Provision of Mobile Medical Unit (MMU) for improved outreach services.

Major Initiatives under NRHM:
- Selection of ASHA.
- Rogi Kalyan Samiti (RKS): Patient welfare committee / hospital management society. Members act as trustees to manage the affairs of the hospital and upkeep of facilities.
- United grants to the sub-centres: SCs are better equipped with blood pressure measuring equipments, haemoglobin (Hb) measuring equipment, stethoscope, and weighing machine.
- Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Committee (VHSNC): Important tool for community empowerment and participation at the grass-root level.
- Janani Suraksha Yojna (JSY).
- Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK).
- National Mobile Medical Units (NMMUs): Repositioned with universal colour and design.
- National Ambulance Services: Free ambulance services for patient transport (e.g., Dial 108 and 102).
- Web enabled Mother and Child Tracking System (MCTS): Name-based tracking of pregnant women, infants, and children up to the age of three years to ensure delivery of services like ANC, PNC, and immunization.

Newer Initiatives:
- Home delivery of contraceptives (condoms, oral contraceptive pills, emergency contraceptive pills) by ASHA.
- Conducting District Level Household Survey (DLHS) 4.
- Promotion of menstrual hygiene.
- Allocation of funds for RKS grants.
- Involving ASHA in Home Based Newborn Care.
- Modification of centrally sponsored schemes for the development of AYUSH hospitals and clinics.
- Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK).
- Rashtriya Kishor Swasthya Karyakram (RKSK).
- MCH wings: 30 bedded MCH wings sanctioned in public health facilities with high bed occupancy.
- National Iron+ Initiative: IFA supplementation for children, adolescents, and women of reproductive age group.
- Delivery points (DPs): Objective of providing comprehensive RMNCH+A services at these facilities.
- Universal Health Coverage: Key goal of the 12th 5-year plan.
- Comprehensive Primary Health Care: Strengthening institutional structures, improving access to technologies, drugs, and diagnostics.
- Kilkari: Interactive voice response based mobile service that delivers time-sensitive audio messages about pregnancy and child health directly to mobile phones.
- Anti TB resistance survey to estimate the burden of MDR-TB.
- Kala-azar elimination plan including active search, new drug regimen, and coordinated indoor residual spray.

REPRODUCTIVE AND CHILD HEALTH (RCH) PROGRAMME

RCH - Phase I Major Interventions:
1. Essential obstetric care: Early registration of pregnancy, minimum 3 antenatal checkups, safe delivery at home or institution, and 3 post-natal checkups.
2. Emergency obstetric care: FRUs strengthened through the supply of emergency obstetric kits.
3. 24 hour delivery services at PHCs/CHCs: Additional honorarium to staff to encourage round-the-clock delivery facilities.
4. Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP): Engaging doctors trained in MTP to visit PHCs on fixed dates.
5. Control of RTI and STD: Setting up RTI/STD clinics up to the district level.
6. Immunization: Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) became a part of RCH.
7. Essential newborn care: Resuscitation of newborn with asphyxia, prevention of infection, exclusive breast feeding, and referral of sick newborn.
8. Diarrheal disease control: Low osmolarity oral rehydration solution and addition of zinc.
9. Acute respiratory disease control: Treat pneumonia with cotrimoxazole.
10. Prevention and control of vitamin A deficiency in children: Vitamin A to all children under 5 years of age. 1st dose: 1 lac IU at 9 months. 2nd dose: 2 lac IU after 9 months. Subsequent doses: 2 lac IU at 6-month intervals up to 5 years of age.
11. Prevention and control of anaemia in children:
  - Infants 6 months to 5 years: Iron supplement in liquid formulation with 20 mg of elemental iron and 100 mcg folic acid per day for 100 days in a year.
  - Children 6 to 10 years: 30 mg elemental iron and 250 mcg folic acid for 100 days in a year.
  - Children above 10 years: Adult dose.
12. Training of dais: To train at least one Dai in every village.
13. Empowered Action Group (EAG): Constituted in the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare under the Union Minister to get focused attention for different health programmes in socio-demographically deficient states.
14. District surveys: Rapid household survey to assess the reproductive health status of women. Key indicators: Percentage of pregnant women with full ANC, Percentage of institutional deliveries and home deliveries, Percentage of home deliveries by trained birth attendant, Current contraceptive prevalence rate, and Percentage of children fully immunized.

RCH - Phase II Strategies:
1. Essential obstetric care:
  - Institutional delivery: Fifty percent of the PHCs and all the CHCs would be made operational as 24-hour delivery centres.
  - Skilled attendance at delivery: Guidelines for normal delivery and management of obstetric care complications.
  - Policy decision: ANMs/LHVs/SNs permitted to use drugs in specific emergency situations to reduce maternal mortality.
2. Emergency obstetric care: Minimum services provided by FRU include 24-hour delivery services, surgical interventions like caesarian sections, newborn care, emergency care of sick children, family planning services including laparoscopic services, safe abortion services, treatment of STD/RTI, blood storage facility, laboratory services, and referral transport services.
3. Strengthening referral system: Involve local self-help groups, NGOs, and women groups.
4. Safe abortion services:
  - Medical method of abortion: Mifepristone (RU 486) followed by Misoprostol. Recommended up to 7 weeks (49 days).
  - Manual vacuum aspiration (MVA): Feasible to be used in primary health centres.
5. Village health and nutrition day: Once a month at Anganwadi centre to provide antenatal/postpartum care, promote institutional delivery, health education, immunization, family planning, and nutrition services.
6. Maternal death review: To improve quality of obstetric care and reduce maternal mortality and morbidity.
7. Pregnancy tracking: Screening of pregnant women and providing essential and emergency obstetric care.

Vandemataram Scheme:
- Any obstetric and gynaec specialist, maternity home, nursing home, lady doctor, or MBBS doctor can volunteer.
- Enrolled doctors display the Vandemataram logo at their clinic.
- Iron and folic acid tablets, oral pills, TT injections, etc., are provided by the medical officer for free distribution to beneficiaries.

JANANI SURAKSHA YOJANA (JSY)

- Launched on 12th April 2005, modifying the National Maternity Benefit Scheme.
- Objectives: Reducing maternal mortality and infant mortality through encouraging delivery at health institutions.
- Salient features: 100 percent centrally sponsored scheme. Integrates the benefit of cash assistance with institutional care during antenatal, delivery, and immediate post-partum care.
- Yojna subsidizes the cost of caesarean section and management of obstetric complications up to Rs 1500 per delivery.
- ASHA receives her incentive after her post-natal visit to the beneficiary and when the child has been immunized for BCG.

Cash Assistance Structure:
1. Rural Area:
  - Low Performing States (LPS): Mother's package Rs 1400, ASHA's package Rs 600. Total = Rs 2000.
  - High Performing States (HPS): Mother's package Rs 700, ASHA's package Rs 600. Total = Rs 1300.
2. Urban Area:
  - Low Performing States (LPS): Mother's package Rs 1000, ASHA's package Rs 400. Total = Rs 1400.
  - High Performing States (HPS): Mother's package Rs 600, ASHA's package Rs 400. Total = Rs 1000.

Eligibility for Cash Assistance:
- LPS: All women delivering in government health centres.
- HPS: Below poverty line women and women from SC and ST categories.
- Limitation of cash assistance: In LPS, it applies to all births. In HPS, it is limited up to 2 live births.

JANANI SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (JSSK)

- Launched on 1st June 2011.
- Entitles all pregnant women delivering in public health institutions to absolutely free and no-expense delivery, including caesarian section.
- Facilities provided for pregnant women:
  - Free delivery and caesarian section.
  - Free drugs and consumables.
  - Free diet up to 3 days during normal delivery and 7 days in caesarian section.
  - Free diagnostics and free blood.
  - Free transport from home to institution, between facilities in case of referral, and drop back home.
- Similar facilities are provided to all sick newborns for treatment till 30 days after birth.

CHILD HEALTH COMPONENTS

1. Nutritional Rehabilitation Centres (NRCs):
- Facility-based units providing medical and nutritional care to Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) children under 5 years of age with medical conditions.
- Services provided: 24-hour care and monitoring, treatment of medical complications, therapeutic feeding, sensory stimulation and emotional care, counseling on appropriate feed and hygiene, demonstration on preparation of energy-dense food, social assessment of the family, and follow up of discharged children.

2. Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness (IMNCI):
- Indian version of IMCI developed by WHO and UNICEF. Encompasses a range of interventions to prevent and manage the commonest major childhood diseases, including early neonates of 0-7 days of age. Currently implemented in 433 districts.
- Core strategy: Integrated case management of most common childhood problems i.e., diarrhoea, ARI, malaria, measles, and malnutrition.
- Objectives: To reduce death, illness, and disability; to improve growth and development.
- Components: Improvement of case management skills of health staff, improvements in health system, and improvements in family and community practices.
- Integrated Case Management Process (OPD):
  1. Check for danger signs (Convulsions, Unconsciousness, Inability to breastfeed).
  2. Assess main symptoms (Difficulty in breathing, Diarrhoea, fever).
  3. Assess nutrition and immunization status.
  4. Classify condition using colour-coded treatment:
    - Pink: Urgent referral, Emergency treatment in IPD.
    - Yellow: Treatment at OPD, Give oral drugs.
    - Green: Home management, Treat local infection at home.

3. Facility Based Newborn Care:
- Primary Health Centre / Sub-Centre (PHC/SC): Newborn Care Corner (NBCC).
- Community Health Centre (CHC): NBCC and OT leading to Newborn Stabilization Unit (NBSU).
- District Hospital (DH): NBCC and OT leading to Special Newborn Care Unit (SNCU).
- Newborn Care Corner (NBCC): Space within a delivery room where immediate care is provided to all newborns. Mandatory for all health facilities where deliveries are conducted.
- Newborn Stabilization Unit (NBSU): Facility within the maternity ward where sick and low birth weight newborns can be cared for. All FRUs/CHCs should have NBSU. Requires a space of a 4-bedded unit, with 2 beds in the post-natal ward for rooming-in.
- Special Newborn Care Unit (SNCU): Neonatal unit in the vicinity of the labor room to provide special care for sick newborns. Any facility with > 3000 deliveries/year should have an SNCU. Minimum recommended number of beds is 12.

4. Triage for Sick Newborn:
- Emergency Signs (Require immediate action - Assess & Counsel): Hypothermia, Severe Respiratory Distress, Shock, Coma/Convulsions.
- Priority Signs (Assess & Act Rapidly): Cold Stress, Respiratory Distress, Severe Jaundice, Bleeding, Major Congenital Malformation.
- Non Urgent Signs (Assess & Counsel): Minor Birth Trauma, Minor Malformation, Jaundice.

5. Home Based Newborn Care (HBNC):
- Aimed at improving newborn survival. ASHA is the main person involved.
- Objective: Decrease neonatal mortality and morbidity through prevention of complication, early detection of LBW and preterm, and early identification of illness in newborn.
- Responsibilities of ASHA: Mobilize all pregnant mothers to ensure ANC, undertake birth planning and preparedness, assess if the baby is at risk, detect signs and symptoms of sepsis, and provide newborn care through home visits.
- Visit Schedule:
  - Institutional delivery (6 visits): Days 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, and 42.
  - Home delivery (7 visits): Days 1, 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, and 42.
- Incentives to ASHA: Rs 250 for completing visits, plus Rs 50 for monthly follow-up of LBW babies and newborns from SNCU. Twins carry an incentive of Rs 500. Incentives are paid on the 45th day of delivery.

6. Navjat Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (NSSK):
- Launched for the prevention of hypothermia and infection.
- Aim: To train health personnel in basic newborn care and resuscitation.
- Objective: To have a trained health person in basic newborn care and resuscitation unit at every delivery point.

7. Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK):
- Launched in 2013. Provision for child health screening and early intervention services through early detection and management of 4Ds. Covers 30 identified health conditions.
- 4Ds include:
  1. Defects at birth: Neural tube defects, Cleft lip/palate, Club foot.
  2. Deficiencies: Anaemia, Vitamin A deficiency, Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM).
  3. Disease of childhood: Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD), Reactive airway disease, Dental caries.
  4. Developmental delays: Motor delay, Cognitive delay, Language delay.
- Programme Implementation:
  - For newborns: Facility-based screening at public health facilities (by ANM, MO, Gynecologist). Community-based screening through ASHA during home visits (priority to LBW/chronic illness).
  - For children 6 weeks to 6 years: Anganwadi centre-based screening conducted twice a year by dedicated Mobile Health Teams.
  - For children 6 years to 18 years: Government and Government-aided school-based screening conducted once a year by dedicated Mobile Health Teams (at least 2 teams per block).

RMNCH+A STRATEGY

Launched by the Government of India in February 2013 as "A strategic approach to reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health (RMNCH+A) in India".
- Strategy: Linking maternal and child health to reproductive health, family planning, adolescent health, HIV, gender, preconception, and prenatal diagnostic techniques. Linking home and community-based services to facility-based care. Ensuring linkages and referrals among health facilities at primary (PHC), secondary (CHC), and tertiary levels (DH).
- Aim: To reach the maximum number of people in the remotest corners of the country through a continuum of services, constant innovation, and routine monitoring of interventions.
- Management Tools: 5x5 matrix identifying 5 high impact interventions across each of the 5 thematic areas, 5 cross-cutting, and health system strengthening interventions.

5 Thematic Interventions:
1. Reproductive health: PPIUCD, IUCD at all facilities, Home Delivery of Contraceptives (HDC) and Ensuring Birth Spacing (EBS), Pregnancy test kits (PTK Nishchay kits), strengthening abortion services, and sterilization services.
2. Maternal health: Mother and Child Tracking System (MCTS), detect high-risk pregnancy, EmOC services, review of maternal, infant and child deaths, distribute Misoprostol to selected women in the 8th month of pregnancy for consumption during the 3rd stage of labour.
3. Newborn health: Early initiation and exclusive breastfeeding, Home based newborn care, Essential newborn care, Special newborn care services, and community-level use of Gentamycin.
4. Child health: Complimentary feeding and IFA supplementation, Diarrhoea management using ORS and zinc, Management of pneumonia, Full immunization coverage, and RBSK.
5. Adolescent health: Increase contraceptive prevalence, community-based services through peer educators, strengthen ARSH clinics, National Iron Plus initiatives, and promote menstrual hygiene.

Adolescent Health Programmes:
- Adolescent Reproductive and Sexual Health Programme (ARSH): Includes Adolescent Friendly Health Clinics (AFHC) providing routine checkups, contraceptives, menstrual problem management, and RTI/STI management. Also includes facility-based counseling and outreach activities in schools, teen clubs, and VHNDs.
- Weekly Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation Programme (WIFS): Weekly supplements of 100 mg elemental iron and 500 ug folic acid using a fixed-day approach, screening of target groups, biannual de-worming (albendazole 400mg) six months apart, and counseling for improving dietary intake.
- Menstrual Hygiene Scheme: Community-based health education, training of ASHA and nodal teachers, ensuring regular availability and distribution of sanitary napkins, and safe disposal mechanisms.

Delivery Points (DPs):
- Objective is providing comprehensive RMNCH+A services at these facilities.
- L1: Minimum 3 normal deliveries per month.
- L2: Minimum 10 deliveries per month, including management of complications.
- L3: Minimum 20 to 50 deliveries per month, including C-section.

Maternal and Child Health (MCH) Wing:
- Established in high case load facilities with adequate provisions of beds (30, 50, or 100 bedded units).
- Includes antenatal waiting rooms, labour wing, essential newborn care room, SNCU, OT, blood storage units, post-natal ward, and an academic wing.
- Ensures emergency maternal and newborn care as well as a 48-hour stay.

INDIA NEWBORN ACTION PLAN (INAP)

Developed in response to the Global Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP), INAP was launched in June 2014.
- Goal: To attain "Single Digit Neonatal Mortality Rate by 2030" and "Single Digit Stillbirth Rate by 2030".
- The plan is built on six pillars of intervention packages:
  1. Pre-conception and antenatal care.
  2. Care during labour and childbirth.
  3. Immediate newborn care.
  4. Care of healthy newborn.
  5. Care of small and sick newborn.
  6. Care beyond newborn survival.

subsections.title: National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND BACKGROUND

India is experiencing a rapid health transition with a rising burden of chronic non-communicable diseases (NCDs), especially cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, cancer, stroke, and chronic lung diseases. In 2016, NCDs accounted for 60 percent of deaths. Considering this burden, the National Programme on Prevention and Control of Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke was integrated with the National Cancer Control Programme to form NPCDCS in 2010.

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- Prevent and control common NCDs through behaviour and lifestyle changes.
- Provide early diagnosis and management of common NCDs.
- Build capacity at various levels of health care for prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of common NCDs.
- Train human resources within the public health setup, including doctors, paramedics, and nursing staff, to cope with the increasing burden of NCDs.
- Establish and develop capacity for palliative and rehabilitative care.

PROGRAMME COMPONENTS AND STRATEGIES

The programme comprises two major components:
A. Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease and Stroke (DCS) Component.
B. Cancer Component.

The key strategies include:
- Health education and promotion for healthy lifestyles.
- Opportunistic screening of persons above the age of 30 years.
- Establishment of Non-Communicable Disease (NCD) clinics at Community Health Centres (CHC) and District levels.

A. DIABETES, CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE AND STROKE (DCS) COMPONENT

1. Activities at Sub-Centre
- Health promotion for behaviour and lifestyle change through organizing camps, interpersonal communications, posters, banners, etc.
- Opportunistic screening of the population above 30 years of age using BP measurement and blood glucose by strip method.
- Suspected cases of diabetes and hypertension are referred to CHCs of higher health facilities for further diagnosis and management.
- Provision of glucometer optium xceed, optium test strips, and auto disabled lancets procured at the central level.
- Regular home visits by ANM/Male Health Workers for domiciliary visits to elderly and disabled persons, providing support and rehabilitative equipment.

2. Activities at Community Health Centre (CHC)
- Establishment of NCD Clinic at CHC.
- Diagnosis and required investigations/tests like blood sugar measurement, lipid profile, ultrasound, X-ray, and ECG.
- Management and stabilization of common CVD, diabetes, and stroke cases (out-patient as well as in-patients).
- Health workers supervise the work and attend monthly clinics held in villages on a random basis.
- Complicated cases of diabetes, high blood pressure, etc., are referred to the district hospital.

3. Activities at District Hospital
- Establishment of NCD Clinic at the District Hospital.
- Detailed investigation and screening of persons above the age of 30 years to identify individuals who are at high-risk of developing diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular diseases.
- Management and annual assessment of persons suffering from cancer, diabetes, and hypertension.
- Provision of home-based palliative care for chronic, debilitating, and progressive patients.
- Involvement in the promotion of healthy lifestyle through health education and counselling.

URBAN HEALTH CHECK-UP SCHEME FOR DIABETES AND HIGH BLOOD PRESSURE

- Objectives: To screen the urban slum population for diabetes and high blood pressure, and to sensitize the urban slum population about a healthy lifestyle.
- The blood sugar and blood pressure are checked for all individuals aged 30 years and above, and all pregnant women of all ages.
- Creates a database for the prevalence of diabetes and high blood pressure in urban slums.

GUIDELINES FOR REFERRAL AND TREATMENT

- Individuals with a systolic blood pressure > 140 mm of Hg and diastolic blood pressure > 90 mm of Hg, or random blood sugar >= 140 mg/dl, are referred to a medical officer at the nearest facility for confirmation, laboratory investigations, and treatment initiation.
- Those found positive for cancer or precancerous lesions are referred to the PHC / CHC / District Hospital for confirmation and treatment by trained specialists.
- Once the diagnosis of hypertension or diabetes is established, the patient must receive at least a month's supply of drugs from the PHC. A three-month supply can also be provided to the patient each month with the ANM/ASHA to ensure compliance.
- Individuals who are already on treatment under the care of a private practitioner could be offered the choice of taking drugs from the public health system.
- Follow up of individuals by ASHA making visits to enable behavioral modification, treatment compliance, and encouraging regular checkups of BP and blood glucose.

B. CANCER COMPONENT UNDER NPCDCS

Cancer Objectives:
1. Primary prevention: Health education.
2. Secondary prevention: Early diagnosis and screening (cervix, mouth, breast, and tobacco-related cancers).
3. Tertiary prevention: Palliative care and strengthening of existing institutions for comprehensive therapy.

Cancer Schemes and Services:
1. Regional Cancer Centre Scheme: Existing regional cancer centres are strengthened to act as referral centres for complicated cases at the tertiary level.
2. Oncology Wing Development Department: Initiated to fill up geographic gaps in the availability of cancer treatment facilities. Central assistance is provided for the purchase of equipment, including a cobalt unit.
3. Decentralized NGO Scheme: NGOs are given financial assistance for undertaking health education and early detection activities of cancer.
4. IEC Activities: Giving wider publicity about anti-tobacco legislation and ill effects of tobacco. November 7th is observed as National Cancer Awareness Day.
5. Research and Training: Manuals developed for health professionals, cytology, palliative care, and tobacco cessation.

Cancer Services Provided:
- Diagnostic services.
- Surgery.
- Chemotherapy (Chemotherapy drugs are provided for 100 patients at each district hospital; Day care chemotherapy facilities established at 100 district hospitals).
- Palliative care (Home based palliative care is being provided for chronic, debilitating, and progressive cancer patients at 100 districts).

TOBACCO CONTROL LEGISLATION AND PROGRAMME

Cigarette and Other Tobacco Products Act (COTPA) 2003:
- Prohibition of smoking in public places.
- Prohibition of direct and indirect advertisement of cigarettes and other tobacco products.
- Prohibition of sale of cigarettes and tobacco products to a person below the age of 18 years.
- Prohibition of sale of tobacco products near educational institutions.
- Statutory warnings (pictorial warnings) on tobacco packs.
- Mandatory depiction of tar and nicotine contents along with maximum permissible limits.

National Tobacco Control Programme Components:
- Mass media campaigns for awareness building and behavioural change.
- Establishment of tobacco product testing laboratories to build regulatory capacity.
- Adult tobacco surveys.
- Dedicated tobacco control cells for effective implementation and monitoring.
- Provision of tobacco cessation facilities.
- School programmes and training of health and social workers, NGOs, and school teachers.

NEW INITIATIVES UNDER NPCDCS

1. Intervention for prevention and control of Rheumatic Heart Disease under NPCDCS.
2. Integration with Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK).
3. Integration of AYUSH with NPCDCS.
4. Integration of RNTCP (NTEP) with NPCDCS for the management of TB and Diabetes comorbidities.

subsections.title: National Mental Health Programme (NMHP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The National Mental Health Programme (NMHP) was launched during 1982 with a view to ensure the availability of mental health care services for all, especially the community at risk and underprivileged sections of the population.

The programme covers 241 districts (as highlighted in specific programme rollouts) and has expanded its reach across the country to encourage the application of mental health knowledge in general healthcare and social development.

AIMS AND OBJECTIVES

Aims:
1. Prevention and treatment of mental and neurological disorders and their associated disabilities.
2. Improve quality of life of the population.
3. Application of mental health technology to improve general health services.

Objectives:
1. To ensure availability and accessibility of minimum mental health care for all in the foreseeable future, particularly to the most vulnerable and underprivileged sections of the population.
2. To encourage application of mental health knowledge in general health care and in social development.
3. To promote community participation in mental health services development, and to stimulate efforts towards self-help in the community.

STRATEGIES

The programme strategies are:
1. Integration of mental health with primary health care through the NMHP.
2. Provision of tertiary care institutions for treatment of mental disorders.
3. Eradicating stigmatization of mentally ill patients and protecting their rights through regulatory institutions like the Central Mental Health Authority and State Mental Health Authority.

DISTRICT MENTAL HEALTH PROGRAMME

Components:
1. Training of all workers in the mental health team at the identified nodal institute in the state.
2. Public education in mental health to increase awareness and to reduce stigma.
3. For early detection and treatment, OPD and indoor services are provided.
4. Providing valuable data and experience at the level of community to the state and centre for future planning and improvement in service and research.

Activities:
The District Mental Health Programme has incorporated promotive and preventive activities for positive mental health which includes:
1. School mental health activities:
  - Life skills education in schools.
  - Counseling services.
2. College counseling services:
  - Provided through trained teachers and counselors.
3. Workplace stress management:
  - Covers formal and informal sectors, including farmers, women, etc.
4. Suicide prevention services:
  - Counseling centre at the district level.
  - Sensitization workshops and IEC.
  - Help lines.

THRUST AREAS

1. District mental health programme in an enlarged and more effective form covering the entire country.
2. Streamlining and modernization of mental hospitals in order to modify their present custodial role.
3. Upgrading departments of psychiatry in medical colleges and enhancing the psychiatric content of the medical curriculum at the undergraduate as well as postgraduate level.
4. Strengthening the central and state mental health authorities with a permanent secretariat. Appointment of medical officers at state headquarters in order to make the monitoring role more effective.
5. Research and training in the field of community mental health, substance abuse, and child adolescent psychiatric clinics.

THE MENTAL HEALTHCARE ACT, 2017

The United Nations convention on the rights of persons with disabilities was ratified by the Government of India, thus making it obligatory to align the policies and laws of the country with the convention.

Key provisions and milestones:
- The Mental Healthcare Bill, 2013 was introduced in Parliament in order to protect, promote and fulfill the rights of persons with mental illness during the delivery of healthcare in institutions and in the community.
- It ensures healthcare, treatment, and rehabilitation of persons with mental illness is provided in the least restrictive environment possible.
- Aims to regulate the public and private mental health sectors within a rights framework, to achieve the greatest public health good, and to promote principles of equity, efficiency, and active participation of all stakeholders in decision making.
- Suicide has been decriminalized under the Act.
- The Bill received the assent of the President of India on 07.04.2017.

subsections.title: Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The Integrated Disease Surveillance Project (IDSP) is a decentralized state-based surveillance system in the country. It was launched in November 2004 as a 5-year project up to March 2010. The project was restructured and extended up to March 2012, and continues in the 12th Five Year Plan with a domestic budget as the Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme under the National Health Mission (NHM) for all states.

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- Intended to detect early warning signals of impending outbreaks and help initiate an effective response in a timely manner in urban and rural areas.
- To provide essential data to monitor the progress of ongoing disease control programmes and help allocate health resources more efficiently.
- The important information in disease surveillance involves identifying who gets the disease, how many get the disease, where they get the disease, why they get the disease, and what needs to be done as a public health response.

ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE AND ADMINISTRATION

- Central Surveillance Unit (CSU): Established and integrated in the National Centre for Disease Control (NCDC), Delhi.
- State Surveillance Unit (SSU): Established at all State / UT headquarters.
- District Surveillance Unit (DSU): Established at all districts in the country.
- An IT network connecting 776 sites in states / district headquarters and premier institutes has been established with the help of the National Information Centre (NIC) and ISRO (Indian Space Research Organization) for data entry, training, video conferencing, and outbreak discussions.

TYPES OF INTEGRATIONS IN IDSP

Different types of integration are proposed and implemented in the project:
1. Health and non-health sectors (Developing effective partnership).
2. Non-communicable and communicable diseases surveillance.
3. Private sector and NGOs.
4. Academic institutions and medical colleges (Bringing them into the primary public health activity of disease surveillance).
5. Sharing of surveillance information of disease control programmes.

COMPONENTS OF SURVEILLANCE

The prerequisite of effective surveillance is the use of standard case definitions, ensuring regularity of reports, and action on reports. The components are:
1. Collection of data
2. Compilation of data
3. Analysis and interpretation of data
4. Follow up action
5. Feedback

CLASSIFICATION OF SURVEILLANCE

Weekly disease surveillance data on epidemic-prone diseases are collected on 'S' (syndromic), 'P' (probable/presumptive), and 'L' (laboratory) formats. More than 90 percent of districts report such weekly data through e-mail/portals.

1. Syndromic diagnosis: Diagnosis is made on the basis of the clinical pattern by paramedical personnel and members of the community (e.g., Sub-centre health worker / ANM).
2. Presumptive (Probable) diagnosis: Diagnosis made on typical history and clinical examination by a Medical Officer (e.g., PHC / CHC MO).
3. Confirmed diagnosis: Clinical diagnosis confirmed by a medical officer and positive laboratory identification.

SYNDROMES LISTED UNDER SURVEILLANCE

The paramedical health staff undertake disease surveillance based on broad categories of presentation. These syndromes are intended to pick up all priority diseases listed under regular surveillance.
- Fever:
  - Less than 7 days duration without any localizing signs (Malaria, Typhoid, JE, Dengue, Measles).
  - With rash (Measles, Dengue).
  - With altered sensorium or convulsions (JE, Meningitis).
  - Bleeding from skin or mucus membrane (Dengue, Haemorrhagic fevers).
  - More than 7 days with or without localizing signs (Typhoid, Malaria).
- Cough: More than 3 weeks duration (Tuberculosis).
- Acute flaccid paralysis: Polio.
- Diarrhoea: Cholera, Acute diarrhoeal disease.
- Jaundice: Hepatitis, Leptospirosis, Dengue, Malaria, Yellow fever.
- Unusual events causing death or hospitalization: Anthrax, Plague, Emerging epidemics.

CONDITIONS UNDER SURVEILLANCE

1. Regular Surveillance:
  - Vector borne diseases: Malaria.
  - Water borne diseases: Acute diarrhoeal disease (Cholera), Typhoid.
  - Respiratory diseases: Tuberculosis.
  - Vaccine preventable diseases: Measles.
  - Diseases under eradication: Polio.
  - Other conditions: Road traffic accidents (Link-up with police computers).
  - International commitments: Plague.
  - Unusual clinical syndromes: Meningoencephalitis / Respiratory distress, Haemorrhagic fevers, other undiagnosed conditions.
2. Sentinel Surveillance:
  - Sexually transmitted diseases / blood borne: HIV / HBV / HCV.
  - Other conditions: Water quality monitoring, Outdoor air quality (Large urban centres).
3. Regular periodic surveys:
  - NCD risk factors: Anthropometry, Physical activity, Blood pressure, Tobacco, Nutrition, etc.
4. Additional state priorities:
  - Each state may identify up to five additional conditions for surveillance.

OUTBREAK AND RAPID RESPONSE

In epidemiology, an outbreak is a sudden increase in the occurrence of a disease in a particular time and place. Whenever there is a rising trend of illness, it is investigated by the Rapid Response Team (RRT) to diagnose and control the outbreak. It is a multi-specialty team of an epidemiologist, a clinician, a microbiologist, and other specialists as per requirement.

Warning signs of an impending outbreak:
- Clustering of cases or deaths in time and/or space.
- Unusual increase in the number of cases or deaths.
- Even a single case of measles, AFP, cholera, plague, dengue, or JE.
- Acute febrile illness of unknown aetiology.
- Occurrence of two or more epidemiologically linked cases of meningitis or measles.
- Unusual isolate.
- Shifting in age distribution of cases.
- Sudden increase or high vector density.
- Natural disasters.

Trigger events for investigation:
- Acute watery stools: A single case of severe dehydration or death in a patient > 5 years of age with diarrhoea, OR more than 10 houses having at least one case of loose stools irrespective of age, per village or urban ward.
- Fever < 7 days duration:
  - Only fever: 5 cases in 1000 population.
  - With rash (Measles / Dengue): 2 similar cases in a village (1000 population).
  - Altered consciousness: 2 cases of fever with altered consciousness in the village / 1000 population.
  - Fever with bleeding: 2 cases of fever with bleeding in a village / 1000 population.
  - Fever with convulsions: 2 cases of fever with convulsions in a village / 1000 population.
- Fever more than 7 days: More than 2 cases in a village / 1000 population.
- Jaundice: More than 2 cases in a village / 1000 population.
- Unusual event: More than 2 deaths or hospitalizations.

REPORTING UNITS IN IDSP

1. Public Health Sector:
  - Rural: CHCs, District hospitals.
  - Urban: Urban hospitals, ESI / Railway / Medical college hospitals.
2. Private Health Sector:
  - Rural: Sentinel private practitioners and Sentinel hospitals.
  - Urban: Sentinel private nursing homes, Sentinel hospitals, Medical colleges, Private and NGO laboratories.

subsections.title: Demography Basics and Demographic Trends
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section covers the basics of demography, the demographic cycle, global and Indian demographic trends, population pyramids, and key concepts like demographic dividend, bonus, and burden.

DEFINITIONS
- Demography: Derived from 'Demos' meaning population and 'Graphy' meaning study. It is the scientific study of human populations, focusing primarily on population size, composition, and distribution in space.
- Demographic transition: The process of transition from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates as part of the economic development of a country from a preindustrial to an industrial economy.
- Epidemiological transition: The change in the pattern of disease in a country, moving away from infectious diseases towards degenerative or non-communicable diseases.
- Carrying capacity: The maximum supportable population of an organism, given the food, habitat, water, and other necessities available within an ecosystem. If a population is below carrying capacity, the population increases; if above carrying capacity, the population decreases.
- Demographic bonus: The period when the dependency ratio declines because of a decline in fertility. It eventually rises again due to increasing longevity (life expectancy over 65 years increases, driving the dependency ratio up).
- Demographic burden: The increase in the total dependency ratio caused by an increased old age population.
- Demographic window: The period of time in a nation's demographic evolution when the proportion of the population of working age group is particularly prominent.
- Demographic dividend: The rise in economic growth due to a rise in working age people in the population.
- Demographic gift: The favorable effect of falling fertility rates on the dependency ratio (fertility drops, birth rate drops, less than 14 years dependency ratio drops). It reaches its maximum in stage 3 of the demographic cycle.

CORE CONCEPTS

DEMOGRAPHIC PROCESSES AND TYPES
- There are five continuous demographic processes that determine population size, composition, and distribution:
  - Marriage
  - Migration
  - Social mobility (change in socioeconomic status of individual or family over a period of time)
  - Fertility
  - Mortality
- Types of demography:
  - Formal demography: Deals with the measurement of population processes.
  - Social demography: Analyzes the relationship between economic, social, cultural, and biological processes influencing a population.
- Sources of demographic statistics include the Census, National Sample Surveys, registration of vital events, and demographic studies.

DEMOGRAPHIC CYCLE (5 STAGES)
- The history of world population suggests a cycle of 5 stages through which a nation passes.
- 1. First Stage (High Stationary):
  - Characterized by a high birth rate and a high death rate, which cancel each other out.
  - The population remains stationary.
  - Features a young population and a pyramidal shaped age pyramid.
  - India was in this stage until 1920 (the year 1921 is known as the big divide).
- 2. Second Stage (Early Expanding):
  - The death rate begins to decline, but the birth rate is unchanged (remains high).
  - The population is increasing or expanding.
  - Features a young population and a losing pyramidal shaped age pyramid.
  - Many countries in Africa and South Asia are in this phase.
  - Demographic trap is seen in this stage (population is growing rapidly due to high birth rate and low death rate).
- 3. Third Stage (Late Expanding):
  - The birth rate starts to decrease, but the death rate declines further such that the number of births is still higher than total deaths.
  - The population is still increasing or expanding.
  - Features a young population and a globular shaped age pyramid.
  - Examples include India, China, and Singapore (other developing countries).
- 4. Fourth Stage (Low Stationary):
  - Characterized by a low birth rate and a low death rate.
  - Zero population growth is seen.
  - Features a mixed population and a cylindrical shaped age pyramid.
  - Examples include Austria (in 1985), Belgium, Sweden, UK, Denmark, and Canada.
- 5. Fifth Stage (Declining):
  - The birth rate declines further, such that the number of births are now less than total deaths.
  - The population begins to decline.
  - Features an aged population and a losing cylindrical shaped age pyramid.
  - Examples include Germany, Italy, Spain, Portugal, Greece, Japan, Hungary, Bulgaria, and Eastern European countries.

DEMOGRAPHIC GAP
- Defined as the difference between the birth rate and the death rate (Birth rate - Death rate).
- Attains maximum limit in late Stage 2.
- Contraction of the gap starts in early Stage 3.
- Minimum or reversed in Stage 5 (late part).

DEMOGRAPHIC INDICATORS
- Divided into population statistics and vital statistics.
- Population statistics include indicators that measure population size, sex ratio, dependency ratio, density, etc.
- Vital statistics include indicators such as birth rate, death rate, natural growth rate, etc.

POPULATION TRENDS IN INDIA AND THE WORLD
- The world population reached 8 billion on 15th November 2022. It took 12 years for the population to grow from 7 to 8 billion.
- Ten most populated countries (mid-2024 estimates): India (1451 million), China (1419 million), USA (345 million), Indonesia (283 million), Pakistan (251 million), Nigeria (233 million), Brazil (212 million), Bangladesh (174 million), Russia (145 million), Ethiopia (132 million).
- India's population crossed the 1 billion mark on May 11, 2000.
- The year 1921 is called the big divide because the absolute number of people added to the population during each decade has been on the increase since 1921.
- The most populous states in India are Uttar Pradesh (approx 16.7 percent of India's population), Maharashtra, and Bihar. These three alone account for nearly 33.5 percent of the total population.
- Total Fertility Rate (TFR) categorization by Government of India:
  - High-focus states (TFR more than 2.1): Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Meghalaya, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Assam.
  - Non-high focus states (TFR less than or equal to 2.1): Southern states like Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka.

AGE AND SEX COMPOSITION
- Population composition of India:
  - 0 to 14 years: 27 percent. Features more male population. The proportion of children under 14 is declining as the birth rate is declining.
  - 15 to 49 years: 56 percent. Women of reproductive age group (15 to 49 years) constitute 22 to 25 percent of the total population.
  - 50 to 59 years: 8 percent.
  - 60 years and above: 8.5 percent. Features more female population. The proportion of elderly is increasing as life expectancy increases.
- Adolescent age is defined by WHO as 10 to 19 years.

POPULATION PYRAMID
- Also known as the Age and Sex pyramid. It represents the age and sex structure of a population.
- Built as a double histogram (2 back to back histograms) with one showing number of males and the other showing females in particular age groups.
- The age pyramid of India has a broad base and is tapering or narrow at the top.
- Types of population pyramid:
  - Stationary pyramid: Shows an unchanging pattern of fertility and mortality.
  - Expansive pyramid: Has a broad base, high proportion of children, and low proportion of older people.
  - Constrictive pyramid: Shows a lower percentage of younger people and a higher percentage of older people (graying population).
- Utility of population pyramid by Shape:
  - Broad base and narrow top or apex (upright triangle): Indicates a high proportion of younger population (typical of developing countries like India).
  - Bulge in the middle (spindle shape): Indicates a high proportion of adults (typical of developed countries).
  - Broad belly and constricting type.
- Utility by Height:
  - Taller pyramid: Indicates higher life expectancy (developed countries).
  - Shorter pyramid: Indicates lower life expectancy (developing countries).
- Utility by Symmetry:
  - Symmetric pyramid: Indicates an ideal sex ratio (developed countries).
  - Asymmetric pyramid: Indicates an unfavorable sex ratio less than 1000 (developing countries).

DENSITY OF POPULATION AND SETTLEMENT TYPES
- Density of population is the ratio between total population and land area.
- As per the 2011 census, India's population density was 382 per square kilometer. For the year 2020, the estimated density is 464 per square kilometer.
- Classification of settlements by population size:
  - Isolated dwelling: Less than 10 families living together in a small area.
  - Small village: Less than 500 to 600 population.
  - Village: 1000 to 2000 population (Hallmark of a village is the Panchayat).
  - Town: 2000 to 20000 population (Hallmark of a town is that it has a municipality as its governing body).
  - City: Greater than 20000 to 1 lakh population (Hallmark of a city is a municipal corporation with different departments like water, electricity, health departments etc).
  - Block: 1 lakh population (Functions as an administrative unit).
  - Mega or Metropolitan city: Greater than 1 crore population.
- Population living below poverty line (BPL) in India was 22 percent in 2013 and 29.5 percent in 2014.

KEY POINTS
- The crude birth rate for the world fell below 30 for the first time around 1975 and is currently around 16 (mid-2024 data).
- Death rates have declined globally. The global death rate declined from 11.0 in 1975-1980 to 8 per 1000 population in mid-2024.
- In 2018, for the first time in history, persons aged 65 years or over outnumbered children under age 5 worldwide.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
- Basic demographic equation: Population = Population + Natural increase + Net migration
- Natural increase = Births - Deaths
- Net migration = Immigration - Emigration
- Migration rate = (Number of migrants / Total population) x 100
- Rule of 70 (Growth Model): Doubling time of population = 70 / Annual growth rate
  - 0.5 percent growth results in doubling of population every 140 years.
  - 1 percent growth results in doubling of population every 70 years.
  - 1.2 percent growth results in doubling of population every 60 years.
  - 1.5 percent growth results in doubling of population every 50 years.
  - 2 percent growth results in doubling of population every 35 years.

MNEMONICS
- Demographic indicators can be remembered as FULL AS DAD:
  - F: Family size
  - U: Urbanization
  - L: Literacy Rate
  - L: Life expectancy
  - A: Age pyramid
  - S: Sex ratio
  - D: Dependency ratio
  - A: Age and sex composition
  - D: Density of population
- Five Demographic Processes can be remembered as Shadi - Vidai - Bachha - Mout:
  - Shadi: Marriage
  - Vidai: Migration
  - Bachha: Fertility
  - Mout: Mortality
  - Social mobility

subsections.title: Demographic Indicators, Surveys, and Registration Systems
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section continues the discussion on key demographic indicators including sex ratio, dependency ratio, family size, urbanization, literacy rate, and life expectancy. It also details the National Family Health Survey (NFHS), Sample Registration System (SRS), and Civil Registration System (CRS).

DEFINITIONS
- Sex ratio: The number of females per thousand males. It is determined by the sex composition of the population.
- Female deficit syndrome: A decrease in the number of female population which leads to ecological imbalance. A low sex ratio indicates strong male child preference, gender inequalities, female foeticide, and higher maternal mortality.
- Societal Dependency Ratio: Another term for the total dependency ratio, which is the proportion of persons above 65 years of age and children below 14 years of age considered to be dependent on the economically productive age group (15 to 64 years).
- Potential support ratio: The number of working-age group persons (15 to 64 years) per 1 older person (greater than or equal to 65 years). It gives the burden placed on the working population and is the inverse of old-age dependency.
- Family size: In common parlance, the total number of persons in a family. In demography, it means the total number of children a woman has borne at a point in time.
- Completed family size: Indicates the total number of children borne by a woman during her child-bearing age (assumed to be 15 to 45 years). Total Fertility Rate (TFR) gives the magnitude of completed family size.
- Centralization: The number of persons living together due to occupational distribution and industrialization.
- Segregation: The number of persons living together due to sociocultural forms.
- Literate (UN Definition): The ability to read and write a simple sentence in any language.
- Literate (Indian Definition): Any person who can read and write with understanding in any one language and who is strictly greater than 7 years of age.
- Functional literacy: The ability of an individual to use reading and writing skills in everyday life situations.
- Transliteracy: The ability to read, write, and interact across various platforms, media tools, and social networks.
- Alliteracy: The state of being able to read but being uninterested in doing so.

CORE CONCEPTS

SEX RATIO INDICATORS AND DATA
- Since 1901, the sex ratio in India has been generally unfavorable for women.
- Interpretation of sex ratio:
  - Ideal or Evolutionary stable sex ratio is 1000 (equal number of males and females).
  - Favorable sex ratio is greater than 1000 (females outnumber males).
  - Unfavorable sex ratio is less than 1000 (females are less than males).
- Indian sex ratio statistics (NFHS-4 data):
  - India average: 991 females per 1000 males.
  - Rural average: 947.
  - Urban average: 926.
  - State-wise: Highest in Kerala (1084) and Lowest in Haryana (877).
  - Union Territory-wise: Highest in Pondicherry (1038) and Lowest in Daman and Diu (618).
- Child sex ratio (0 to 6 years):
  - Measured as the number of females (0 to 6 years) per thousand males (0 to 6 years of age).
  - India average: 914.
  - Highest in Mizoram and Lowest in Haryana.
- Natural sex ratio at birth:
  - Estimated ideal is 950.
  - India average is 919.

DEPENDENCY RATIO DETAILS
- The total dependency population in India is 50.55 percent (41.1 percent is young dependency population aged 0 to 14 years, and 9.4 percent is old dependency population aged 65 years and above).
- India's dependency ratio was 53 in 2017. This means 100 earning people are supporting 153 people (100 supporting themselves plus 53 non-earning dependents).
- Subdivided into two categories:
  - Young age dependency ratio.
  - Old age dependency ratio.

FAMILY SIZE AND URBANIZATION
- The target for the family planning campaign is based on a 2 Child Norm, to attain the goal of Net Reproductive Rate (NRR) equal to 1, and for that Couple Protection Rate (CPR) should be greater than 60 percent.
- Urbanization statistics in India: Urban population is 31.8 percent and Rural population is 68.84 percent.
- Criteria to classify an area as Urban:
  - Population greater than 5000.
  - Population density greater than 390 per square kilometer.
  - Greater than 75 percent of the male working population engaged in non-agricultural occupation.

LITERACY RATE
- The threshold level of literacy is considered to be 75 percent.
- Global literacy rate is 86 percent. Highest is North Korea (100 percent) and Lowest is Chad or Niger (22 percent).
- India literacy rate is 74.04 percent. Male literacy is 82.14 percent and Female literacy is 65.46 percent.
- State-wise literacy in India:
  - Best: Kerala (94 percent) or may be Tripura (94.6 percent).
  - Worst: Bihar (64 percent).
- Indian government schemes and initiatives for literacy:
  - Government of India has made education compulsory up to the age of 14 years in the country.
  - Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (launched in 2001) ensures all children in the age group of 6 to 14 years attend school and complete 8 years of schooling.
  - District primary education programme.
  - Mid-day meal programme.
  - National literacy mission.
  - International Literacy Day is observed on 8th September every year.

LIFE EXPECTANCY
- In India, life expectancy is 67.5 years. The target is to reach 70 years by 2025.
- Japan has the highest life expectancy in the world.

NATIONAL FAMILY HEALTH SURVEY 4 (NFHS-4)
- It is a large-scale multi-round survey conducted in a representative sample of households throughout India.
- Till date, multiple rounds have been conducted. Key dates: NFHS-1 (1992-93), NFHS-2 (1998-99), NFHS-3 (2005-06), NFHS-4 (2015-16).
- Goals:
  - To provide data needed by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
  - To provide information on emerging health and family welfare issues.
- Objectives: To provide information on family planning, Maternal and Child Health (MCH), Reproductive and Child Health (RCH), quality, and utilization of health and family planning services.
- Nodal agency: International Institute for Population Science (IIPS), Mumbai.
- Key findings from NFHS-4 (2015-16):
  - Total Fertility Rate (TFR) is 2.2.
  - Mothers receiving full Antenatal Care (ANC) is 21 percent.
  - At least 4 ANC visits is 51 percent.
  - Institutional deliveries is 79 percent.
  - Exclusive breastfeeding is 55 percent.
  - Fully immunized child is 62 percent.
  - Low weight for age children (under 5 years) indicating undernutrition is 36 percent.
  - Low weight for height children (under 5 years) indicating wasted is 21 percent.
  - Low height for age children (under 5 years) indicating stunted is 38 percent.

DISTRICT LEVEL HOUSEHOLD SURVEY (DLHS)
- Also known as the Adhoc Survey, because it is not done at frequent intervals.
- It includes social parameters affecting health.

NATIONAL POPULATION STABILIZATION FUND
- Aimed at achieving population stabilization by the year 2045.
- Kerala is used as the yardstick of health in India because of its high literacy rate, older age of marriage, and higher life expectancy.

SAMPLE REGISTRATION SYSTEM (SRS)
- Started in 1960. It provides a system of collection of data to give national and subnational estimates of vital indicators. It consists of continuous enumeration backed by a 6 months survey.
- Uses a Dual record system:
  - Field investigation: continuous enumeration of births and deaths by an enumerator.
  - Independent retrospective survey: conducted every 6 months by an investigator-supervisor.
- Done every 6 months, hence also known as the Biannual Registration System or Dual Registration System. It is done at state and national level for triangulation and matching of events.
- It now covers the entire country and provides annual national and state-level estimates of fertility and mortality.
- Advantage: Eliminates duplication and is a self-evaluating technique.
- Objectives:
  - Primary objective: Statistics on the most probable cause of death using lay diagnosis reporting (post-death verbal autopsy method).
  - Main objective: Estimation of birth rate, death rate, Infant Mortality Rate (IMR), and annual growth rate (estimated at the division level in rural areas and at the state level in urban areas).
- Components of SRS:
  - To obtain the usual resident population of sample areas.
  - Enumerate vital events: Recording births and deaths, updating house lists, listing women in the reproductive age group along with their pregnancy status (useful for estimation of fertility).
  - Matching of events during enumeration and half-yearly survey.
  - Field verification of unmatched and partially matched events.
  - Filling of Verbal Autopsy Forms.
- Findings of Sample Registration System 2018:
  - Crude Birth Rate (CBR): 20.4
  - Crude Death Rate (CDR): 6.4
  - Infant Mortality Rate (IMR): 34
  - Growth rate: 1.4 percent (14 per 1000).
- Regular reporting of health statistics is done to enumerate trends of disease, appreciate health personnel efforts, and for epidemiological research.

CIVIL REGISTRATION SYSTEM (CRS)
- The birth and death registration system is technically known as the Civil Registration System. It is also known as the Vital Registration System.
- Registration of births and deaths is compulsory under the Birth and Death Registration Act 1969.
- Functions under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
- Birth must be registered within 21 days.
- Deaths must be registered within 21 days.
- Specific conditions for registration:
  - Non-Resident Indians (NRI) must register within 90 days of birth or death.
  - If a child is born to an Indian couple outside India, the birth must be registered within 60 days of arrival in India.
- Delayed registration rules:
  - After 21 days till 30 days: Late fee applies.
  - After 30 days till 1 year: Late fee plus written permission from the District Registrar is required.
  - After 1 year: Late fee plus an order of the Executive Magistrate is required.
- Registration coverage in India:
  - Births registration is 89 percent.
  - Death registration is 74 percent.
- Registration of the name of the child:
  - Within 12 months of birth: Free of charge.
  - After 12 months of birth up to 15 years: Rs. 5 fee.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
- Total dependency ratio =[(Population of 0 to 14 years + Population of 65 years and above) / Population of 15 to 64 years] x 100
- Young age dependency ratio = (Population of 0 to 14 years / Population of 15 to 64 years) x 100
- Old age dependency ratio = (Population of 65 years and above / Population of 15 to 64 years) x 100
- Effective literacy rate = (Number of literate persons aged 7 and above / Population aged 7 and above in a given year) x 100
- Crude literacy rate = (Number of literate persons / Total population in a given year) x 100

KEY POINTS
- The 2-child norm aims to attain a Total Fertility Rate of 2.1, achieving a Net Reproductive Rate of 1, supported by a Couple Protection Rate of over 60 percent.
- Kerala is the benchmark state for health metrics in India due to high female literacy, delayed age of marriage, and high life expectancy.
- The Sample Registration System serves as the primary source for estimating the Infant Mortality Rate and relies heavily on verbal autopsy to determine the cause of death.

subsections.title: Family Planning, National Population Policy, and Fertility Indicators
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section deals with the fundamentals of family planning, the concept of eligible and target couples, the National Population Policy, and an exhaustive list of fertility-related statistics and indicators that measure population dynamics.

DEFINITIONS
- Family planning: The way of thinking and living that is adopted voluntarily, upon the basis of knowledge, attitudes, and responsible decisions by individuals and couples, in order to promote the health and welfare of the family group and thus contribute effectively to the social development of a country. It involves couples planning when to have children using birth control and other techniques.
- Birth control: Regimen, devices, or medications followed in order to deliberately prevent or reduce the likelihood of pregnancy or childbirth.
- Contraception: Mechanisms which are intended to reduce the likelihood of the fertilization of an ovum by a spermatozoon.
- Eligible Couple: An eligible couple refers to a currently married couple wherein the wife is in the reproductive age, which is generally assumed to lie between the ages of 15 and 45 (or 49) years. In India, they constitute 15 to 18 percent of the population (i.e., 150 to 180 eligible couples per 1000 population). About 20 percent of eligible couples are in the age group of 15 to 24 years. An "Eligible Couple Register" is maintained at the Subcentre level. Contraceptive methods are preferred in eligible couples.
- Target Couple: A couple in which the female is in the reproductive age group (15 to 49 years) and has achieved the desired family size or has at least 2 to 3 living children. Sterilization method is preferred in target couples. The term 'eligible couple' is now more widely used over 'target couple' as the target-free approach has replaced the target-oriented approach in the RCH programme.
