- 2 years: Horizontal line; 6 cubes tower; Undressing.
- 3 years: Circle; 9 cubes tower; Dressing and undressing (but needs help for buttoning and unbuttoning).
- 4 years: Plus (+) sign.
- 5 years: Triangle; Dress and undress completely (without any help) and tie shoelaces.
- 6 years: Diamond.

LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT
- 3 months: Cooing.
- 6 months: Monosyllables (ma, pa).
- 9 months: Bisyllables (mama, papa).
- 1 year: 1 to 2 words.
- 15 months: Jargon speech (so many words without any understandable meaning).
- 18 months: 8 to 10 words.
- 2 years: 2-word sentence.
- 3 years: Age, Name, Gender.
- 4 years: Tell story or poem.
- 5 years: Ask meaning of words.

SOCIAL DEVELOPMENT
- 2 months: Social smile (smile at any human face).
- 6 months: Stranger anxiety; Smile at mirror image.
- 9 months: Bye-Bye wave.
- 12 months: Play a simple ball game; Comes when called.
- 18 months: Domestic mimicry.

subsections.title: Growth Charts, Low Birth Weight, Prematurity, and Kangaroo Mother Care
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
Monitoring physical growth is a fundamental aspect of child health care. The growth chart is a vital tool used to visualize a child's progress. Low Birth Weight (LBW) is a major public health problem in India, encompassing both prematurity and intrauterine growth retardation, and demands specialized interventions like Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC).

GROWTH CHART
- Used for monitoring Growth and Development of the child.
- Designed by David Morley and later modified by WHO.
- Also known as the Road to health chart.
- It is a Weight for Age chart. Height of the child is not taken because with under-nutrition, a child can lose weight but not height.
- 49 types of growth charts are used in India.
- Used under National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) and Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS).
- Present in the Mother and Child Protection card (MCP Card).
- In the MCP card, 2 separate growth charts are present for boys (blue) and girls (pink).

INFORMATION PROVIDED BY GROWTH CHART
- Date of birth (age).
- Birth weight and Weight for age.
- Developmental milestones.
- Immunization status and Immunization procedures.
- Introduction of supplementary food.
- Antenatal Care (ANC) and History of sibling health.
- Child spacing (contraceptive method used).

TYPES OF GROWTH CHARTS

1. ICDS growth chart
- Separate for both sexes.
- Based on WHO Multicentre Growth Reference Study (MGRS) child growth standards.
- Used for growth monitoring at Anganwadi centres.
- Contains 3 reference curves:
  - Reference standard.
  - 2 SD below reference standard.
  - 3 SD below reference standard.
- The growth chart shows specific zones:
  - Normal zone (green).
  - Under nutrition (yellow): Between -2SD to -3SD.
  - Severely under nutrition (red): Below -3SD.
- Objective is to keep the child in the normal zone.
- Flattening of the curve signals growth failure, which is the earliest sign of Protein Energy Malnutrition (PEM).

2. WHO growth chart
- Same for both sexes.
- Contains 2 reference curves:
  - Upper reference curve (URC): 50th percentile for boys.
  - Lower reference curve (LRC): 3rd percentile for girls (2SD below median weight for age).
- Road to health or Zone of normality is the space between the 2 curves.
- Based on National Centre for Health Statistics (NCHS) Standards USA.

USES OF GROWTH CHART
- Growth monitoring.
- Diagnostic tool for identifying high-risk children.
- Educational tool to teach mothers on the benefits of adequate feeding.
- Tool for action to help the health worker decide the type of intervention needed.
- Evaluation of effectiveness and impact of programs.
- Planning and policy making.

REFERENCE OR STANDARD VALUES OF GROWTH
- Harvard (Boston) standards.
- NCHS standards (WHO reference values).
- Indian standards (ICMR values).
- MGRS (WHO 2006 values): Derived from the Multicentric Growth Reference Study (1997-2003).
  - It was a cohort study on 8440 healthy breast-fed infants and children (0 to 60 months or 5 years).
  - Conducted in 6 countries: Brazil, India, Ghana, States (USA), Oman, Norway.

LOW BIRTH WEIGHT (LBW)
- Defined as a birth weight of less than 2.5 kg irrespective of gestational age.

CLASSIFICATION OF BIRTH WEIGHT
- Less than 2.5 kg: Low birth weight (LBW).
- 2.5 to 1.5 kg: Moderately Low birth weight (MLBW).
- 1.5 to 1 kg: Very Low birth weight (VLBW).
- Less than 1 kg: Extremely Low birth weight (ELBW).

PREVALENCE AND RISKS OF LBW
- Prevalence: 18 percent in India and 15 percent in the world.
- In India, most of the LBW babies weigh between 2 to 2.5 kg. If the cutoff is reduced to 2 kg, the expected prevalence drops to 5.5 percent.
- Percentage is based on the measurement of at least 500 babies.
- Most common cause of LBW: Prematurity.
- Leading causes of death in LBW babies (Pneumonia, Atelectasis, Pulmonary hemorrhage, Intra cranial bleeding).
- LBW is NOT a contraindication for vaccination, except Hepatitis B which is contraindicated in premature children weighing less than 2 kg.
- LBW babies include Preterm babies and Small for date babies.

RISK FACTORS OF LBW BABIES
- Maternal malnutrition: Majority of LBW in India is associated with fetal growth retardation. There is a linear relation between maternal nutrition and birth weight.
- Hard physical labor during pregnancy.
- Smoking: Reduces birth weight by approximately 170 gm.
- Infections.
- Short maternal stature.
- Young age.
- Unregulated fertility.
- High parity.

PRE-TERM BABIES
- Defined as babies born alive before 37 weeks (less than 259 days).
- Gestational Age Classification:
  - Greater than 42 weeks: Post term pregnancy.
  - 42 to 37 weeks: Term pregnancy.
  - Less than 37 weeks: Pre-term.
  - 37 to 34 weeks: Late preterm.
  - 34 to 32 weeks: Moderate preterm.
  - 32 to 28 weeks: Very preterm.
  - Less than 28 weeks: Extremely preterm.
- Types of Pre-term birth:
  - Spontaneous preterm birth: Spontaneous onset of labour or following Pre-labor Premature Rupture of Membranes (pPROM). Risk factors include multiple pregnancy, diabetes, undernutrition, smoking, excess alcohol.
  - Provider initiated preterm birth: Induction of labor or elective caesarian before 37 completed weeks.

SMALL FOR DATE BABIES
- Weigh less than the 10th percentile for the gestational age.
- Can be born at term or post term.
- These are babies born as a result of Intrauterine Growth Retardation (IUGR).
- Factors associated:
  - Maternal causes: Very young age, Short maternal status, Malnutrition, Anemia.
  - Placental causes: Placental abnormalities (abruptio placenta or placenta previa).
  - Fetal causes: Intrauterine infections, IUGR, Multiple gestation.

PREVENTION OF LBW
1. Direct intervention:
  - Increasing food intake: Supplementary feeding, distribution of iron folic acid tablets, food fortification.
  - Controlling infections: Treating maternal infections like cytomegalovirus, toxoplasmosis, rubella, syphilis.
  - Early detection and treatment of medical disorders like hypertension and diabetes.
2. Indirect intervention:
  - Family planning.
  - Avoidance of smoking.
  - Improved sanitation.
  - Maternal leave with full wages.

TREATMENT OF LBW BABIES
- Under 2 kg: Neonatal care in NICU until the weight reaches 2 kg.
- Between 2 to 2.5 kg: Incubatory care, feeding through nasal catheter, prevention of infection, and Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC).

KANGAROO MOTHER CARE (KMC)
- Introduced for low-birth-weight babies in Columbia in 1979 by Dr. Hector Martinez and Edzar Rey.
- Developed as a response to high infection and mortality rates due to overcrowding.
- Essential for ensuring the best care option for Low-Birth-Weight babies.
- 4 Components of KMC:
  1. Skin to skin positioning of a baby on the mother's chest.
  2. Adequate nutrition through Breast Feeding.
  3. Ambulatory care because of earlier discharge from hospital.
  4. Support for Mother and her Family in caring for the baby.

MNEMONICS
- 6 Countries for WHO MGRS study: BIGSON (Brazil, India, Ghana, States-USA, Oman, Norway).
- Leading causes of death in LBW babies: PAPI (Pneumonia, Atelectasis, Pulmonary hemorrhage, Intra cranial bleeding).
- Maternal causes for Small for Date babies: Very Short MA (Very young age, Short maternal status, Malnutrition, Anemia).
- Fetal causes for Small for Date babies: IIM (Intrauterine infections, IUGR, Multiple gestation).
- 4 Components of KMC: SBA Support (Skin to skin, Breast feeding, Ambulatory care, Support).

subsections.title: Mortality Indicators: Maternal, Perinatal, Neonatal, Infant, and Child Mortality
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
Maternal and child health status is assessed through measurements of mortality, morbidity, growth, and development. Mortality indicators are the most widely used metrics for evaluating the overall socio-economic development of a country, the effectiveness of health services, and the specific impact of maternal and child health interventions. 

MATERNAL MORTALITY RATIO (MMR)

DEFINITIONS
- Maternal death: Death of a woman while pregnant or within 42 days (6 weeks) of termination of pregnancy, irrespective of the duration and site of pregnancy, from any cause related to or aggravated by the pregnancy or its management, but not from accidental or incidental causes.
- Late maternal death: Death of a woman from direct or indirect obstetric causes after more than 42 days but less than one year after termination of pregnancy.
- Pregnancy-related death: Death of a woman while pregnant or within 42 days of termination, irrespective of the cause of death (includes accidental/incidental causes).

ICD CLASSIFIED MATERNAL DEATH INTO
- Direct obstetric deaths: Those resulting from obstetric complications of the pregnancy, labour, and puerperium interventions, omissions, or incorrect treatment (e.g., eclampsia, abortion, hemorrhage).
- Indirect obstetric deaths: Those resulting from previously existing disease or disease that developed during pregnancy, which was not due to direct obstetric causes but was aggravated by the physiological effects of pregnancy.

STATISTICAL MEASURES OF MATERNAL MORTALITY
- Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR): (Number of maternal deaths in a period / Total number of Live births in the same period) x 100,000.
- Maternal Mortality Rate (MMRt): (Number of maternal deaths in a period / Total number of Women of reproductive age group) x 100,000. It captures both the risk of maternal death per pregnancy and the level of fertility in the population.
- Proportion of maternal deaths: (Number of maternal deaths in a given time period / Total deaths of women of reproductive age group) x 1000.
- Adult lifetime risk of maternal death: The probability that a 15-year-old girl will eventually die from a maternal cause.

APPROACH FOR MEASURING MATERNAL MORTALITY
- Civil registration system: Routine registration of births and deaths. Ideal but often incomplete in developing countries.
- Household survey: Used where civil registration data is not available.
- Sisterhood method: Interviewing a representative sample about the survival of all their adult sisters.
- RAMOS (Reproductive Age Mortality Studies): Identifying the cause of death of women of reproductive age group in a defined area using multiple sources.
- Verbal autopsy: Interview with family or community members where the medical certification of cause of death is not available.
- RHIME: Representative, Re-sampled, Routine Household Interview of Mortality with Medical Evaluation. It is an enhanced form of verbal autopsy used in the Sample Registration System (SRS) in India.
- Census: A national census with limited questions to provide estimates and eliminate sampling error.

INCIDENCE OF MATERNAL MORTALITY
- Global: 223 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births (2020 data).
- India: 97 per 100,000 live births (SRS 2018-2020). MMR in developing regions is significantly higher than in developed regions.
- High-burden states in India: Assam has the maximum MMR (195), followed by Madhya Pradesh (173) and Uttar Pradesh (167).
- Low-burden states in India: Kerala has the minimum MMR (19), followed by Maharashtra (33).

DETERMINANTS AND CAUSES OF MATERNAL MORTALITY IN INDIA

1. Medical Causes
- Obstetric causes: Toxemias of pregnancy, Hemorrhage, Infection, Obstructed labour, Unsafe abortion.
- Non-obstetric causes: Anaemia, Associated diseases (cardiac, renal, hepatic, metabolic), Malignancy, Accidents.

2. Social Factors
- Age at childbirth (optimal age is 20 to 30 years).
- Too close pregnancies (short birth intervals).
- Parity (high parity contributes to high mortality).
- Family size.
- Poverty, illiteracy, ignorance, and prejudices.
- Poor environmental sanitation and lack of maternity services.

DISTRIBUTION OF CAUSES
- Maternal death mostly occurs from the 3rd trimester to the 1st week after birth. 
- Mortality risks for mothers are highly elevated within 2 days after birth.
- Majority (70 percent) of maternal deaths occur in the postpartum period, of which 45 percent occur in the first 24 hours of delivery.
- World causes: Hemorrhage (25 percent), Indirect causes (20 percent), Infection (15 percent), Unsafe abortion (13 percent), Eclampsia (12 percent), Obstructed labour (8 percent).
- India causes: Hemorrhage (38 percent), Other conditions (34 percent), Sepsis (16 percent), Abortion (8 percent), Obstructed labour (5 percent), Hypertensive disorders (5 percent). In India, approximately 50 percent of maternal death is caused by Hemorrhage and Sepsis.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES AND INITIATIVES
- Early registration of pregnancies and at least 4 antenatal checkups.
- Correction of anemia and Tetanus prophylaxis.
- Institutional deliveries or home delivery by trained Local Dais (LD).
- Prevention of complications like Eclampsia, hemorrhage, and infections.
- Treatment of medical conditions (hypertension, diabetes).
- Promotion of family planning and Safe abortion services.
- LaQshya (Labour Room Quality Improvement Initiative): To improve the quality of care provided in the labour room and maternity OT for safe delivery and newborn care.
- PMSMA (Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan): To provide quality and free of cost ANC services to women in the 2nd / 3rd trimester in designated government hospitals on the 9th of every month.
- Dakshata: Training of health care providers to improve MCH care during institutional delivery. It is a new initiative for capacity building by NHM and MoHFW.

PERINATAL MORTALITY RATE (PNMR)
- Perinatal period: Lasting from the 28th week of gestation to the 7th day after birth (WHO defines it as 22nd week to 7th day).
- Viable child: Baby chosen should be above 1000 gm of weight. If birth weight is not available, gestation period of at least 28 weeks is used. If both are not available, body length of at least 35 cm is used. Birth weight is the preferred criteria.
- PNMR formula: (Late fetal deaths i.e., Still births + Early neonatal deaths) / Total live births in the same year x 1000.
- It includes death from 28 weeks of gestation to the 1st week of life.
- Indicator of both obstetric and pediatric (neonatal) care given before and around the time of birth.
- Incidence in India: 18 per 1000 live births (SRS 2020).

CAUSES OF PERINATAL MORTALITY
- Antenatal causes: 
  - Maternal diseases (Hypertension, Diabetes, Anemia).
  - Pelvic diseases (Endometriosis, Ovarian tumor).
  - Anatomical defects (Uterine anomalies, Incompetent cervix).
- Intranatal causes: Birth injuries, Asphyxia, Obstetric complications.
- Postnatal causes: Prematurity (most common cause in India), Respiratory distress syndrome, Congenital anomalies.
- Social and Biological risk factors: Low socioeconomic status, short stature, high maternal age (35 years or more) or low maternal age (under 16 years), high parity, poor past obstetric history, heavy smoking, severe anemia.

PREVENTION OF PERINATAL MORTALITY
- Before pregnancy and during pregnancy: Well-timed and well-spaced pregnancies, Tetanus immunization, prevention of anemia.
- During and after delivery: Safe and clean delivery by Skilled Birth Attendant (SBA), early initiation of exclusive breast feeding.
- Abortion and Stillbirth: Account for 10 percent of wasted pregnancies. Expected number of total pregnancies in 1 year = Number of expected live births + 10 percent for pregnancy wastage. The ANM should have at least 50 percent pregnancies registered with her.

NEONATAL MORTALITY RATE (NMR)
- NMR formula: (Number of Neonatal Deaths in a given year / Total number of live births in the same year) x 1000.
- Neonatal mortality (NM): Deaths within 28 days of birth.
- Early neonatal mortality (ENM): Death within 1 to 7 days of life.
- Late neonatal mortality (LNM): Death between 8 to 28 days of life.
- Incidence in India: 20 per 1000 live births (SRS 2020). Target is less than 10 till 2035. NMR in boys is greater than in girls.
- Causes of NM globally: Preterm birth complications (35 percent), Intrapartum related complications / Asphyxia (24 percent), Sepsis (15 percent), Congenital abnormalities (11 percent).
- In India, the most common cause of NM is Preterm birth (LBW and Prematurity). 
  - ENM causes: Prematurity and Congenital anomalies.
  - LNM causes: Infections (Pneumonia, Diarrhea, Tetanus).

NEONATAL SCREENING
- Disease screened among neonates include:
  - Neonatal hypothyroidism (most common screened disorder).
  - Phenylketonuria (mass screening in neonates done by Guthrie test).
  - Sickle cell anemia.
  - Thalassemia.
  - Congenital dislocation of hip.
  - G6PD deficiency.

NEONATAL TETANUS
- Also known as the 8th day disease.
- August 2015: Maternal and Neonatal Tetanus was eliminated (less than 1 case per 1000 live births) from India.
- 5 cleans of safe delivery used for elimination of Neonatal Tetanus:
  1. Clean delivery surface (use clean plastic sheath).
  2. Clean hands (soap and water).
  3. Clean cord cut (new razor blade).
  4. Clean cord tie (clean thread).
  5. Clean cord stump (nothing to be applied).

POST NEONATAL MORTALITY RATE (PNMR)
- PNMR formula: (Number of Post Neonatal Deaths in a given year / Total number of live births in the same year) x 1000.
- Post Neonatal Deaths: Deaths between the age of 28 days to 1 year.
- Incidence in India: 8 per 1000 live births (SRS 2020).
- Common causes in India: Malnutrition, Diarrheal diseases, ARI.

INFANT MORTALITY RATE (IMR)
- IMR formula: (Number of Infant Deaths in a year / Total number of live births in the same year) x 1000.
- Important indicator of: 
  - Availability, utilization, and effectiveness of health services.
  - Health in a community.
  - Most universally accepted indicator for health status.
  - It is the 2nd best indicator of socioeconomic development of a country (Under-5 Mortality Rate being the 1st).
- Incidence in India: 28 per 1000 live births (SRS 2020). 
  - Maximum: Madhya Pradesh (43).
  - Minimum: Kerala (6).
  - IMR accounts for roughly 18 percent of total deaths in India.
- Mortality pattern: 68 percent of infant deaths occur within the 1st month. Of these, 75 percent may die during the 1st week. Risk of death is greatest during the first 24 to 48 hours. In India, after the age of 1 month, female deaths are higher.
- Principal cause of IMR in India: LBW and Prematurity (most common), followed by Pneumonia, Diarrheal diseases, Congenital malformations, and Cord infections.
- Most common cause of IMR in the World: Pneumonia.

CRITERIA FOR AT RISK INFANTS
- LBW (less than 2.5 kg).
- Failure to gain weight during 3 successive months.
- Weight below 70 percent of the expected weight.
- Child with PEM, diarrhea.
- Birth order 5 or more.
- Twins.
- Artificial feeding.
- Working mother.
- Single parent.

FACTORS AFFECTING INFANT MORTALITY
- Age of mother: IMR is greater when the age of the mother is very young (under 19 years) or relatively old (over 35 years).
- Maternal education: Plays a major role in the decline of infant and child mortality (e.g., Kerala experience).
- Birth order: Highest mortality is found in the 1st born, lowest mortality is among the 2nd born child, and mortality risks increase from the 3rd born onwards.
- Birth spacing: IMR is highest for infants born at an interval of 1 year, and lowest for an interval of 2 to 3 years.
- Breast feeding: Early weaning and bottle-fed infants are more prone to die.
- Sex of a child: Female infant mortality is higher in India due to less attention and care by parents compared to males.
- Habits/customs: Higher with habits like not feeding colostrum and applying cow dung to the umbilical stump.

CHILD DEATH RATE (1 TO 4 YEAR MORTALITY RATE)
- Formula: (Number of deaths of children aged 1 to 4 years in a year / Midyear population of children aged 1 to 4 years) x 1000.
- Important Note: Do NOT have live births as the denominator, but have midyear population.
- Most common cause: 
  - Developing country: Diarrheal diseases and respiratory infections.
  - Developed countries: Accidents.
- Highest risk of death is in the 2nd year of life.
- It is a better indicator of the Social Situation in a country than IMR.
- It does NOT suggest under-5 year care of the community.

CHILD SURVIVAL RATE (CSR) / CHILD SURVIVAL INDEX
- Formula: (1000 - U5MR) / 10.
- Mainly includes U5MR. 
- Current CSR in India is approximately 96.

UNDER-5 MORTALITY RATE (U5MR) / CHILD MORTALITY RATE (CMR)
- CMR formula: (Number of deaths of children less than 5 years in a year / Number of Live births in a year) x 1000.
- Incidence in India: 32 per 1000 live births (SRS 2020).
- It is the single best indicator of socio-economic development and wellbeing.
- Neonatal conditions lead to nearly 47 percent of total U5MR.
- Denominator of Under-5 Proportionate mortality rate includes total deaths in the same year, whereas U5MR uses total live births.

MNEMONICS
- Obstetric causes of maternal death: Hyper He Obstructed In Unsafe Toxic OBG (Hypertension, Hemorrhage, Obstructed labour, Infection, Unsafe abortion, Toxemia of pregnancy).
- Non-obstetric causes of maternal death: AAAM (Anemia, Associated diseases, Accidents, Malignancy).

subsections.title: Under-5 Clinic, Congenital Disorders, and Battered Baby Syndrome
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The delivery of comprehensive health care to young children is facilitated through specialized facilities like the Under-5 Clinic. Additionally, early identification and management of congenital disorders, as well as addressing social and clinical emergencies like child abuse and the Battered Baby Syndrome, are critical components of preventive paediatrics.

UNDER-5 CLINIC
- It is a centre where Preventive, Promotive, Curative, Referral, and Educational services are provided in a package manner through trained nurses to children under 5 years of age.
- Goal: To provide comprehensive health care to young children in a separate specialized facility.
- Essential activities:
  - Supervision of health of all the children up to 5 years of age.
  - Prevention of malnutrition, malaria, measles, pertussis, TB, etc.
  - Provision of simple treatment for diarrhea, dehydration, pneumonia, and skin infections.

LOGO OF THE UNDER-5 CLINIC
- The logo consists of 1 outer triangle with 4 inner triangles.
- An Outer enveloping triangle represents Health Education, which touches all the other four inner triangles.
- The four inner triangles represent the core services:
  - Care in illness (Apex / top triangle).
  - Growth monitoring (Left triangle).
  - Preventive care (Right triangle).
  - Family planning (Central inverted triangle).

FUNCTIONS OF THE UNDER-5 CLINIC

1. Care in illness
- Essential diagnostic services: Including laboratory investigations and X-ray facilities.
- Treatment services: For Acute and Chronic illness, and disorders of growth and development.
- Effective referral system.

2. Growth monitoring
- Weight, height, and mid-arm circumference are measured periodically.
- Frequency of weighing the child:
  - 1st year: Every month.
  - 2nd and 3rd year: Every 2 months.
  - 4th and 5th year: Every 3 months.
- Growth is monitored through the growth chart.
- Milestones are recorded and any delay is evaluated.

3. Preventive care
- Immunization, iron, and vitamin A supplementation, and anti-Helminthic treatment.
- Also provides regular checkup and use of ORS during diarrhea.

4. Family planning
- Mothers during early puerperium and lactation are counseled on the various options available to them along with their merits and demerits.

5. Health education
- Educate the mother regarding childcare, breast feeding, nutrition, growth monitoring, immunization, and hygiene of safe water and food.

CONGENITAL DISORDERS AMONG NEWBORNS

DEFINITIONS AND INCIDENCE
- Definition: Diseases that are determined before or during birth and which are recognizable in early life.
- Global Incidence: 30 per 1000 live births (Most common are Cardiovascular and Nervous system disorders).
- Incidence in India: Birth defects in Indian newborns account for approximately 2.5 percent.
- Regional variations in India:
  - North India: Neural tube defects or Spina bifida are most common.
  - Rest of India: Musculoskeletal disorders are most common.

CAUSES OF CONGENITAL DISORDERS
- Genetic factors:
  - Chromosomal abnormalities: Down's syndrome, Klinefelter's syndrome, Turner's syndrome.
  - Inborn errors of metabolism: Phenylketonuria (PKU), Tay-Sachs disease, galactosemia.
  - Single gene disorders: Huntington's chorea, thalassemia, sickle cell disease, hemophilia.
- Environmental factors:
  - Intra-uterine infections: Rubella, cytomegalovirus (CMV), toxoplasmosis, syphilis.
  - Drugs: Thalidomide, anti-convulsants, high dose of vitamin A.
  - Maternal diseases: Diabetes, cardiac deficiency.
  - Others: Alcohol, anaesthetics, irradiation, and dietary factors (e.g., folic acid deficiency).

RISK FACTORS AND PRENATAL DIAGNOSIS
- Risk factors: Advanced maternal age (risk of giving birth to an infant with Down's syndrome rises sharply after 40 years of age to 1 in 67) and consanguinity (first-cousin and uncle-niece marriages increase the incidence of mental retardation and congenital malformations).
- Prenatal diagnosis techniques:
  - Alpha fetoprotein: Detected in maternal blood and amniotic fluid for Neural tube defects.
  - Ultrasound: To visualize the fetus and detect structural anomalies.
  - Amniocentesis: Possible only in the second trimester (after the completion of 12 weeks) to detect chromosomal abnormalities.
  - Chorionic villus sampling: Allows prenatal diagnosis at 9 to 11 weeks of pregnancy.

BATTERED BABY SYNDROME AND CHILD ABUSE

DEFINITIONS AND SYNONYMS
- Battered Baby Syndrome is also known as Caffey Syndrome, Shaking Baby Syndrome, Infantile Whiplash Syndrome, or Non-Accidental Injury.
- Definition: A clinical condition in young children, usually under 3 years of age, who have received non-accidental, wholly inexcusable violence or injury on one or more occasions, including trauma by a parent, guardian, or foster parent.
- WHO Definition of Child Abuse: All forms of physical and/or emotional ill-treatment, sexual abuse, neglect, or commercial or other exploitation resulting in actual or potential harm to the child's health, survival, development, or dignity in the context of a relationship of responsibility, trust, or power.

CLINICAL FEATURES OF BATTERED BABY SYNDROME
- Most common cause of death in fatal child abuse is Head trauma.
- Skull fracture: Diastatic fracture with Egg Shell Calcification.
- Frequent shaking: Leads to Subdural and Periorbital hematoma.
- Rib injuries: Multiple fractures on ribs (Callus formation with String of Beads appearance).
- Skin injuries: Six Penny Bruises caused by finger tips.

TYPES OF CHILD ABUSE
- Physical abuse: Intentional use of physical force (hitting, kicking, shaking, burning).
- Sexual abuse: Pressuring or forcing a child to engage in a sexual act.
- Emotional abuse: Behaviors that harm a child's self-worth or emotional well-being (name calling, shaming, rejection, withholding love and threatening).
- Neglect: Failure to meet a child's basic physical and emotional needs (housing, food, clothing, education, and access to medical care).

PREVENTION STRATEGIES (INSPIRE)
INSPIRE is a set of seven evidence-based strategies developed by WHO to end violence against children:
- I: Implementation and enforcement of laws.
- N: Norms and values change.
- S: Safe environments.
- P: Parent and caregiver support.
- I: Income and economic strengthening.
- R: Response and support services.
- E: Education and life skills.

subsections.title: Behavioral Problems, Juvenile Delinquency, Child Rights, and APGAR Score
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The psychosocial development of a child is as important as their physical growth. Behavioral problems and juvenile delinquency are significant issues that require intervention through proper schooling, child guidance clinics, and specialized child placement facilities. Ensuring the rights of women and children and using a risk approach in MCH helps to target interventions effectively. The APGAR score is a critical tool used immediately after birth to assess the newborn's physical condition.

BEHAVIORAL PROBLEMS IN CHILDREN
- Behavioral disorders are due to mental deficiency, organic disease, and failure in adjustment to the external environment.
- Answer to these problems lies in the improvement of living conditions, better social environment, and education.
- Classification of behavioral problems:
  - Antisocial in nature: Stealing, lying, cruelty, destructiveness.
  - Habit disorders: Thumb sucking, nail biting, bed wetting, masturbation.
  - Personality disorders: Jealousy, temper tantrums, shyness, fears, anxieties, unsociability.
  - Psychosomatic complaints: Depression, delusion, hallucination, tremors, headache, asthma.
  - Educational difficulties: School phobia, school failures, backwardness in studies.

JUVENILE DELINQUENCY
- Delinquent: A child who has committed an offence.
- Juvenile: A boy or a girl who has not completed 18 years of age.
  - Note: Juvenile Justice Act 2015 allows for a juvenile of 16 years or older to be put on trial as an adult for heinous offences like rape and murder.
- Delinquency: Deviation from normal youthful behavior. It embraces deviations from normal youthful behavior and includes the incorrigible, ungovernable, habitually disobedient, and those who desert their homes and indulge in anti-social activities.
- Incidence: Highest incidence is found in children aged above 15 years. It is 5 times higher in boys than among girls.

CAUSES OF JUVENILE DELINQUENCY
- Biological causes: Hereditary defects, feeble-mindedness, physical defects, glandular imbalance, and Extra Y chromosomes (XYY men).
- Social causes: Parental neglect, broken homes (death of parents, separation of parents, stepmother), poverty, alcoholism, ignorance about child care, and too many children.
- Other causes: Absence of recreation facilities, cheap recreation, urbanization and industrialization, cinemas and television, and slum-dwelling.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES
- Improvement of family life: Parents should be prepared for parenthood to ensure a well-adjusted family.
- Schooling: A healthy teacher-student relationship helps detect early signs of maladjustment.
- Social welfare services: Provision of recreation facilities, parent-counselling, child guidance, and educational facilities.

CHILD GUIDANCE CLINIC
- Started in Chicago in 1909 and was originally intended to deal with Juvenile Delinquency.
- Deals with children or adolescents who are not fully adjusted to their environment.
- Objective: To prevent children from becoming neurotics and psychotics in later life.
- Team of child guidance clinic includes:
  - Psychiatrist, Clinical psychologist, Educational psychologist, and Psychiatric social workers.
  - Pediatrician, Neurologist, and Public health nurses.
  - Speech therapist and Occupational therapist.
- Services provided:
  - Take care of the physical health of a child and change the physical environment.
  - Psychotherapy and Play therapy to restore positive feelings of security.
  - Counseling and Reconstruction of parental attitude.

CHILDREN IN DIFFICULT CIRCUMSTANCES
- Homeless or Abandoned children (pavement dwellers, displaced/evicted).
- Separated from parents or Orphaned children whose parents cannot take care of them.
- Migrants or Refugee or Street children.
- Children who are victims of crime or Trafficked children.
  - Ujjawala scheme: A comprehensive scheme for prevention of child trafficking and for rescuing, rehabilitation, and repatriation of victims trafficked for commercial sexual exploitation.
  - Immoral Traffic Prevention Act 1956.
  - Children's Act 1960.
- Children of sex workers/prisoners or Children in bondage/prostitution.
- Children suffering from terminal diseases or with disabilities.

RIGHTS OF WOMEN AND CHILDREN
- Child rights include:
  - Rights to Expression.
  - Right to Recreation.
  - Right to Name and Nationality.
- Article 21 A: Free education (6 to 14 years of age).
- Article 24: Protects from hazardous environment till 14 years of age.
- Article 39: Prevents abuse of children of tender age.
- Article 45: Free compulsory education (till 14 years of age).
- INSPIRE strategy: Used for monitoring and implementation of policies to prevent and end violence against children (Implementation and Enforcement of Laws, Norms and Values, Safe environment, Parent and Caregiver support, Income and Economic strengthening, Response and Support services, Education and Life skills).

CHILD PLACEMENT
- Orphanages: For children with no home or who cannot be taken care of by their parents. Mass care of infants is generally considered undesirable.
- Foster homes: For rearing children other than in natural families (temporary arrangement where a child lives usually on a temporary basis with an extended or unrelated family member).
- Adoption: Confers upon adoptive parents rights and responsibilities similar to natural parents. An abandoned child legally accepted by a couple is called an adaptation and foster home placement.
- Remand homes: A child in conflict with the law is placed under the care of doctors and psychiatrists to improve mental and physical health. Elementary schooling, arts, crafts, and games are provided.
- Borstals:
  - Boys over 16 years who are too difficult to be handled in school or have misbehaved are sent to borstals.
  - Falls in between a certified school and an adult prison.
  - Sentence is usually for 3 years, regarded as a method of training and reformation.
  - In India, it does not come under the Children's Act but is governed by the state inspector general for prisons.
  - Bombay Borstal School Act 1929: Authorizes first-class magistrate and superior courts to order detention in borstals (in place of imprisonment).
  - Detention is not less than 3 years and not more than 5 years.
  - Applies to young offenders: Boys 16 to 21 years of age, Girls 18 to 21 years of age.

RISK APPROACH IN MCH
- Definition: A managerial tool for better use of scarce resources by early detection of mothers and children with high-risk factors.
- Means of improving coverage and efficiency of MCH care and family planning.
- Helps in better use of scarce resources and enables the determination of priority activities.
- High risk mothers and children are given additional and more skilled care, including hospitalization if needed.
- Degree of risk is assessed on the basis of:
  1. Magnitude: Extent and severity.
  2. Treatability: Responsiveness to treatment and control.
  3. Cost effect: In terms of alleviating human suffering.
  4. Community attitude: Social concern.

APGAR SCORE
- Provides an immediate estimate of the physical condition of the baby.
- Taken at:
  - 1 minute after birth: Tells overall status of the baby at the time of birth.
  - 5 minutes after birth: Tells effectiveness of neonatal resuscitation.
- Infants with a low Apgar score at 5 minutes of age are subject to high risk.
- A score of 6 or less needs prompt action.
- Total Score Interpretation:
  - 0 to 3: Severely depressed.
  - 4 to 6: Moderately depressed.
  - 7 to 10: Excellent condition.

PARAMETERS OF APGAR SCORE
- A (Appearance / Skin colour):
  - 0: Blue or Pale all over.
  - 1: Pink body, Blue extremities.
  - 2: Pink body completely.
- P (Pulse / Heart rate):
  - 0: No Pulse / Absent.
  - 1: Less than 100 bpm (Slow).
  - 2: More than 100 bpm (Over 100).
- G (Grimace / Reflex irritability):
  - 0: Floppy / No response.
  - 1: Grimaces or weak cry.
  - 2: Prompt response, cries and pulls away.
- A (Activity / Muscle tone):
  - 0: No Movement / Flaccid.
  - 1: Flexed arms and Legs / Some flexion of extremities.
  - 2: Active motion / Good crying.
- R (Respiration effort):
  - 0: Absent.
  - 1: Slow, irregular.
  - 2: Strong cry.

subsections.title: School Health Services
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
School health services are an economical and powerful means of raising community health and, more importantly, future generations. The school is a strategic place for shaping the child's behavior and promoting mental and physical health. The most important functionary of the school health program is the School Teacher.

SCHOOL HEALTH SERVICES
- India introduced school health services for the first time in 1909 in Baroda city.
- School health program was systematically started in 1960.
- Most important functionary is the School Teacher.
- School health services emphasize on malnutrition, infectious diseases, intestinal parasites, disease of skin / eye / ear and dental caries.

HEALTH PROBLEMS OF A SCHOOL CHILD
1. Dental defects (most prevalent).
2. Goiter.
3. Malnutrition.
4. Intestinal parasites.
5. Eye diseases.

OBJECTIVES OF SCHOOL HEALTH SERVICES
- Positive health.
- Disease prevention.
- Early diagnosis, treatment and follow-up.
- Health consciousness.
- Healthful environment.

SCHOOL HEALTH EXAMINATION RECOMMENDATIONS

1. Rennuka Roy school health committee (1961)
- Laid the foundation of the school health program in India.
- Recommended medical examination of children at the time of entry and then every 4 years.

2. NRHM (National Rural Health Mission)
- Recommended medical examination once every 6 months.

3. School eye screening program
- Target group: V - VIII class (10 to 14 years).
- Staffing requirement: 1 teacher to screen 150 students.
- Visual acuity cutoff for referral to Primary Health Centre (PHC) is less than 6/9.

ASPECTS OF SCHOOL HEALTH SERVICES

1. School Environment
- Location: 
  - Centrally situated with proper approach roads.
  - Away from noise i.e. from roads, cinemas, factories, railway tracks and marketplaces.
  - Properly fenced.
- Site: 
  - Primary school requires 5 acres.
  - Higher secondary school requires 10 acres.
- Structure: 
  - Single storied buildings are preferred.
- Classroom: 
  - Maximum 40 students per class.
  - Space requirement is 10 sq ft per capita.
- Furniture: 
  - Single desk and chair.
  - Minus type desk is recommended (Negative / Minus type of desks encourage proper posture).
- Doors and windows: 
  - Combined door and window area should be 25 percent of the floor space.
  - Placed on opposite walls for cross ventilation.
  - Ventilators should be 2 percent of the floor space.
- Color: 
  - White colored walls.
- Lighting: 
  - Natural light preferably coming from the left.
- Water supply: 
  - Safe and potable water.
- Eating facilities: 
  - Separate room provided for the mid-day meal.
- Lavatory: 
  - Separate for boys and girls.
  - 1 urinal for 60 students.
  - 1 latrine for 100 students.

2. Health Appraisal
- Periodic medical examination to detect defects.
- Daily morning inspection by the teacher to detect changes in child's appearance or behavior.

3. Nutritional Services
- Mid-day meal program.
- Applied nutrition program (providing seeds, manure, and equipment for school gardens).

4. First Aid and Emergency Care
- Teacher training program to prepare teachers to handle first aid for accidents and emergencies on school premises.

5. Dental Health
- Dental examination is recommended once a year to prevent and treat dental caries and periodontal diseases.

6. Eye Health Services
- Detection of Refractive errors and Trachoma.
- Administration of Vitamin A to prevent nutritional blindness.

7. Personal Hygiene
- Education on the hygiene of skin, hairs, nails, teeth, and clothing.

subsections.title: Geriatrics and Health Care of the Elderly
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
Ageing is a natural, inevitable biological phenomenon. The study of the physical and psychological changes incident to old age is called gerontology, while the care of the aged is called geriatrics. With the lengthening span of life due to discoveries in medical science and improved social conditions, the proportion of elderly people is continually on the increase, bringing a variety of social, economic, and healthcare policy challenges.

DEFINITIONS
- Gerontology: Study of Physical and Psychological changes at old age.
- Clinical gerontology / Geriatrics: Care of aged people.
- Social gerontology: Care of the aged, focusing on humanitarian and social attitudes towards the problems of the elderly.
- Experimental gerontology: Research into biological problems of aging, its psychology, biochemistry, and pathology.
- Geriatric Gynaecology: A new chapter in gynaecology dealing with older patients presenting with prolapse, specific vaginitis, ovarian tumours, and psychic aberrations.

DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGEING
- Geriatric age group in India: Defined as 60 years and above.
- Global population: Italy and Japan have the highest number and proportion of older persons globally.
- Indian population: The elderly constitute approximately 8.1 percent to 9.3 percent of the total population (based on recent estimates), but the absolute size is considerable.

HEALTH PROBLEMS OF THE AGED

1. Problems due to Ageing
- Cataract (Senile cataract).
- Glaucoma.
- Nerve deafness.
- Osteoporosis (affecting mobility).
- Emphysema.
- Failure of special senses.
- Changes in mental outlook.

2. Problems due to Long term illness
- Atherosclerosis: Narrowing of blood vessels leading to diminished blood supply and hypertension.
- Hypertension: Major cause of death in the developed countries.
- Cancer prostate: Common after the age of 65.
- Fracture neck of femur: A very common geriatric problem due to accidents and fragile bones from decalcification.
- Rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, gout, spondylitis, fibrositis, and myositis.
- Asthma, emphysema, and chronic bronchitis.
- Diabetes mellitus.

3. Psychological problems
- Impaired memory.
- Diminution of sexual activity.
- Social maladjustment.
- Depression, despondency, irritability, and jealousy.
- Rigidity of outlook and dislike of change.

COMMON ELDERLY AILMENTS AND CAUSES OF DEATH
- Common Elderly ailments (in order of frequency):
  1. Visual impairment (most common).
  2. Locomotive disorder.
  3. Neurological complaints.
  4. Cardiovascular disease.
  5. Respiratory disorder.
- Most common health disorder: Visual impairment (cataract).
- Most common cause of death in 70 years and above: Cardiovascular disorders.

LIFESTYLE AND HEALTHY AGEING
- Diet and Nutrition: Reduce saturated fats, trans-fatty acids, salt, and sugar. Include plenty of calcium-rich food and high fibre diet.
- Exercise: Helps maintain good health, controls weight, improves blood circulation, increases flexibility, and lowers blood sugar levels.
- Weight: Overweight and obesity are major factors in heart disease, stroke, hypertension, diabetes, and arthritis.
- Smoking and Alcohol: Cessation of smoking reduces the risk of dying. Alcohol tolerance decreases with age; heavy drinking is linked to liver diseases, stomach ulcers, and hypertension.
- Social Activities: Getting out and mixing with others creates a sense of belonging and helps prevent isolation and depression.

SERVICES AND PROGRAMS FOR THE AGED IN INDIA
- National policy on older person: Announced in January 1999 to provide financial security, health care, nutrition, shelter, and education.
- National Council for Older Persons (NCOP): Constituted to operationalize the National Policy.
- National social assistance program: Provides old age pension to destitute elderly over the country.
- Old Age Social and Income Security (OASIS): A project launched to comprehensively examine pension questions connected with old age income security.
- Bhavishya Arogya Mediclaim and Rural group life insurance schemes: Healthcare and insurance provisions for the elderly.
- Indira Gandhi national old age pension scheme: Launched on 19th Nov 2007 to provide a monthly pension to people over 65 years living below the poverty line.
- Help-Age India: The largest voluntary organization working for the cause and care of the disadvantaged older people. Provides free cataract operations, mobile medicare units, micro-credit, old-age homes, day-care centres, and an "Adopt-A-Gran" initiative.
- Travel concessions: Provided by Indian Railways, Indian Airlines, and State Transport Corporations.
- Income Tax concessions: Available to elderly citizens.

POTENTIAL FOR DISEASE PREVENTION IN THE ELDERLY

1. Primary prevention
- Habits: Cessation of Smoking and Alcohol abuse.
- Physical activity and adequate sleep.
- Nutrition and Obesity control.
- Injury prevention (preventing falls) and iatrogenesis prevention.
- Osteoporosis prevention.
- Immunization: Influenza, pneumovax, and tetanus.

2. Secondary prevention (Screening)
- Screening for hypertension and diabetes.
- Screening for cancer (colo-rectal cancer, breast cancer, cervical cancer, prostatic cancer).
- Screening for depression.
- Screening for periodontal disease, dental caries, and sensory impairment.
- Checking for medication side-effects, urinary incontinence, podiatric problems, and fall risk.
- High-risk screening for tuberculosis, syphilis, and myocardial infarction.

3. Tertiary prevention
- Rehabilitation to manage physical deficits, cognitive deficits, and functional deficits.
- Caretaker support and introduction of support necessary to prevent loss of autonomy.

subsections.title: National Programs and Schemes in Maternal and Child Health
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The Government of India has launched multiple vertical and integrated programmes targeting maternal and child health, adolescent health, and nutrition to reduce morbidity and mortality. Key programmes include the Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS), Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness (IMNCI), Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY), and various Rashtriya Karyakrams.

INTEGRATED CHILD DEVELOPMENT SERVICES (ICDS) SCHEME
- Launched: 2 October 1975.
- It is a Centrally sponsored scheme.
- Ministry: Ministry of Women and Child development (human resource development department).
- Heart of the ICDS system: Anganwadi.
- Focal point of ICDS services: Anganwadi worker.

ICDS PROJECT STRUCTURE
- 1 Community Development Block acts as the administrative unit of ICDS in rural areas.
- Rural / Urban project: Covers 1 lakh population.
- Tribal project: Covers 35000 population.

STAFFING HIERARCHY
- Child Development Project Officer (CDPO): 1 CDPO for 1 ICDS project (1 CDPO = 1 lakh population).
- Supervisor (Mukhya Sevika): Looks after the work of Anganwadi worker. 4 Supervisors work under 1 CDPO, and each supervisor covers 25 Anganwadis (1 Mukhya Sevika = 25000 population).
- Anganwadi Worker (AWW): 100 AWWs operate under 1 CDPO (1 AWW = 800 to 1000 population).

ANGANWADI CENTRE DETAILS
- Staff: 1 Anganwadi worker and 1 Helper (Sahayaka).
- Rural / urban project Anganwadi: Covers 400 to 800 population.
- Tribal project Anganwadi: Covers 300 to 800 population.
- Mini Anganwadi centre: Covers 150 to 300 population.
- Anganwadi on demand: Can be set up for a settlement with 40 or more children under 6 years.
- Mahila mandals: Includes previous beneficiaries in order to encourage others and spread awareness regarding services available at the Anganwadi centre.

OBJECTIVES OF ICDS SCHEME
- To improve Nutritional and Health status of children in the age group 0 to 6 years.
- To lay the foundation for proper Psychological, Physical and Social development of the child.
- To reduce Mortality, Morbidity, Malnutrition and School dropout.
- To promote Safe motherhood.

BENEFICIARIES OF ICDS
- I. Children less than 3 years.
- II. Children 3 to 6 years.
- III. Adolescent girls (10 to 19 years).
- IV. Women of reproductive age group (15 to 45/49 years).
- V. Pregnant women.
- VI. Nursing / Lactating mothers.

SERVICES PROVIDED UNDER ICDS
1. Supplementary nutrition: Given for 300 days in a year.
  - Children: Provided 500 K.cal and 12 to 15 gm Protein as 1 meal at Anganwadi (Cost Rs 8).
  - Pregnant and Nursing woman: Provided 600 K.cal and 15 to 20 gm Protein as Take home rashan (Cost Rs 9.5).
  - Malnourished child: Provided 800 K.cal and 20 to 25 gm Protein as 1 meal at Anganwadi (Cost Rs 12.5).
2. Nutrition and Health Education: Given to women of reproductive age group, Pregnant Women and Nursing Mothers.
3. Nonformal or Preschool education: For children 3 to 6 years using locally produced inexpensive toys.
4. Immunization: For children against vaccine preventable diseases, and for pregnant women against Tetanus and Diphtheria (Td).
5. Health checkups: 
  - ANC and PNC services.
  - Care of children under 6 years including record of weight for age (using Growth Charts), height and milestones, immunization, deworming, and prophylaxis against Vitamin A and Anemia.
6. Referral services: To First Referral Units (FRU) i.e., mostly Community Health Centres (CHC).

SCHEMES UNDER ICDS
- Kishori Shakti Yojna: Rename of Adolescent girl's scheme. Activities include Hygiene and Nutrition education, Family life education, Skill development and Vocational training. Interventions are executed by Adolescent girls I (11 to 15 years old girls using a Girl to girl approach) and Adolescent girls II (11 to 18 years old girls through Balika mandals).
- Rajiv Gandhi scheme for empowerment of Adolescent girls (SABLA): Aimed to improve Nutrition and Health status of adolescent girls. Utilizes Direct cash transfer. Includes National Skill Develop Program (NSDP), Adolescent Reproductive Sexual Health (ARSH) clinic, Adolescent Friendly Health Clinic (AFHC), Wheat Based Nutrition Program (WBNP), and Anemia Mukt Bharat (AMB).
- Nutrition program for Adolescent girls: Targets undernourished adolescent girls in age group 11 to 19 years. 6 kg of food grains are provided to each beneficiary per month.
- Indra Gandhi Matritva Yojna: Cash transfer is made to pregnant and lactating women in order to improve their nutritional and health status.
- Sneha Shivir: Targeted for the Malnourished child.
- Anganwadi Karyakarta Bima Yojna: Health insurance scheme for Anganwadi workers.

INTEGRATED MANAGEMENT OF NEONATAL AND CHILDHOOD ILLNESS (IMNCI)
- IMNCI is the Indian version of IMCI (Integrated Management of Childhood Illness).
- IMCI was developed by WHO and UNICEF targeting children less than 5 years.
- Major IMNCI adaptations in India:
  - Includes early neonates of 0 to 7 days of age.
  - Emphasis is on sick neonates, as compared to sick older children.
  - Incorporating national guidelines on Malaria, Anemia, Vitamin A supplementation and Immunization schedule.
  - Training of health workers.
- According to IMNCI, the term Young Infants includes children below 2 months of age.
- 3 Strategies:
  - Improvement of Case management skills of health staff.
  - Improvements in the Health system.
  - Improvements in family and community Health practices.
- Preventive component: Breast feeding and Nutritional counseling, Vitamin A and Iron supplementation, Immunization and Treatment of Helminthic infestation.
- Curative component: Integrated case management of most common childhood problems. (Mnemonic DM - MAM: Diarrhea, Malnutrition, Measles, ARI/pneumonia, Malaria).

CASE MANAGEMENT STEPS IN IMNCI
- i. Assess the child: Check for danger signs, possible bacterial infection, main symptoms, and examine nutrition and immunization status.
- ii. Classify illness: Uses a color-coded triage system.
- iii. Identify treatment.
- iv. Treat the child: Administer oral drugs, feed, and give fluids.
- v. Counsel mother.
- vi. Give follow up care.

INTEGRATED CASE MANAGEMENT PROCESS
1. Check for danger Signs: Inability to breast feed / drink, Persistent vomiting, Severe malnutrition, Unconsciousness / lethargy, Convulsions.
2. Assess main Symptoms: Fever, Ear problems (Otitis media), Difficulty in breathing, Diarrhea.
3. Classify condition using Color-Coded Triage:
  - Pink (NOT RED): Urgent referral and Emergency treatment in IPD.
  - Yellow: Treatment at OPD by giving oral drugs.
  - Green: Home management can be done by Treating local infections at home.

F-IMNCI (FACILITY BASED IMNCI)
- Integration of IMNCI package and Facility-based care package into one.
- Added component: Asphyxia management and Care of sick newborn at the facility level.
- Helps in Skill building of Medical officers and Staff nurses posted in facilities to manage newborn and childhood illness.

JANANI SURAKSHA YOJNA (JSY)
- The National Maternity Benefit Scheme was modified into Janani Suraksha Yojna (JSY) in 2005.
- Objectives: Reducing Maternal mortality and Infant mortality BY encouraging Institutional Delivery.
- Salient features: 
  - 100 percent Centrally Sponsored scheme.
  - Under NRHM, it integrates the benefit of Cash Assistance (to Mother and ASHA) with institutional care during antenatal care, delivery care and immediate post-partum care.
- Cash Assistance structure:
  - Low Performing State (Rural): Mother 1400, ASHA 600.
  - Low Performing State (Urban): Mother 1000, ASHA 400.
  - High Performing State (Rural): Mother 700, ASHA 600.
  - High Performing State (Urban): Mother 600, ASHA 400.
- Eligibility criteria:
  - Age greater than 20 years.
  - NOT more than 2 live children.
  - Low performing states (LPS): Any woman with Institutional Delivery (either BPL or not BPL).
  - High performing states (HPS): Only Below Poverty Line (BPL) women with institutional delivery.
- ASHA should get her money after her post-natal visit to the beneficiary and when the child has been immunized for BCG.
- Yojna subsidizes the cost of caesarean section and for management of obstetric complications upto 1500 per delivery.

JANANI SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (JSSK)
- Objectives: To promote Public Health Facility utilization.
- Beneficiaries: Pregnant Women and Infants.
- Facilities provided for pregnant women:
  - Free Delivery and Caesarian section.
  - Free Drugs and Diet upto 3 days during Normal delivery and 7 days in Caesarian section.
  - Free Diagnostics and Blood transfusion.
  - Free Transport from home to institution and back to home.
- Similar facilities are provided to all sick Newborns for treatment till 30 days after birth.

RASHTRIYA KISHOR SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM (RKSK)
- India's first comprehensive Adolescent Health Programme.
- Target group: Adolescent 10 to 19 years age in urban and rural areas (includes Girls and Boys, Married or Unmarried, Poor or Affluent, School or Out of school).
- Strategy: RMNCH+A.
- Strategic priorities: Nutrition, Mental health, Substance misuse, Sexual and Reproductive health, NCD's, Injuries and Violence.
- Approach: Peer to Peer approach / Sathiya approach.
- 7 components: Clinics, Content, Communication, Counseling, Communities, Convergence and Coverage.

NAVJAAT SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (NSSK)
- Basic Newborn Care and Resuscitation Program.
- Administered by Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) under NRHM.
- Launched for prevention of Hypothermia and Infection.
- Aim: To Train health personnel in basic newborn care and resuscitation.
- Objective: To have a trained health person in basic Newborn care and Resuscitation unit at every delivery point.

RASHTRIYA BAL SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM (RBSK)
- Launched in 2013.
- Target group: 
  - 0 to 6 years children (Rural areas and Urban slums).
  - 6 to 18 years children (enrolled in Government hospitals and schools).
- Provision for Child health screening and Early intervention services programme (under NRHM) through early detection and management of 4 D's.
- The 4 D's cover 34 conditions:
  - I. Defects at birth: NTD, Cleft Lip, Cleft Palate, Club foot, Congenital heart disease, Congenital deafness, Congenital cataract, Developmental dysplasia of hip, Retinopathy of prematurity.
  - II. Diseases of childhood: Dental caries, Otitis media, Rheumatic heart disease, Reactive airway disease, Scabies, Eczema, Fungal infections, Convulsive disorders.
  - III. Deficiencies: Anemia, Vitamin A deficiency (Bitot's spot), Vitamin D deficiency (Rickets), Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM), Goiter.
  - IV. Disabilities and Developmental delays: Vision impairment, Hearing impairment, Neuro-motor impairment, Motor delay, Language delay, Cognitive delay, Behavior disorder (Autism), Learning disorder, Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), Congenital hyperthyroidism, Sickle cell anemia, Thalassemia.

PROGRAMME IMPLEMENTATION UNDER RBSK
1. For Newborns:
  - Facility based newborn screening at public health facilities (Screening of birth defects in institutional deliveries by ANM, MO, Gynecologist).
  - Community based newborn screening through ASHA during Home visits (ASHA mobilizes caregivers to attend the local Anganwadi centres for screening, giving priority to children with LBW and children from households with known chronic illness).
2. For Children 6 weeks to 6 years:
  - Anganwadi centre-based screening.
3. For Children 6 years to 18 years:
  - Government aided school-based screening.
  - Screening is conducted at Anganwadi centre (Twice a year) and at School (Once a year).
  - At least 2 dedicated Mobile health teams are assigned in each block.
  - Suggested Mobile health team composition: 2 AYUSH Medical Officers (1 male, 1 female), 1 ANM / staff nurse, and 1 Pharmacist.

title: Health Care of the Community
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter outlines the sprawling architecture of the healthcare delivery system in India. It highlights the historical committees that shaped the system, the population norms for health centers, and the vital roles played by grassroots workers in achieving universal health coverage.

DEFINITIONS
PRIMARY HEALTH CARE: Essential health care made universally accessible to individuals and acceptable to them, through their full participation and at a cost the community and country can afford.
FIRST REFERRAL UNIT (FRU): A facility equipped to provide round-the-clock services for emergency obstetric care, newborn care, and blood storage. Usually a Community Health Centre (CHC).

CORE CONCEPTS
MAJOR HEALTH COMMITTEES:
- BHORE COMMITTEE (1946): The Health Survey and Development Committee. Proposed the 3 million plan and heavily emphasized the integration of preventive and curative services at all administrative levels.
- MUDALIAR COMMITTEE (1962): The Health Survey and Planning Committee. Focused on consolidating the gains of the early five-year plans rather than massive expansion.
- CHADHA COMMITTEE (1963): Recommended that basic health workers should visit 10,000 population to conduct malaria vigilance and family planning duties.
- KARTAR SINGH COMMITTEE (1973): The Committee on Multipurpose Workers. Replaced separate unipurpose workers (like malaria or family planning workers) with unified Multipurpose Health Workers.
- SHRIVASTAV COMMITTEE (1975): Group on Medical Education and Support Manpower. Recommended the creation of the Village Health Guide scheme to bring healthcare to the doorstep.
RURAL HEALTH INFRASTRUCTURE NORMS:
- SUB-CENTRE: Serves a population of 5000 in plains and 3000 in hilly/tribal/difficult areas. Manned by a Health Worker Female (ANM) and Health Worker Male.
- PRIMARY HEALTH CENTRE (PHC): Serves a population of 30,000 in plains and 20,000 in hilly/tribal areas. It is the first port of call to a qualified medical officer.
- COMMUNITY HEALTH CENTRE (CHC): Serves a population of 1,20,000 in plains and 80,000 in hilly/tribal areas. Requires four core specialists: Surgeon, Physician, Obstetrician/Gynecologist, and Pediatrician.

GRASSROOTS WORKERS:
- ASHA (ACCREDITED SOCIAL HEALTH ACTIVIST): A female resident of the village, married/widowed/divorced, aged 25 to 45 years. Norm is generally 1 ASHA per 1000 population. Acts as the primary bridge between the community and the health system.
- ANGANWADI WORKER: Operates under the ICDS scheme, providing supplementary nutrition, non-formal preschool education, and health education to a population of 1000.

KEY POINTS
The Bhore Committee laid the foundational blueprint for India's post-independence health system.
Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (formerly Health and Wellness Centres) represent the upgrading of Sub-Centres and PHCs to deliver Comprehensive Primary Health Care (CPHC) beyond just maternal and child health.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
POPULATION NORMS FOR PLAINS (5, 30, 120):
SUB-CENTRE: 5 THOUSAND
PHC: 30 THOUSAND
CHC: 120 THOUSAND

title: Nutrition and Health
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter focuses on macronutrients and micronutrients, their requirements, and the profound public health impact of malnutrition. It details specific deficiency disorders like Xerophthalmia, Nutritional Anemia, and Iodine Deficiency, along with diseases caused by food adulteration.


DEFINITIONS
NET PROTEIN UTILIZATION (NPU): The proportion of ingested protein that is retained in the body under standard conditions.
BIOLOGICAL VALUE (BV): The proportion of absorbed nitrogen that is retained in the body for maintenance and/or growth.
CONSUMPTION UNIT (CU): A standardized unit to calculate calorie requirements per family. An average adult male doing sedentary work is assigned a coefficient of 1.0 (equivalent to roughly 2400 Kcal/day).

CORE CONCEPTS
VITAMIN A DEFICIENCY (VAD): The leading cause of preventable childhood blindness. The WHO classification tracks the progression from early functional loss to severe structural damage.
WHO CLASSIFICATION OF XEROPHTHALMIA:
X1A: CONJUNCTIVAL XEROSIS
X1B: BITOT SPOTS (silvery, foamy triangular patches on the bulbar conjunctiva)
X2: CORNEAL XEROSIS
X3A: CORNEAL ULCERATION (<1/3 of cornea)
X3B: KERATOMALACIA (>= 1/3 of cornea)
XN: NIGHT BLINDNESS (Earliest functional symptom)

NUTRITIONAL ANEMIA: WHO cutoffs define anemia. For pregnant women and children (6 to 59 months), Hb < 11.0 g/dL. For non-pregnant women, Hb < 12.0 g/dL. For adult males, Hb < 13.0 g/dL.
IODINE DEFICIENCY DISORDERS (IDD): An area is endemic if goiter prevalence in school children is > 5 percent, or median urinary iodine is < 100 mcg/L. Iodized salt must contain 15 ppm iodine at the consumer level and 30 ppm at production.
FOOD TOXINS AND ADULTERATION:
LATHYRISM: Spastic paraplegia from consuming Lathyrus sativus (Khesari dhal). Toxic principle is BOAA (beta-oxalyl amino alanine).
EPIDEMIC DROPSY: Caused by mustard oil adulterated with Argemone mexicana. Toxic principle is Sanguinarine. Leads to severe bilateral pitting edema, glaucoma, and heart failure.
AFLATOXICOSIS: Toxin from Aspergillus flavus on improperly stored grains/groundnuts; strongly linked to hepatocellular carcinoma.

KEY POINTS
The NPU of a whole egg is assumed to be 100, serving as the reference standard.
Night blindness (XN) is the earliest symptom of VAD, while conjunctival xerosis (X1A) is the earliest clinical sign.
Vitamin A prophylaxis dose in India is 1 lakh IU at 9 months, then 2 lakh IU every 6 months until 5 years of age (total 9 doses).

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NET PROTEIN UTILIZATION = (RETAINED NITROGEN / INTAKE OF NITROGEN) X 100

MNEMONICS
FOOD TOXINS MATCHING (L-B, E-S):
LATHYRISM = BOAA
EPIDEMIC DROPSY = SANGUINARINE

title: Medicine and Social Sciences
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter explores the sociological and behavioral dimensions of health. It highlights how cultural factors, family structures, and socio-economic status fundamentally influence health-seeking behavior and disease patterns in communities.

DEFINITIONS
SOCIOLOGY: The scientific study of human society, including social interactions, relationships, and institutions.
CULTURE: The acquired, learned, and shared behavior of a group of people, transmitted from generation to generation.
FAMILY: The primary social unit, consisting of individuals related by blood, marriage, or adoption.

CORE CONCEPTS
TYPES OF FAMILY: 
1. NUCLEAR FAMILY: Consists of married parents and their dependent children.
2. JOINT FAMILY: Traditional in India, consisting of three or more generations living together under one roof, sharing property and kitchen.
ROLE OF FAMILY IN HEALTH: The family is the basic unit of epidemiological study and the primary setting for health education and care. It shapes dietary habits, hygiene practices, and psychological well-being.
CULTURAL FACTORS IN HEALTH: Cultural beliefs significantly impact disease perception. Concepts like the 'evil eye' or specific dietary taboos during pregnancy are common in traditional societies.

KEY POINTS
The family provides critical social security and emotional support to vulnerable members (elderly, children, sick).
Acculturation refers to the cultural changes that occur when two distinct cultures come into continuous contact.
Medical social workers play a vital role as a bridge between the hospital and the community.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
FAMILY FUNCTIONS (E B S):
E - ECONOMIC SUPPORT
B - BIOLOGICAL REPRODUCTION
S - SOCIALIZATION

title: Tribal Health in India
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter examines the unique health challenges faced by the tribal populations of India, who often reside in geographically isolated and socio-economically disadvantaged areas. It highlights the high burden of endemic diseases, malnutrition, and genetic disorders within these communities.

DEFINITIONS
SCHEDULED TRIBES: Indigenous communities formally recognized by the Constitution of India, characterized by primitive traits, distinctive culture, geographical isolation, shyness of contact with the community at large, and backwardness.
PARTICULARLY VULNERABLE TRIBAL GROUPS (PVTGs): Sub-groups among Scheduled Tribes characterized by a declining or stagnant population, pre-agricultural level of technology, extremely low literacy, and subsistence level of economy.

CORE CONCEPTS
HEALTH PROFILE OF TRIBES: Characterized by high maternal and infant mortality rates compared to the national average. 
COMMUNICABLE DISEASES: Extremely high burden of Vector-Borne Diseases, particularly P. falciparum Malaria. High prevalence of Tuberculosis and neglected tropical diseases like Yaws (historically) and Leprosy.
NUTRITIONAL DISORDERS: Severe protein-energy malnutrition and micronutrient deficiencies (iron, iodine, vitamin A) are rampant due to reliance on forest produce and subsistence farming.
GENETIC DISORDERS: High prevalence of Sickle Cell Anemia and G6PD deficiency, particularly in Central and Western Indian tribes (e.g., Bhils, Gonds), historically acting as a protective trait against severe malaria.

KEY POINTS
The Ministry of Tribal Affairs and the National Health Mission have dedicated sub-plans to improve tribal health infrastructure.
Healthcare delivery requires deep cultural sensitivity, often integrating modern medicine with traditional tribal healers to build trust.
Mobile Medical Units (MMUs) are frequently utilized to reach deep forest and hilly terrain where stationary health facilities are unviable.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

title: Sustainable Development Goals
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter introduces the global framework established by the United Nations to achieve a better and more sustainable future for all by 2030. It bridges the gap between health and broader socio-economic and environmental determinants.
[Image of sustainable development goals

DEFINITIONS
SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.
SDGs (GLOBAL GOALS): A collection of 17 interlinked global goals designed to be a blueprint to achieve a better and more sustainable future for all, adopted in 2015 as the successor to the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).

CORE CONCEPTS
THE 17 GOALS: While all are interconnected, specific goals directly target health and well-being.
SDG 1: No Poverty.
SDG 2: Zero Hunger (directly linked to nutrition).
SDG 3: GOOD HEALTH AND WELL-BEING. This is the primary health goal. It aims to ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.
SDG 4: Quality Education.
SDG 5: Gender Equality.
SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation.

KEY TARGETS UNDER SDG 3 (BY 2030):
- Reduce the global maternal mortality ratio to less than 70 per 100,000 live births.
- End preventable deaths of newborns and children under 5 years of age (aiming for neonatal mortality at least as low as 12 per 1000 live births and under-5 mortality at least as low as 25 per 1000 live births).
- End the epidemics of AIDS, tuberculosis, malaria, and neglected tropical diseases.
- Reduce by one-third premature mortality from non-communicable diseases.
- Achieve Universal Health Coverage (UHC).

KEY POINTS
Unlike MDGs which primarily targeted developing nations, SDGs are universally applicable to all countries.
Health is explicitly placed as a central outcome of sustainable development, heavily dependent on the success of other goals like sanitation, poverty reduction, and gender equality.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
SDG HEALTH TARGETS (M I N N):
M - MATERNAL mortality reduction (<70)
I - INFANT/child mortality reduction
N - NCD mortality reduction (by 1/3)
N - NEGLECTED tropical diseases and epidemics (end them)

title: Environment and Health
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter emphasizes the paramount importance of a safe environment. It extensively covers the methods of large-scale water purification, criteria for safe drinking water, the mechanics of slow and rapid sand filters, and the public health implications of air pollution.

DEFINITIONS
SAFE WATER: Water that is free from pathogenic agents, free from harmful chemical substances, pleasant to the taste, and usable for domestic purposes.
POLLUTED WATER: Water that contains pathogenic organisms, undesirable foreign matter, or chemicals rendering it unfit for human consumption.
BIOCHEMICAL OXYGEN DEMAND (BOD): The amount of oxygen absorbed by a sample of sewage during a specified period (usually 5 days) at a specified temperature (usually 20 C) for the aerobic destruction of organic matter.

CORE CONCEPTS
WATER PURIFICATION ON A LARGE SCALE:
1. STORAGE: Provides time for natural settlement of physical impurities and reduction of bacterial count.
2. FILTRATION:
- SLOW SAND (BIOLOGICAL) FILTERS: The vital active layer is the Schmutzdecke (zoogleal layer) which removes 98 percent of bacteria. Cleaning is done by scraping off the top layer. Cannot treat highly turbid water.
- RAPID SAND (MECHANICAL) FILTERS: Requires chemical coagulation (usually alum) before filtration. Cleaning is done efficiently by backwashing.
3. DISINFECTION (CHLORINATION): Destroys pathogenic organisms. 
- Free residual chlorine must be at least 0.5 mg/L for a contact period of 1 hour to ensure safe drinking water.
- HORROCKS APPARATUS is used to find the exact chlorine demand of a water sample.
- ORTHOTOLIDINE TEST measures both free and combined chlorine. To measure them separately, the Orthotolidine-Arsenite (OTA) test is used.
AIR POLLUTION:
- INDICATORS: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is traditionally considered the most reliable index of air pollution.
- HEALTH IMPACTS: Prolonged exposure to particulate matter (PM 2.5 and PM 10) directly correlates with exacerbations of COPD, asthma, and increased cardiovascular mortality.

KEY POINTS
Chlorine is highly effective against bacteria and viruses but has virtually no effect on the cysts of Entamoeba histolytica or the ova of helminths at normal doses.
The Schmutzdecke in a slow sand filter takes several days to form (the ripening period) and is biologically active, containing algae, plankton, diatoms, and bacteria.
PM 2.5 particles are small enough to penetrate deep into the alveolar regions of the lungs.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
CHLORINE DEMAND = CHLORINE APPLIED MINUS FREE RESIDUAL CHLORINE

MNEMONICS
WATER FILTRATION STAGES (S F D):
S - STORAGE
F - FILTRATION
D - DISINFECTION

title: Hospital Waste Management
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter is critically important for infection control in healthcare settings. It details the statutory guidelines for the segregation, transportation, treatment, and disposal of biomedical waste generated in hospitals to prevent occupational hazards and environmental pollution.
[Image of biomedical waste segregation color coding

DEFINITIONS
BIOMEDICAL WASTE (BMW): Any waste generated during the diagnosis, treatment, or immunization of human beings or animals, or in research activities pertaining thereto.
SEGREGATION: The basic separation of different categories of waste generated at source to reduce the risks and cost of handling and disposal.
INCINERATION: A controlled combustion process where waste is completely oxidized and harmful microorganisms are destroyed at very high temperatures.

CORE CONCEPTS
COLOR CODING AND SEGREGATION RULES (LATEST GUIDELINES):
- YELLOW CATEGORY: Used for human anatomical waste, animal anatomical waste, soiled waste (blood-soaked cotton/bandages), expired or discarded medicines, chemical waste, and microbiology/biotechnology waste. Treatment is strictly by Incineration or deep burial.
- RED CATEGORY: Used for contaminated recyclable waste primarily made of plastics (e.g., IV tubes, catheters, syringes without needles, urine bags, gloves). Treatment is by Autoclaving or microwaving followed by shredding. Must never be incinerated to prevent toxic dioxin release.
- WHITE CATEGORY (TRANSLUCENT AND PUNCTURE-PROOF): Used for all sharps, including needles, scalpels, blades, and any contaminated metal object. Treatment is by dry heat sterilization or autoclaving followed by shredding.
- BLUE CATEGORY (CARDBOARD BOXES): Used for broken or discarded glassware and metallic body implants. Treatment is by disinfection and recycling.

SPILL MANAGEMENT: Blood spills should be contained and treated with 1 percent Sodium Hypochlorite solution with a contact time of at least 20 minutes before being wiped and discarded into the Yellow bin.

KEY POINTS
Segregation at the source is the single most important step in biomedical waste management.
Chlorinated plastics must never be incinerated as they release highly toxic dioxins and furans into the atmosphere.
Mercury spills must never be collected with bare hands or a vacuum cleaner; use stiff paper to scoop droplets into a sealable container.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
BMW CATEGORY DISPOSAL (Y I, R A):
Y - YELLOW means INCINERATE (Burn it to ash)
R - RED means AUTOCLAVE (Sterilize and recycle plastics)

title: Disaster Management
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter outlines the public health response to natural and man-made disasters. It focuses on the crucial phases of the disaster cycle, effective triage systems, and strategies to prevent post-disaster disease outbreaks.


DEFINITIONS
DISASTER: Any occurrence that causes damage, ecological disruption, loss of human life, or deterioration of health and health services on a scale sufficient to warrant an extraordinary response from outside the affected community.
TRIAGE: The process of rapidly classifying the injured on the basis of the severity of their injuries and the likelihood of their survival with prompt medical intervention.
MITIGATION: Measures aimed at reducing the impact of disasters on a community.

CORE CONCEPTS
DISASTER CYCLE:
1. DISASTER IMPACT
2. DISASTER RESPONSE: Immediate actions taken after a disaster, including search and rescue, triage, and provision of basic needs.
3. REHABILITATION AND RECONSTRUCTION (RECOVERY)
4. MITIGATION
5. PREPAREDNESS: Proactive measures like early warning systems, stockpiling supplies, and conducting mock drills.

TRIAGE COLOR CODING:
- RED (IMMEDIATE): High priority, life-threatening injuries but treatable (e.g., tension pneumothorax).
- YELLOW (DELAYED): Medium priority, severe injuries but not immediately life-threatening.
- GREEN (AMBULATORY): Low priority, minor injuries (the 'walking wounded').
- BLACK (EXPECTANT/DEAD): No priority, injuries are incompatible with life or the patient is already dead.

DISEASE CONTROL: The immediate priority post-disaster is ensuring a safe water supply and adequate sanitation to prevent outbreaks of water-borne diseases like cholera and typhoid.

KEY POINTS
Routine mass immunization programs (e.g., for typhoid or cholera) are generally NOT recommended immediately following a disaster due to logistical challenges and delayed immunity.
Chlorination of water is the most critical public health intervention in the aftermath of floods.
Epidemiological surveillance must be rapidly established in relief camps to detect early signs of outbreaks.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
TRIAGE PRIORITIES (R Y G B):
R - RED (RUN to them first)
Y - YELLOW (Yield slightly)
G - GREEN (Go to them last)
B - BLACK (Beyond help)

title: Occupational Health
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter explores the relationship between work and health. It details the physiological principles of ergonomics, the wide spectrum of occupational hazards (physical, chemical, biological, and dust-related), and the vital labor legislations designed to protect worker health and provide social security.

DEFINITIONS
ERGONOMICS: Derived from Ergon (work) and Nomos (law). It is the science of fitting the job to the worker, aiming to achieve mutual adjustment of man and his work to improve efficiency and well-being.
OCCUPATIONAL HAZARD: A risk accepted as a consequence of a particular occupation.
PNEUMOCONIOSIS: A group of interstitial lung diseases caused by the inhalation of specific organic or inorganic dusts in the workplace.

CORE CONCEPTS
OCCUPATIONAL HAZARDS:
PHYSICAL: Heat (heat stroke), Noise (chronic exposure > 85 dB leads to Noise-Induced Hearing Loss classically at the 4000 Hz frequency), Radiation (leukemia).
BIOLOGICAL: Brucellosis (dairy/abattoir workers), Anthrax (wool sorters disease), Leptospirosis (sewer/rice field workers).
CHEMICAL (LEAD POISONING / PLUMBISM): Common in battery manufacturing and smelting. Clinical features include abdominal colic, Burtonian line (blue line on gums), wrist/foot drop, and anemia with basophilic stippling. Coproporphyrin in urine is a useful screening test.

PNEUMOCONIOSES (DUST DISEASES): Dust particles between 0.5 to 3 microns are most dangerous as they reach the alveoli.
- SILICOSIS: Inhalation of free silica. X-ray shows a snow-storm appearance. Strongly predisposes to Tuberculosis.
- ANTHRACOSIS: Coal workers pneumoconiosis.
- ASBESTOSIS: Strongly associated with bronchogenic carcinoma and Mesothelioma (pleural cancer).
- BYSSINOSIS: Inhalation of cotton dust (textile workers). Characterized by Monday Fever (chest tightness on the first day back to work).
- BAGASSOSIS: Inhalation of sugarcane dust containing Thermoactinomyces sacchari.
IMPORTANT LEGISLATIONS:
